books.name: मुमुक्षुव्यवहारप्रकरणम्
books.subtitle: Book of Conduct of the Seeker

chapters.subtitle: A king seeks liberation and visits a wise sage.
chapters.es: Sutīkṣṇa asks Agastya whether action or knowledge leads to mokṣa. Agastya explains that both are necessary, like two wings for flight. He tells of King Ariṣṭanemi, who rejects Indra's offer of heaven upon learning its rewards are finite. On Indra's advice, the king approaches Valmīki for guidance. The sage begins the story of the Vasiṣṭha-Rāma dialogue, recounting the divine curses that compelled Viṣṇu to take human form as Rāma and endure the trials of earthly existence.

chapters.subtitle: Brahma orders the writing of Rama's story.
chapters.es: Individuals seeking liberation through the study of mokṣa qualify for these teachings. Following a request for a remedy to human suffering, the creator Brahma visits a sage’s hermitage. He commands that the history of Rama be recorded to serve as a bridge over the ocean of rebirth. Bharadvaja questions how Rama and his companions lived without sorrow. These figures lived as jīvanmukta, performing daily duties with unattached minds. By following their example of detached action, a person crosses the sea of worldly existence.

chapters.subtitle: Rama goes on a pilgrimage to holy sites.
chapters.es: Inquiry removes the mental impression of the world, much like realizing the sky lacks actual color. Liberation occurs when malina vāsanā, or impure tendencies, vanish, leaving only śuddha vāsanā to sustain the body. Rāma requests permission from his father to visit sacred sites. Accompanied by his brothers, he travels to the banks of the Mandākinī, Sarayū, and Sarasvatī rivers. He visits hermitages and holy places like Vārāṇasī and Puṣkara, showered with grain by citizens before returning home.

chapters.subtitle: Rama returns home and follows a daily routine.
chapters.es: Rāma enters the city as citizens shower him with flowers. He bows to his father, the sage Vasiṣṭha, and his kin before joining an eight-day festival. His daily life follows a regular pattern: morning rituals, discussions on wisdom with court sages, and hunting expeditions for boar and buffalo. He shares stories of the various customs observed during his tīrthayātrā and spends his evenings dining with brothers and friends, fulfilling the duties of a prince within the royal household.

chapters.subtitle: Rama becomes silent and detached after his pilgrimage.
chapters.es: Upon returning from a pilgrimage, sixteen-year-old Rāma falls into a silent withdrawal. He sits in padmāsana with his cheek against his palm, his face pale and his body thinning like a pond in autumn. He neglects daily rituals and refuses to speak. His brothers follow his example, causing King Daśaratha great concern. When questioning Rāma yields no answers, the king turns to the sage Vasiṣṭha, who explains that such changes in a noble person only occur for a major reason.

chapters.subtitle: Sage Viśvāmitra arrives to ask King Daśaratha for help.
chapters.es: While King Daśaratha and his court wait in anxious silence, the sage Viśvāmitra arrives at the gates of Ayodhyā. Seeking protection for his yajña from disrupting demons, the ascetic stands with matted hair and a water pot. Daśaratha, Vasiṣṭha, and Vāmadeva descend from their thrones to greet him with pādya and arghya. The king compares this arrival to rain during a drought or the birth of a son. He promises to fulfill any request the sage brings, offering his full support.

chapters.subtitle: Viśvāmitra asks for Rāma to protect his sacrifice.
chapters.es: Viśvāmitra describes the desecration of his ritual grounds by flesh-eating rākṣasa-s who pour blood and meat upon the sacred fires. Bound by the constraints of his dīkṣā, the sage refrains from using his ascetic power to curse the invaders. Instead, he requests that the young prince Rāma accompany him for ten nights to guard the yajña. Viśvāmitra assures the king that Rāma’s arrows will defeat the demons. Vasiṣṭha and other seers support this request, recognizing the prince's innate strength.

chapters.subtitle: Dasharatha refuses to send Rama to fight demons.
chapters.es: Dasharatha refuses the request for his son, citing the boy’s youth and lack of military training. He offers his entire akṣauhiṇī army and his own sword to confront the rākṣasa forces instead. The king describes Rama as a child under sixteen who prefers palace gardens to battlegrounds. Stricken by fear and fatherly affection, he admits his inability to face the power of Ravana. Dasharatha pleads for mercy, claiming he cannot survive even a moment if separated from his eldest son.

chapters.subtitle: Vasiṣṭha convinces the king to keep his promise.
chapters.es: Viśvāmitra reacts with anger when Daśaratha hesitates to fulfill his promise, causing the earth to tremble. Vasiṣṭha intervenes, advising the king to uphold the honor of the Ikṣvāku lineage and the sanctity of his word. He explains that Rāma remains safe because Viśvāmitra commands ancient astra weapons born from the daughters of Dakṣa. Vasiṣṭha insists that fulfilling this pratijñā is essential to preserve the king’s spiritual merit, or iṣṭāpūrta. He urges the king to release his son to the sage's care.

chapters.subtitle: Attendants report Rama’s withdrawal from royal life and duties.
chapters.es: Following Daśaratha’s summons, attendants describe Rāma’s withdrawal from palace life. He neglects bathing, eating, and sensory pleasures, sitting instead in padmāsana with his cheek resting on his hand. The prince views the world as a void and rejects the prospect of kingship. He gives his wealth to the needy and retreats to silent, solitary places. Thin and pale, he dismisses royal duties as unreal. Lakṣmaṇa and Śatrughna imitate his detached state, leaving the household distressed for their survival.

chapters.subtitle: Rāma enters the assembly and is questioned by sages.
chapters.es: Viśvāmitra requests that the king summon Rāma, noting that the prince's state arises from viveka and vairāgya. Rāma enters the assembly with his brothers and bows before Daśaratha, Vasiṣṭha, and the gathered sages. After an embrace, Rāma sits on a cloth on the floor. Daśaratha advises his son to follow the wisdom of elders. Viśvāmitra then asks Rāma to describe the source of his sorrow. Hearing this, the prince sheds his grief like a peacock hearing the thunder of a rain cloud.

chapters.subtitle: Rama explains why he feels indifferent toward the world.
chapters.es: Rama describes his return from pilgrimage and his growing detachment from worldly life. He observes a repetitive cycle where birth leads to death and death to birth. He compares material objects to disconnected iron needles held together only by mental imagination. Human existence appears as a mirage, or mṛgatṛṣṇā, trapping the mind like a deer in a desert. This internal anxiety burns like fire in a hollow tree. He views people as hollow bamboos, or kīcaka-veṇu, through which the wind of breath whistles without purpose.

chapters.subtitle: The negative impact of wealth on human character.
chapters.es: Wealth flows like a monsoon river, bringing waves of anxiety that drown stable thought. It acts as a flickering lamp flame, producing the soot of mental distress. This fortune obscures the virtues of the wise like dust on a gemstone and hardens human relationships. It serves as frost to the vine of vairāgya and an eclipse to the moon of viveka. Ultimately, wealth is a sharp sword's edge or a flowering vine growing from a pit, hiding venomous snakes within its coils.

chapters.subtitle: Human life is short and full of suffering.
chapters.es: Human life is a water droplet trembling on a leaf’s tip, vanishing without warning. It serves as a nest for birds of disease and a target for the snakes of decay. Time gnaws at the body like a rat chewing through a thread, while death watches its prey like a cat stalking a mouse. Without ātmajñāna, scriptures and the physical form remain heavy burdens. Only the person who stills the restless mind truly lives, moving beyond the repetitive cycle of grief.

chapters.subtitle: Rama explains how the ego causes human suffering.
chapters.es: The sense of ego, or ahaṃkāra, acts as a persistent enemy that fuels worldly suffering. It manifests like a hunter’s snare, trapping the mind in a cycle of anxiety and desire. This internal force behaves as a rogue elephant in the mountains of the mind or a lion stalking the forest of the body. When this cloud of ego dissipates, thirst for material objects vanishes. Relinquishing the identity of Rama, the speaker seeks a state of quietude, free from the burdens of agency.

chapters.subtitle: Rama describes the difficulty of controlling his mind.
chapters.es: The mind wanders like a stray dog or a deer seeking grass near a hidden pit. It remains empty like a wicker basket in water and restless like an ocean churned by a mountain. This citta traps the self like a bird in a net or a child frightened by a ghost. Controlling its impulses is more difficult than drinking the sea or swallowing fire. Because the mind constructs the three worlds, its conquest through viveka is the only path to end the cycle of suffering.

chapters.subtitle: Rama describes the restless and destructive nature of desire.
chapters.es: Desire, or tṛṣṇā, acts as a dark night where faults flutter like owls. It parches the heart like mud under the sun and dances like a ghost in the forest of the mind. This craving pulls the intellect like a rope binds an ox and shifts like a restless monkey. It cuts through virtues as a rat gnaws a lute string. Even the steady become like dry grass before its wind, trapped in a net that entangles all living beings.

chapters.subtitle: Rama describes the decaying nature of the physical body.
chapters.es: The body stands as a temporary structure of bone, muscle, and red blood. It functions like a house where the senses peek through window-like eyes and the tongue moves like a captive monkey. In this forest of flesh, hair grows like wild grass while the vulture of ahaṃkāra nests within the hollow chest. This frame serves as a vessel for decay and disease. Like a water bubble or lightning, the physical form collapses, proving itself a fragile machine of bone and sinew.

chapters.subtitle: The suffering and helplessness of childhood
chapters.es: A human enters bālya as a period of physical weakness and constant crying. This stage brings fear of wind and heat, alongside the misery of unsatisfied cravings, such as reaching for the moon. Like a tethered elephant, a child remains under the strict control of parents and teachers. The mind fluctuates ten times more than an adult’s, moving like lightning or ocean waves. This state consists of total dependence, where a person remains trapped by ignorance and the recurring terrors of the unknown.

chapters.subtitle: Youth is a dangerous and brief stage of life.
chapters.es: Youth functions as a turbulent transition where the mind falls into a cycle of sensory distress. Desires act as a kāmapiśāca, a lustful demon that seizes the intellect. This period flickers like a lightning flash or a mirage in the desert. Mental clarity darkens like a silted river during the monsoon. Men burn with the heat of separation as if caught in a dāvāgni or forest fire. While this stage promises pleasure, it passes quickly like a bird leaving a tree, leaving behind only regret.

chapters.subtitle: Examining physical beauty and its eventual decay
chapters.es: The body appears as a meat puppet held by nerves and bones. A pearl necklace on a breast resembles the Gaṅgā flowing over Meru, yet dogs soon scavenge that same flesh in cemeteries. Physical attraction functions as a snare, like a net trapping birds or a post binding a rutting elephant. This viṣalatā, or poisonous creeper, withers until hair hangs from cemetery trees and dust absorbs the blood. Relinquishing these fleeting forms allows the mind to find stillness and escape the fear of decay.

chapters.subtitle: Old age weakens the body and invites death.
chapters.es: Old age, or jarā, descends like frost upon a lotus, withering the body until it resembles a tree on a collapsing riverbank. White hair covers the head like moonlight on a peak, signaling the approach of death. While physical strength fails, internal greed and the fear of the afterlife intensify. Family members mock the trembling frame, and wisdom departs like a fleeing rival. This state serves as a white fly-whisk, heralding the arrival of death, which eventually leaps upon the person like a monkey.

chapters.subtitle: Time destroys everything in the universe.
chapters.es: Kāla consumes every being, relentless as a fire beneath the ocean. Like a parrot cracking pomegranate seeds, it shatters the foundations of the world. This force plays with the sun and moon like toy balls in a courtyard and wears a garland of bones at the end of a kalpa. It treats entire universes like wild fig fruits, devouring gods and mortals alike. Using the night as a broom and the sun as a lamp, it searches every corner to seize its prey.

chapters.subtitle: Time acts as a hunter and destroyer of worlds
chapters.es: Time moves through the world like a prince hunting in a dense thicket. He treats the ocean of dissolution as a private pond where vaḍavānala fires bloom like lotuses. This force, mahākāla, consumes the universe like a seasoned meal and keeps the fierce nṛsiṃha as a caged pet bird. Using a bow of abhāva, he strikes down all beings. Time acts as a restless monkey, tearing apart aging structures while dancing through the forest of the universe.

chapters.subtitle: Time and Fate dance to destroy and recreate worlds.
chapters.es: Kṛtānta dances within the theater of the universe alongside his consort, Niyati. He wears the sun and moon as earrings, the seven oceans as bracelets, and the mountains Meru and Himavat as ornaments. Niyati’s jewelry consists of the celestial realms and hellish regions. At the end of a cycle, their dance dissolves all existence. Kṛtānta then recreates the worlds and their inhabitants as easily as a child fashions shapes from mud, renewing the inevitable sequence of birth, growth, and decay.

chapters.subtitle: Rāma explains why everything in the world eventually ends.
chapters.es: Rāma observes how kāla and niyati consume all beings like fire or a serpent. Youth fades into jarā, and sensory bhoga leads to disease. Even cosmic forces like the sun mārtaṇḍa, Indra, and the gods hari and bhava face destruction. Mountains crumble and oceans dry up while the mind remains trapped by ahaṃkāra. Human life consists of fleeting waves on an ocean. Only the awakened find peace, while the rest waste away in the forest of rebirth.

chapters.subtitle: Human life and worldly pleasures are temporary and disappointing.
chapters.es: Human life withers like a lotus in a parched pond once youth departs. The body is a fragile nāu or boat, navigated through an ocean where the senses strike like crocodiles. Desires act as a tṛṣṇā river, eroding the roots of contentment. Men wander like deer in a forest of poisonous vines, eventually falling like dry leaves. Neither wealth nor family offers lasting shelter against death. Even the vast spans of time reveal that worldly achievements are as fleeting as bubbles on water.

chapters.subtitle: Everything in the world is constantly changing and dying.
chapters.es: The world moves like a dream where mountains flatten into pits and forests transform into bustling cities. Fine silks rot in ditches while powerful men crumble into piles of ash. Life flickers like a lamp flame in the wind, shifting between wealth and poverty. Even Brahmā, Viṣṇu, and Rudra eventually fall before the fire of kāla. This saṃsāra acts as a restless dancer, turning water into land and men into beasts, as every physical form inevitably decays into its opposite.

chapters.subtitle: Rama explains his detachment and asks for spiritual guidance.
chapters.es: Rama describes his mind as a forest scorched by fire where desires flicker like mirages. He observes social order breaking like dry bean pods and human kindness thinning. Sensual pleasures appear as a poison that kills across multiple births, deadlier than physical venom. He compares the senses to tight knots and the mind to a lotus crushed by an elephant. Rejecting kingdoms and wealth, he asks for the vijñāna-dīpa, or lamp of wisdom, to dispel the fog of ignorance.

chapters.subtitle: Rāma asks for guidance to overcome worldly suffering.
chapters.es: Rāma describes his mind trembling like the leaves of an aged tree. He views his senses as deer falling into deep pits and his intellect as a child lost in a hollow space. He questions how ancient rulers like Janaka remained untroubled while active in the world. He asks for a method to remain unstained by saṃsāra, comparing the ideal state to a lotus leaf in water. Seeking an end to worldly suffering, he requests a teaching that provides inner coolness and lasting pūrṇatā.

chapters.subtitle: A request for a way to end worldly suffering.
chapters.es: Life trembles like a water drop on a leaf, and death stalks every creature like a cat hunting mice. In this forest of saṃsāra, the mind remains stained by desires while greed dances like a peacock. Seeking a yukti to end this suffering, a question arises on how to act without being burned by the world. If no path to peace exists, the speaker resolves to abandon food and water, remaining motionless like a painted figure until life expires like a lamp without oil.

chapters.subtitle: The assembly and celestial beings react to Rāma’s words.
chapters.es: Rāma concludes his discourse on worldly detachment, leaving the assembly in motionless silence. Sages like Vasiṣṭha, King Daśaratha, and even the palace animals remain still. A shower of mandāra and kunda flowers falls from the sky, covering the court. From the heavens, invisible siddha beings declare they have never heard such wisdom in their travels. These sky-dwellers acknowledge that Rāma’s clarity on the nature of suffering provides an immediate awakening, surpassing even the speech of the celestial teachers.

chapters.subtitle: Sages visit the court to praise Rāma’s detachment.
chapters.es: Celestial siddhas and sages including Vyāsa and Nārada descend from the sky to King Daśaratha’s court. Carrying vīṇās, deer skins, and water pots, they mingle with the earthly assembly. Vasiṣṭha and Viśvamitra exchange ritual arghya and pādya with the newcomers. After taking their seats, the sages praise Rāma’s words on detachment. They compare his discernment to a steady lamp and his wisdom to a rare vine blossoming in a world of decay, noting his clarity among men.

chapters.subtitle: Śuka confirms his spiritual knowledge through King Janaka.
chapters.es: Viśvāmitra compares Rāma’s intellect to that of Śuka, who attained truth through detachment but lacked inner stillness. Śuka asks his father, Vyāsa, about the origin of saṃsāra. Unsatisfied by the answer, he seeks out King Janaka. The king tests Śuka with indifference and palace luxuries, yet Śuka remains as steady as a mountain. When Janaka repeats the same teaching regarding the dissolution of vikalpa, Śuka finds rest. He retreats to Mount Meru, enters nirvikalpa samādhi, and merges into the self like a drop in the ocean.

chapters.subtitle: Vasiṣṭha agrees to guide Rama toward liberation.
chapters.es: Rama’s indifference to worldly pleasure, similar to that of the sage Śuka, marks his readiness for liberation. This state, where sensory objects no longer attract the mind, defines a jīvanmukta. Viśvāmitra asks the sage Vasiṣṭha to instruct the prince using the wisdom once revealed by the god Brahmā on Mount Niṣadha. Vasiṣṭha agrees to dispel Rama’s inner darkness, likening the process to lighting a lamp. He identifies mokṣa as the thinning of vāsanā, while the strengthening of these latent impressions constitutes worldly bondage.

chapters.subtitle: Identical worlds and people repeat across time cycles.
chapters.es: Beings arise and vanish like dust motes in a sunbeam or waves on an ocean. Within the heart, the soul perceives worlds that appear solid through habit, layering like the leaves of a kadalī tree. Across cycles of time, the same individuals recur with slight variations. This Vyāsa is a tenth incarnation, destined to divide the Veda and compose the bhārata once more. These patterns of avidyā repeat until one becomes a jīvanmukta, steady and free from mental agitation.

chapters.subtitle: Human effort brings success while fate is an illusion.
chapters.es: Water remains water whether calm or wavy; liberation remains the same with or without a physical body. All worldly achievement springs from pauruṣa, or human effort, while daiva is a fiction for the weak. Through persistent exertion, individuals attain the status of Indra, Viṣṇu, or Śiva. Present effort possesses the power to overcome past impressions and can even move Mount Meru. When aligned with śāstra, this directed action secures every aim, while its absence results in ruin.

chapters.subtitle: The power of self-effort over fate
chapters.es: Present effort and past tendencies clash like two fighting rams, where the stronger force prevails. A person must use deliberate action, or pauruṣa, to overcome past errors. Relying on fate, or daiva, leads to failure. Like a lion breaking out of a cage, one escapes the cycle of worldly existence through self-exertion guided by śāstra. Seeking the company of the wise, or satsaṅga, sharpens the intellect. Laziness keeps the world in poverty; clarity comes only to those who actively struggle against their previous habits.

chapters.subtitle: Self-effort is more powerful than fate.
chapters.es: Individual effort, or pauruṣa, overcomes past destiny like a strong man subduing a child. Past and present actions clash like two rams, where the more vigorous force inevitably triumphs. To rely on fate is the mark of a tethered animal; instead, a person uses śāstra and the company of the wise to refine their intellect. This persistent exertion acts as a medicine against the disease of repeated birth. Just as a hand crushes grain, steady action secures results that the weak mistake for luck.

chapters.subtitle: Individual effort is more powerful than fate.
chapters.es: Deliberate exertion, or pauruṣa, drives all success, while reliance on daiva leads to ruin. Effort manifests as the focused movement of thought and action. Figures like Bṛhaspati and Viṣṇu attained their positions through such work. Fate is simply a name for the results of past deeds or a comfort for the weak-minded. By studying scriptures and associating with the wise, a person redirects the mind toward beneficial goals. This persistent application of will ensures prosperity and prevents a lower rebirth.

chapters.subtitle: Human effort is more important than fate.
chapters.es: Fate possesses no physical form or power and exists only as a mental projection, like a snake imagined in a rope. It is a label for the results of past deeds. Because a formless force cannot move a physical body, individuals must rely on pauruṣa or human effort. Figures like Viśvāmitra attained their status through exertion rather than passive waiting. Success in rituals, daily life, and spiritual mastery follows active movement, making the idea of destiny irrelevant.

chapters.subtitle: Human effort overcomes fate and redirects the mind.
chapters.es: Fate, or daiva, exists only as a name for the results of past human effort. It possesses no independent power to act. Individual behavior arises from vāsanā, the latent mental impressions that drive every action. A person redirects the vāsanā-sarit, the river of these impressions, from harmful paths toward auspicious ones through disciplined pauruṣa. By conquering the five senses and following the guidance of teachers, one eventually transcends even good impressions to reach a state beyond all sorrow.

chapters.subtitle: Brahma sends Vasiṣṭha to earth to teach humanity.
chapters.es: Brahmā observes the suffering of humans in Bharata-varṣa. Realizing that rituals and pilgrimages fail to end misery, he concludes that only jñāna provides nirvāṇa. He creates Vasiṣṭha from his mind and temporarily veils his awareness with a curse of ignorance. This allows Vasiṣṭha to experience human grief before receiving the teaching of liberation. Brahmā then instructs him to descend to earth. Vasiṣṭha remains there to guide seekers, performing worldly duties while remaining internally still and free from attachment.

chapters.subtitle: Brahma sends sages to teach kings and end suffering.
chapters.es: Brahmā observes the suffering of beings and sends sages to Earth to provide relief through jñāna. This spiritual science, once shared with ancient rulers, became known as rāja-vidyā. Rāma possesses a natural vairāgya born from internal reflection rather than external loss. Vasiṣṭha urges him to cultivate the four gatekeepers of liberation: śama, vicāra, santoṣa, and sādhu-saṅgama. Like cloth absorbing kuṅkuma dye, Rāma's clear mind receives these teachings to destroy the ignorance that causes worldly distress.

chapters.subtitle: Wisdom and scripture study end worldly suffering.
chapters.es: Vasiṣṭha prepares Rāma to receive knowledge, likening the youth’s detachment to a moonstone softening under light. He describes worldly life as a persistent plague cured only by the mantra of yoga and the study of śāstra. A person practicing vicāra sheds anxieties like a snake casting off its old skin. While the ignorant suffer through fire and blades of desire, the wise wear an armor of knowledge. They view the body as a chariot and treat worldly movement as a simple play.

chapters.subtitle: Tranquility of mind leads to liberation.
chapters.es: Wise individuals move through the world without craving or grief, performing actions while remaining internally still. Ignorance acts as a sharp thorn, causing more distress than disease or poison. Success comes not from wealth or travel but through conquering the mind. The quality of śama, or inner peace, serves as a gatekeeper to liberation. A person possessing śama remains steady during conflicts and treats all beings with the coolness of moonlight. This tranquility protects the heart like armor against the arrows of misfortune.

chapters.subtitle: Self-inquiry helps people overcome suffering and find peace.
chapters.es: The practice of vicāra acts as a sharp saw to clear the dense forest of worldly calamities. It functions as a lamp in the dark, exposing the imaginary ghosts of delusion that haunt a confused mind. By investigating the origin of the self and the nature of saṃsāra, the intellect gains the strength of a lion against the elephants of passion. This inquiry brings a steady calm, allowing a person to witness the world’s movements without attachment, eventually yielding the fruit of absolute peace.

chapters.subtitle: The benefits of practicing contentment and peace.
chapters.es: A person practicing saṃtoṣa treats an empire like a blade of grass. This contentment acts as nectar, cooling the mind so knowledge reflects as in a clean mirror. One who stops longing for the unobtained and accepts what arrives naturally remains steady. Mental distress vanishes like dust in the rain, and prosperity follows the individual like a servant. The mind blooms like a lotus in the sun, finding a stillness that matches the moon and earning the respect of sages.

chapters.subtitle: Good company and personal effort lead to mental peace.
chapters.es: Seeking the company of the wise, or sādhusaṅgama, clears the mind of ignorance like a lamp lighting a dark path. This association acts as a cooling bath, surpassing traditional rituals or pilgrimages. Along with santoṣa, vicāra, and śama, it forms a fourfold path to overcome suffering. By applying pauruṣa, or deliberate effort, an individual subdues the mind and steers it away from harmful impulses, much like redirecting a river toward a steady bank to reach stability.

chapters.subtitle: An overview of the six sections for liberation.
chapters.es: Thirty-two thousand verses serve as a lamp to dispel the world-illusion, just as light reveals a rope is not a snake. Six sections structure this path. Initial parts cultivate vairāgya and define the seeker’s conduct. Subsequent divisions detail how the world arises and persists like a dream-city or a mirage. Through logical inquiry, the false distinctions of "I" and "you" vanish. The final stage leads to nirvāṇa, where the individual remains steady like a stone pillar, viewing the entire universe as a mere reflection within consciousness.

chapters.subtitle: Logic and examples help a person understand reality.
chapters.es: A person adopts logical counsel even from a child, rejecting flawed tradition as one might refuse a father’s stagnant well for the nearby gaṅgā. Through persistent vicāra, the mind clears like a still lake in autumn. Delusion and greed vanish when the world is seen as a harmless painted snake or a fading dream. Using specific dṛṣṭānta to illuminate the formless brahman, the intellect distinguishes reality from appearance, reaching a quiet state where the ego no longer disturbs the heart.

chapters.subtitle: Achieving peace through inquiry and direct experience.
chapters.es: An inquirer uses analogies to grasp the meaning of mahāvākya statements, focusing on shared traits rather than perfect similarity. Like a frog trapped in a stone, one who lacks discernment remains bound to pleasures. True understanding comes through direct perception, or pratyakṣa, rather than empty debate. When the mind rests in the fourth state of turya, it remains still like an ocean without waves. By replacing the notion of fate with personal effort, or pauruṣa, the seeker realizes the world as a movement of consciousness.

chapters.subtitle: Developing wisdom and peace through noble conduct.
chapters.es: A seeker increases wisdom by associating with noble people and adopting their traits. Inner tranquility, or śama, and right knowledge grow together, supporting one another like lotuses and a pond. One must practice both right conduct and intellectual inquiry simultaneously; neither succeeds alone. Listening to scriptures from a teacher clarifies the mind, just as kataka powder settles sediment in muddy water. This dual practice of action and understanding eventually leads to a state of permanent clarity and peace.

chapters.subtitle: The mind creates the world like a dream.
chapters.es: The mind arises from brahman like a wave upon the sea, projecting the universe as a mental dream. This perceived world, or dṛśya, binds the observer when mistaken for reality. Just as a gold bracelet is nothing but gold, the world remains inseparable from its source. Bondage persists as long as the seen appears real, reflecting in the mirror of consciousness called cid-ādarśa. Neither rituals nor temporary samādhi remove this seed, which dwells within the seer like fragrance in a flower or oil in a sesame seed.

chapters.subtitle: Death cannot catch a man with no past actions.
chapters.es: Mṛtyu attempts to seize the brahmin Ākāśaja but his hands pass through the man as if through empty air. He learns from Yama that the power of death requires the presence of past karman. Mṛtyu searches forests, oceans, and distant islands but finds no record of the brahmin’s actions. Because Ākāśaja rose directly from the sky, he has no physical form or history of deeds. He remains pure cidākāśa, leaving Mṛtyu unable to grasp him, just as no one can bind the wind.

chapters.subtitle: Brahma creates the world through his mind and will.
chapters.es: Brahma begins without prior karma or material cause, possessing only an ātivāhika body of pure light. He lacks a physical ādhibhautika frame. As pure manas, he projects the three worlds through saṅkalpa, extending the universe like a sprout from a seed or an imagined city. This creation remains an expansion of consciousness, devoid of physical elements. While others perceive solid matter through habit, the creator remains a mental being. He shapes the cosmos through thought, much like a dreamer experiences a vivid yet unsubstantial world.

chapters.subtitle: Vasiṣṭha explains the mind and the uncreated world.
chapters.es: Vasiṣṭha halts his speech as the sun sets. Cage-bound birds and courtly women grow still, and shadows lengthen across the palace floor. The assembly rises with the chime of keyūra bracelets to perform twilight rituals. At dawn, the gathering returns to hear Rāma inquire about the mind. Vasiṣṭha defines manas as mere saṅkalpa, or volition, lacking any physical form. He explains that the perceived world dwells within the observer like a plant in a seed, yet ultimately remains uncreated.

chapters.subtitle: The supreme reality as the source of all things.
chapters.es: After the mahāpralaya dissolves the visible world, only the peaceful paramātmā remains. This reality acts as the light behind the sun and the ocean from which infinite universes rise like bubbles. It dwells within stones, water, and space, animating the subtle body or puryaṣṭaka. Though it stays still, its presence allows the vine of nature to grow and time to move. Like a lamp, it illuminates itself and objects around it while remaining a changeless, pure saṃvid.

chapters.subtitle: Attaining liberation through self-effort and spiritual study
chapters.es: Realization of the supreme self arises through knowledge rather than physical penance or ritual acts. This divinity remains accessible within the body, requiring only personal effort and discriminative inquiry to clear the illusion of the world. By abandoning anger and greed, the seeker turns toward the company of the wise and the study of scriptures. This persistent effort acts as a medicine for worldly existence. Just as the kataka nut purifies turbid water, steady vicāra removes ignorance and ends the cycle of suffering.

chapters.subtitle: A teacher explains that the world is an illusion.
chapters.es: Rama asks for the location of the divine. The teacher identifies a presence residing within the body called cinmātra. This consciousness remains still like a stone yet fills the universe, appearing in the interval when awareness moves between locations. The world is described as a total non-existence, atyantābhāva, comparable to a mirage or a child born to a barren woman. The instructor promises to wipe this perceived reality from the mind like dust from a mirror, using logic to reveal the unborn Brahman.

chapters.subtitle: Vasiṣṭha explains why Rāma should study this text.
chapters.es: Vasiṣṭha describes ignorance as a persistent plague, viṣūcikā, that only knowledge cures. He urges Rāma to listen to stories; one who persists in effort and keeps company with the wise attains liberation. This Mahārāmāyaṇa contains the essence of all histories, causing the world to vanish like a dream. Wise men know that what is here is found elsewhere, and what is not here is nowhere. This inquiry grants jīvanmuktattva, ending suffering where rituals, wealth, and austerities fail.

chapters.subtitle: Defining liberation and the nature of the ultimate reality
chapters.es: A person liberated while living moves through the world without ego, remaining steady in both pleasure and pain. They act with a clear mind, appearing awake even in deep sleep. When the body perishes, this state becomes formless liberation, where the self encompasses the sun, wind, and elements. Like a gold bracelet that is nothing but gold, the universe exists as an appearance of brahman. After the mahāpralaya, a single consciousness remains, sensing all things without eyes or ears.

chapters.subtitle: The nature of reality after the world ends
chapters.es: After the dissolution of the universe, the world remains within brahman like an uncarved statue hidden inside a stone pillar. This state is not a void; it holds the cosmos as waves exist potentially in calm water or pulp resides within a bilva fruit. This reality lacks the light of the sun or moon, manifesting as pure consciousness akin to deep, dreamless sleep. Even during activity, a practitioner with a quieted mind rests in this undivided state where the distinctions of knower and known vanish.

chapters.subtitle: The world has no reality and was never created.
chapters.es: The world exists only as a conceptual forest in the empty sky or the son of a barren woman. Just as a gold bracelet lacks existence apart from the gold, the jagat holds no reality separate from brahman. It appears like water in a mirage or a city in a dream, yet lacks a cause for its birth. Since no creation occurred at the beginning, no destruction happens now. Liberation follows the firm realization that the seen world never arose, leaving only the singular ātman.

chapters.subtitle: How the universe grows from consciousness.
chapters.es: Pure consciousness stirs, moving from stillness toward awareness. This vibration produces the jīva, which then assumes the form of ahaṃkāra and experiences time. From this intent, five subtle essences called tanmātra emerge like seeds. Śabda gives rise to space and the veda, while sparśa becomes the wind. Rūpa brings light through the sun and fire. Rasa forms the fluidity of water, and gandha establishes the solid earth. These seeds blend together, expanding into the physical universe while remaining rooted in pure cit.

chapters.subtitle: The mental origin of the first body and world.
chapters.es: Consciousness transforms from a still void into the citta and jīva. Imagining itself as a tiny spark of light, the soul develops senses. Pores open in the ātivāhika-deha to facilitate sight and sound, giving rise to the first creator, Brahmā. This self-arisen being perceives a brahmāṇḍa, or world-egg, within the vast ākāśa. Though the world appears as a sprout from a seed, it lacks physical substance. Nothing is truly born; the universe is simply the movement of consciousness within itself, like a city in a dream.

chapters.subtitle: How individual lives emerge from one supreme consciousness.
chapters.es: The universe and the individual ego arise as ripples in the supreme space of parama-ākāśa. From this stillness, the first creator emerges without prior actions, spreading lives like light from a flame. No multitude of souls exists; only pure consciousness perceives itself as mind and movement. The world clings to consciousness like sweetness to sugarcane or heat to fire. By abandoning the labels of 'I' and 'you,' the shifting world reveals itself as a stable, internal shimmer within the vastness of brahman.

chapters.subtitle: Reality is described and King Padma is introduced.
chapters.es: The universe exists within cit like an uncarved figure in a stone pillar or waves in the sea. It lacks physical substance, resembling a city in a dream or a mirage. This world remains identical to brahman and the emptiness of ākāśa. King Padma rules his land alongside his queen, Līlā. She mirrors her husband’s every emotion, responding to his joy and sorrow like a reflection, as they begin their life together.

chapters.subtitle: Līlā seeks a boon to keep her husband's soul.
chapters.es: King Padma and his queen dwell in garden groves and sandalwood forests, enjoying music and games. Fearing her husband’s mortality, the queen performs austere rites for the goddess Sarasvatī after learning that physical immortality is unattainable. She receives a boon ensuring that the king’s jīva will never leave the palace precincts after death. When he eventually expires like a drying leaf, his spirit remains confined to the interior chambers, while the grieving queen faces the sudden reality of her husband's passing.

chapters.subtitle: Līlā meditates and finds her husband in another world.
chapters.es: Līlā covers her husband’s body with flowers. Seeking him, she prays to the goddess Jñapti and enters the cidākāśa, the ether of consciousness. Her spirit leaves her physical form and finds the king ruling a new realm. He sits on a throne surrounded by soldiers and ambassadors. Though Līlā moves through this court unseen, she recognizes the same ministers and subjects from her past. She eventually returns to her own palace, noticing the striking similarities between the people in both worlds.

chapters.subtitle: Līlā asks Sarasvatī about the reality of the world
chapters.es: Līlā withdraws to the inner palace and sits by her husband’s body. Noticing the resemblance between physical landscapes and mental reflections, she summons Sarasvatī to explain the nature of reality. She questions whether the world is an artificial construct of memory or a tangible truth. Sarasvatī describes the universe as a structure dwelling within the cidākāśa, the space of consciousness. She then recounts the history of a Brāhmaṇa who lived with his family in a remote mountain valley.

chapters.subtitle: A priest desires kingship and attains it after death.
chapters.es: A Brāhmaṇa named Vasiṣṭha lives on a grassy mountain ridge with his wife Arundhatī. Upon seeing a monarch hunting with an army of elephants and silver umbrellas, he develops a longing for royal status. He maintains this intent through old age. When death arrives, his jīva lingers in the gṛhākāśa, the subtle space of his house. Though his corpse lies in the hut, his consciousness, shaped by saṃkalpa, begins to experience the life of a world-conquering king.

chapters.subtitle: Sarasvati explains how a kingdom fits inside a house.
chapters.es: Sarasvatī reveals that Līlā was formerly the wife of a village priest. This priest now rules a kingdom that exists entirely within the small space of his original home. When Līlā questions how vast mountains fit inside a room, Sarasvatī explains that worlds reside within every atom. Upon death, the jīva instantly perceives a new body, parents, and years of history through the power of memory. Like a dream, this world arises as a reflection within the cidākāśa, where time and distance have no physical substance.

chapters.subtitle: Understanding the subtle body to visit other worlds.
chapters.es: Worlds appear instantly after death, shaped by the momentum of smṛti. These realms exist as a wall-less city within the cidākāśa. To visit a specific village in another creation, a traveler must set aside the dense ādhibhautika body, which acts as a heavy bolt barring entry. By thinning out long-held habits of perception through vicāra, the subtle ātivāhika form emerges. Just as a rope is cleared of the snake illusion, one recognizes that these solid forms are merely gold taking the shape of a ring.

chapters.subtitle: Methods for thinning desires and practicing spiritual realization.
chapters.es: Understanding the body as a dream-like construct dissolves physical attachment. When latent impressions, or vāsanā, diminish, the mind transforms into a subtle ātivāhikatā state, similar to ice melting into water. One reaches jīvanmukti by recognizing that the perceived world never truly began. This practice, brahmābhyāsa, requires constant reflection and dialogue. As the ego subsides, the seeker moves beyond the illusions of birth and death, resting in the state of turya while the mist of the mind dissolves.

chapters.subtitle: Līlā and Sarasvatī meditate and travel through space.
chapters.es: In the quiet of the inner palace, Līlā and the goddess Sarasvatī sit beside the king’s body. They enter samādhi, becoming as still as stone carvings. They view the world as a mirage or a rabbit’s horn, discarding all external awareness. Their physical forms give way to bodies of pure consciousness. Rising into the cidākāśa, they travel beyond the physical sky, moving millions of miles through the expanse while maintaining their bond as companions.

chapters.subtitle: Two women travel through the sky and see celestial worlds.
chapters.es: Līlā and Sarasvatī ascend through the sky, holding hands. They rest on clouds and breathe air scented by mandāra flowers. In the expanse, they observe millions of worlds and golden peaks of meru. Celestial chariots carry gods and spirits through regions of eternal sunrise or deep shadow. Musicians like Nārada sing near the floating waters of the Gaṅgā. After witnessing these heavenly cities and dancing yoginī groups, the two women turn their gaze back toward the earth’s surface.

chapters.subtitle: The travelers observe the structure of the universe.
chapters.es: Jñapti and the queen observe the universe as a cosmic hṛtpadma with Mount Meru at its center. They survey Jambūdvīpa, enclosed by alternating rings of oceans and islands such as krauñcadvīpa and puṣkaradvīpa. Their gaze passes beyond the Lokāloka range into zones of fire and violent winds that move mountains like dust. This entire structure rests within a golden shell. Amidst these cosmic layers, the queen identifies her house on the earth's surface.

chapters.subtitle: Līlā comforts her son and removes his sorrow
chapters.es: Līlā and Sarasvatī enter the Brahmin’s home, finding it withered like a lotus in frost. The family mourns the death of the parents, observing even the birds and trees in sorrow. Līlā assumes a visible human form, appearing before her son Jyeṣṭhaśarmā like a goddess. The household offers flowers and seeks relief from their grief. Līlā places her hand on Jyeṣṭhaśarmā’s head, immediately dissolving his misery. This action stems from her understanding that solid forms are mere projections of consciousness.

chapters.subtitle: Lila remembers her many past lives in different worlds.
chapters.es: Lila and Sarasvatī remain invisible within a forest cottage. Sarasvatī explains that countless universes reside within a single atom, just as mustard seeds fill a box. She reveals that Lila’s husband exists simultaneously as a deceased priest, a king’s corpse, and a living monarch. Lila then recalls eight hundred previous births. She remembers living as a tribal śabarī, a Surāṣṭra king, a golden apsarā on Meru, and even a mosquito. These memories of varied forms show the movement of consciousness across multiple worlds.

chapters.subtitle: Jñapti and Līlā visit a village within the mind.
chapters.es: Jñapti and Līlā travel through the cidākāśa, observing that the dense universe is actually empty space shaped by thought. They arrive at a mountain village nestled within a priest's house. Below, waterfalls spray mist over kadamba groves and flowering vines. Children with curd-stained faces play in the courtyards while cows return to their pens. This entire world, though appearing solid as stone, exists only as a mental reflection, as a city appears within a dreamer’s mind while he sleeps at home.

chapters.subtitle: Leela remembers her past life and travels through space.
chapters.es: Leela and Sarasvatī descend into a mountain village where Leela recalls her former life as a brāhmaṇī. She views her previous household, remembering the chores of milking cows and scrubbing pots. Recognizing her husband's spirit within the house, she journeys with the goddess through the celestial spheres. They fly past the paths of the sun and stars, eventually piercing the shell of the brahmāṇḍa. Beyond layers of water and fire, they enter the parama-vyoman, an infinite space devoid of beginning or end.

chapters.subtitle: A vision of countless universes in infinite space
chapters.es: Crossing the elemental shells of earth and water, she enters the paramākāśa. Countless brahmāṇḍa universes drift like motes of dust in a sunbeam or bubbles in a great sea. These worlds move upward, downward, and sideways, yet directions remain relative, like ants circling a stone. Some realms begin as seeds, while others melt away like frost. Diverse creators rule these spheres, which vary from sun-drenched heavens to dark forests where beings dance like spirits, unseen by their neighbors in the vastness.

chapters.subtitle: Līlā enters a new world and observes a battle.
chapters.es: Līlā and the goddess Sarasvatī return to the palace where the king’s flower-covered body rests. They pierce the brahmāṇḍa shell, entering a world born of the king’s saṃkalpa. Above the kingdom of bhāratavarṣa, they witness a sky crowded with apsaras and spirits awaiting a coming battle. A true śūra is defined as a warrior who falls for a righteous cause or a lawful sovereign. These heroes are welcomed by celestial women, while those fighting for unholy rulers descend to niraya.

chapters.subtitle: Līlā watches two armies prepare for battle.
chapters.es: Līlā observes two massive armies gathered in a forest clearing from the sky. Thousands of soldiers stand motionless like figures painted on a wall, their eyes fixed on the enemy. Gold armor glints in the sun as warriors grip paraśvadha axes and bows. The troops arrange themselves in complex formations, including the makara-vyūha and garuḍa-vyūha. As drums fall silent, the gathered force resembles a dark, rising ocean. Flags wave above elephants and cavalry, creating a dense thicket that obscures the horizon.

chapters.subtitle: A large battle breaks out between two armies.
chapters.es: Sarasvatī and Līlā watch from a celestial chariot as two armies collide. Soldiers hurl mudgara clubs and release clouds of arrows that obscure the sun. Swords strike armor, sparking fire, while machines launch stones and boiling oil. Severed limbs and nārāca iron arrows fill the sky. Elephants fall, feeding rivers of blood that sweep across the dust. Warriors grapple with fists and teeth, grinding one another like grain in a mortar until the battlefield resembles the end of a cosmic cycle.

chapters.subtitle: Warriors fight in battle and ascend to heaven.
chapters.es: Soldiers and kings clash on a chaotic battlefield where blood reddens the wind. Severed heads fly through the air, turning the sky into a pond of floating lotuses and umbrellas. Fallen warriors shed their mortal skin to receive the embrace of apsaras in the celestial realm. Elephants collapse like shattered mountains while headless trunks dance amidst the carnage. The rhythmic clashing of swords and śūla spears fills the air as dead heroes instantly awaken in divine bodies within the gardens of heaven.

chapters.subtitle: A fierce battle resembles a storming ocean.
chapters.es: The battlefield becomes a turbulent ocean where leaping horses form waves and white umbrellas drift like foam. Elephants stand as stationary mountains amidst the surge of soldiers in makaravyūha formations. Streams of arrows fall like rain, darkening the sky while rivers of blood sweep away chariots. The clashing of cakra discs and the thunder of drums fill the air. Warriors fall and rise through the dust, mirroring the chaotic dissolution of the world at the end of a kalpa.

chapters.subtitle: Allied and enemy armies gather for a large-scale battle.
chapters.es: Soldiers clash on a battlefield where elephant carcasses form hills and blood gathers in pools. Archers release clouds of arrows while others strike with cakra discs and śūla tridents. King Padma’s army includes forces from the Kosala, Magadha, and Draviḍa lands. They face a vast opposition of western and northern tribes, including the Hūṇa and Śaka groups. This massive gathering of regional powers extends from the southern oceans to the peaks of Himavan and Kailāsa.

chapters.subtitle: Regional armies fight and die on a massive battlefield.
chapters.es: Armies from Śūrasena, Pāñcāla, and Kuru collide on a blood-soaked battlefield. Soldiers fall like moths into flames as swords sever limbs and elephants crush ranks. Karṇāṭaka warriors strike with spears while Gauḍa troops face clubs and Kekaya forces are harried by vultures. Arrows saturate the sky, masking the sun and mimicking dense clouds. Rivers of blood carry away the dead like fallen leaves. In the carnage, some soldiers dance in madness, while others are devoured by piśāca spirits under the cover of night.

chapters.subtitle: The battle ends and the armies leave the field.
chapters.es: Armies clash amidst stone rains and darkened skies until the sun loses its heat. Seeking a truce, commanders send messengers to halt the violence. A soldier waves a white aṃśuka from a chariot to signal the ceasefire. As the forces withdraw, the battlefield remains a desolate expanse of rakta-nadī and severed limbs. Scavengers pull at remains while the dying cry for their families. The ground turns into a forest of broken spears, fallen karīndra corpses, and abandoned chariots.

chapters.subtitle: Spirits feast on a battlefield after sunset.
chapters.es: The sun sinks into the ocean like a wounded warrior, leaving the sky stained red. As darkness covers the earth, stars emerge like scattered pearls. On the silent battlefield, vetālas, piśācas, and ḍākinīs arrive to scavenge. They drink blood and feast on the marrow of fallen soldiers. Funeral pyres crackle in the night, lighting up spirits who fight over remains. The air fills with the scent of burning fat and the cries of nocturnal scavengers amidst the heaps of the dead.

chapters.subtitle: The mind creates physical reality and life after death.
chapters.es: King Līlāpati sleeps on a cool bed while Līlā and Sarasvatī enter his chamber like wind passing through a lotus bud. This movement shows that the ativāhika body remains unobstructed by walls. The mind builds the entire world from the emptiness of cidākāśa. Upon death, the soul instantly perceives a new life shaped by its vāsanā, seeing cities, mountains, and families where none exist. Like a whirlpool in the ocean, the physical universe is merely the movement of consciousness appearing as solid matter.

chapters.subtitle: Vidūratha remembers his past life and learns reality is mental.
chapters.es: King Vidūratha awakes to find Sarasvatī and Līlā seated upon thrones. After he offers a kusumāñjali at their feet, Sarasvatī touches his head to dissolve the veil of māyā. He instantly recalls his former life as King Padma, realizing his current seventy years have passed within a single moment of his previous world. Sarasvatī explains that his kingdom and lineage are mental images existing only within the subtle space of a brāhmaṇa’s house, much like a persistent dream or a reflection.

chapters.subtitle: Sarasvatī explains that the world is a long dream.
chapters.es: The world appears solid to the ignorant, much like a child fears a non-existent vetāla or a thirsty deer pursues a mirage. Just as one might see a golden bracelet without recognizing the gold, the mind perceives objects instead of the underlying cit. This universe functions as a long dream where inhabitants interact with mutual certainty. King Vidūratha, recognizing the dream-like nature of his existence, asks Sarasvatī to guide him and his companions back to his former realm as one moves from one dream to another.

chapters.subtitle: An enemy army attacks and burns King Viduratha's city.
chapters.es: An enemy army besieges the city, launching a rain of arrows and clubs. Fire consumes buildings, sending mountains of dhūma into the sky. King Vidūratha watches from a window as his capital collapses amidst the screams of dying citizens and the trumpeting of panicked elephants. Soldiers seize weeping women, whose necklaces break and scatter pearls across the blood-stained streets. Families perish in burning homes while falling walls crush children. The city transforms into a landscape of ash, flame, and violent abduction.

chapters.subtitle: Sarasvatī explains how the mind perceives and creates reality.
chapters.es: A disheveled queen rushes to the king, reporting that enemies have set the city ablaze and captured the palace women. The king departs for battle like a lion leaving a cave. Līlā observes another version of herself and her court, identical in form and action. Sarasvatī explains that this world arises from citti and pratibhā, appearing like a svapna or a rajjusarpa illusion. Consciousness reflects these forms within the cidvyoma, where birth and death remain mere fluctuations of the mind.

chapters.subtitle: A goddess grants a wish and explains consciousness.
chapters.es: The goddess Jñapti grants Līlā a boon to remain with her husband, King Vidūratha, after he falls in battle. Līlā asks to reach his new realm while retaining her physical form. Jñapti attributes this success to the queen’s devotion and the power of saṃvit, or consciousness. Every soul possesses a cicchakti that translates long-held intentions into reality. Results occur through internal effort, much like fruit falling from the sky when the time is right.

chapters.subtitle: King Vidūratha leads his army into a fierce battle.
chapters.es: Vidūratha dons his armor and mounts a chariot decorated with pearls and gold. As he leads his forces, the roar of dundubhi drums and clashing steel fills the sky. A thick cloud of dust obscures the sun, plunging the city into darkness. Līlā and the goddess observe the combat through divine vision. Blood settles the rising dust as rivers flow past dancing kabandha trunks. Soldiers fight with silent resolve, turning the field into an ocean of whistling arrows and clashing bhuśuṇḍi weapons.

chapters.subtitle: A goddess explains fate while two kings fight.
chapters.es: Jñapti explains that saṃvit responds to the specific intent of a seeker; because Sindhu desires victory while Vidūratha seeks mukti, the former triumphs in battle. At sunrise, the field reveals a landscape of severed limbs, rivers of blood, and scavenging vetāla spirits. Amidst this carnage, the two kings clash in their chariots like the sun and moon. They exchange volleys of nārāca arrows and heavy stones, their weapons filling the sky until the air mimics the turbulence of a final dissolution.

chapters.subtitle: Vidūratha and Sindhu fight using magical elemental weapons.
chapters.es: Vidūratha and Sindhu exchange volleys of arrows that obscure the sky like rain clouds. When Sindhu releases a mohanāstra to paralyze the army, Vidūratha revives them with an awakening weapon. Serpent missiles fill the air, only to be consumed by golden garuḍa birds. Light and darkness collide as sun weapons pierce magical shadows. After fire engulfs the horizon, a flood of water douses the flames, which a drying wind eventually evaporates. Finally, a heavy gale shatters stones across the battlefield.

chapters.subtitle: Kings use magical weapons to summon ghosts and monsters.
chapters.es: Violent winds toss Vidūratha’s chariot until he calms them with the pārvatāstra. King Sindhu counters with the vajrāstra to shatter falling mountain peaks, and Vidūratha responds with the brahmāstra. Sindhu then summons piśāca spirits that paralyze the opposing troops. Vidūratha turns this illusion back, prompting Sindhu to release rūpikā ghouls who carry severed heads and entrails. The battle escalates as vetāla vampires and mountain-sized rākṣasa giants emerge, drinking blood and dancing among the dead on the gore-soaked field.

chapters.subtitle: King Sindhu severely wounds King Vidūratha in combat.
chapters.es: Vidūratha invokes the vaiṣṇavāstra, unleashing spinning cakra wheels and iron maces into the sky. Sindhu counters with a matching weapon, and the opposing volleys collide like rivers of metal. When Vidūratha burns his rival's chariot with an āgneyāstra, Sindhu douses the fire with water and strikes on foot. The combatants duel until their swords resemble saws. Finally, Sindhu severs Vidūratha’s knees and throat. A charioteer retreats with the mutilated king, placing his bloodied body before the goddesses.

chapters.subtitle: A kingdom transitions from wartime chaos to peaceful rule.
chapters.es: The king’s death in battle triggers immediate panic. Citizens flee with carts as looters ransack the capital, leaving streets uneven with fallen gems and the palace courtyard wet with entrails. This chaos ends when the victor, Sindhu, enters the city to assume the throne, appearing like the progenitor Manu. His ministers distribute decrees across the realm, establishing laws for every province. The upheaval subsides instantly, resembling the milk ocean after the removal of mount Mandara. Stability and peace return to the land.

chapters.subtitle: Sarasvatī explains how multiple worlds exist within the mind.
chapters.es: Līlā watches her husband’s breath fade. Sarasvatī explains that this vast battlefield and kingdom exist within a priest's house in the Vindhya mountains. Countless worlds reside within a single cid-aṇu, or consciousness-atom, layered like the leaves of a plantain tree. The goddess reveals that the king and the two versions of Līlā are mental projections. As the queen’s life-breath departs, her jīva travels through the inner sky to reunite with her husband, driven by the force of memory.

chapters.subtitle: Lila travels across the universe to find her husband.
chapters.es: Līlā ascends into the sky, guided by a maiden sent by wisdom. She passes through layers of wind, the sun’s path, and the stars until she pierces the cosmic shell. Emerging into the infinite expanse of consciousness, she observes countless universes before entering one particular world. Like a worm entering a fruit, she descends into a palace and finds her husband’s body resting under flowers. She takes a fly-whisk to fan him, recognizing this realm as a projection of pure consciousness and internal saṃkalpa.

chapters.subtitle: How cosmic laws work and what happens during death
chapters.es: Pure consciousness perceives itself as a spark and expands into the appearance of a world. This initial movement of thought, or saṃvit, becomes a fixed order governing the heat of fire and the coldness of snow. While a trained mind leaves the body with ease, an ignorant person experiences death as a violent descent into darkness, feeling as though they are tossed in whirlpools or falling through endless space. The conscious self remains untouched while birth and death occur like knots along a growing vine.

chapters.subtitle: How the soul travels from death to new birth.
chapters.es: When breath ceases, the body becomes inert while the jīva carries its vāsanā into a dream-like state. Sinners encounter a stone-like stupor or hellish forests, while the virtuous experience celestial cities. These spirits eventually descend through vegetation and human fluids to enter a new womb. This cycle of birth, aging, and death repeats as a movement within the cidākāśa. Finally, the spirit of Vidūratha leaves his dying frame to travel toward the pavilion where the body of Padma lies.

chapters.subtitle: A king's soul journeys to the afterlife and returns.
chapters.es: King Padma’s breath departs his withered frame like a bird leaving a falling tree. His jīva drifts into the sky, followed by Līlā and Sarasvatī. Upon reaching Yama’s city, the soul is directed back to its corpse because of its virtuous life. Guided by internal vāsanā, the jīva descends through the heavens. The three beings re-enter the silent palace chamber where the king’s body rests under heaps of mandāra and kunda blossoms.

chapters.subtitle: Leela sees her own body beside the dead king.
chapters.es: The two visitors observe the original līlā reclining beside the king’s corpse. She wears her former ornaments and fans the ruler with a cāmara. Though visible to them, she cannot perceive the observers. The physical body, or ādhibhautika deha, is shown to be a mental habit. Like a mountain in a dream, the material form dissolves into the subtle ātivāhika state once the mind recognizes that all perceived objects exist only as consciousness, or saṃvid.

chapters.subtitle: The king awakens and receives blessings from Sarasvatī.
chapters.es: Jñapti reveals that Līlā’s physical body was cremated after ministers found it cold like wood. Līlā now moves in an ātivāhika form shaped by her mental impressions. They enter the royal chamber where Jñapti directs the jīva into the king’s nostrils. His limbs regain warmth and he awakens to see two identical versions of his wife. Recognizing the goddess Sarasvatī, the king bows at her feet. She touches him and grants blessings of wisdom, longevity, and a peaceful reign for his people.

chapters.subtitle: King Padma returns to life and rules his kingdom.
chapters.es: After Sarasvatī vanishes at dawn, King Padma wakes in his palace alongside both versions of Līlā. The court celebrates with the sound of mṛdaṅga drums and conch shells. Ministers and priests perform a formal abhiṣeka, consecrating the king with waters from the four oceans. Recognizing their state as jīvanmukta, the trio governs for eighty thousand years, recounting their experiences across various realms. They eventually transition from earthly rule to videhamukti, achieving final liberation beyond the physical body.

chapters.subtitle: The mind creates time and the physical world.
chapters.es: Consciousness projects the world like a dream without a physical base. Perception alone dictates reality; a single night expands into years for King Hariścandra, while a moment becomes a kalpa for those in pain. Just as ripples follow a strong wind, individual minds align to perceive shared histories. Objects possess no fixed nature, as thought transforms poison into nectar or enemies into friends. This movement remains a vibration of the cidākāśa, appearing solid yet remaining as empty as space.

chapters.subtitle: The world is identical to the universal consciousness.
chapters.es: The world arises from brahman without an external cause, just as a bracelet is formed from gold or waves emerge from water. Consciousness perceives itself as the five elements, assuming the form of wind through motion and fire through light. These forms reside within the absolute like figures uncarved in stone or saltiness in the sea. Though the mind perceives a separate "I" and universe, they remain identical to the underlying cit. This manifestation is a vibration of the self, neither truly existent nor non-existent.

chapters.subtitle: The relationship between cosmic destiny and human effort.
chapters.es: Worlds emerge within every atom like ripples on water or blossoms on a desert mirage. A fixed cosmic order called niyati governs these realms, setting the roles of gods, men, and elements. This destiny acts as the primary vibration of consciousness, mahāspanda. It does not replace human effort, or pauruṣa, but functions through it. Whether a person acts or remains still, they participate in this inherent law. All movement flows from brahman, appearing as steady as patterns carved in stone.

chapters.subtitle: The infinite reality appears as many through its powers
chapters.es: The infinite ātman holds all powers, appearing as consciousness, peace, or inert matter. Whatever the mind conceives, it immediately perceives. While the wise use categories like śakti and śaktimat for worldly transactions, no true division exists. Like waves in the sea or gold shaped into a bracelet, the distinction between part and whole is a mental construct. This reality does not come from without or within; it simply shines as the singular brahman through whatever form the mind dwells upon.

chapters.subtitle: How the individual soul and the world begin
chapters.es: The infinite brahman undergoes a subtle stir, much like movement in air or heat in fire, giving rise to the jīva. This consciousness becomes the ego, which then projects the five basic elements. Like a seed sprouting into a tree, the mind assumes diverse forms, from stationary plants to the first creator, brahmā. Intent and internal vibrations become karma, binding the soul to physical existence. The world emerges from these mental ripples, manifesting as a continuous sequence of birth and death.

chapters.subtitle: The mind creates the world from pure consciousness.
chapters.es: The mind emerges from the ultimate cause and projects the world as a scent spreads through air. Pure consciousness, or cit, moves like an ocean creating its own waves. This world persists as a long dream or a ghost imagined by a frightened child. From the notion of an individual soul, the ego and physical body arise through mistaken perception. Action and form remain inseparable. Ultimately, the body, mind, and individual soul are identical to the single, underlying reality of śiva.

chapters.subtitle: How the mind creates the illusion of the world.
chapters.es: One reality multiplies like light from a single lamp. The mind creates the world’s movement, just as a man wearing leather shoes perceives the entire earth as carpeted. This cycle of birth and death mirrors a child’s dizziness or a drunkard’s swaying trees. Like a plantain tree made only of leaves, the universe consists entirely of thought. By discarding vāsanā and recognizing the rope-snake illusion, the individual halts the mind's dance and attains a state of saṃśānta.

chapters.subtitle: Consciousness becomes the soul and creates the physical body
chapters.es: Pure consciousness stirs like a ripple on a quiet sea, becoming the jīva through its own movement. This individual soul develops a mind that imagines sense organs, forming ears to perceive sound and eyes to catch light. These mental constructs solidify into a physical body, though the world remains as insubstantial as a city in the clouds. Just as gold is shaped into a bracelet, the mind carves distinct forms from one reality. Recognition of this origin dissolves the illusion of birth and death.

chapters.subtitle: A demon performs penance to satisfy her great hunger.
chapters.es: The rākṣasī Karkaṭī dwells on the northern Himalayan slopes, possessed by a hunger as relentless as the vaḍavānala fire. Her dark, skeletal form features eyes like lightning and ornaments made of human bone. Realizing that the people of Jambūdvīpa are protected by mantras and herbs, she resolves to gain power through tapas. She stands motionless on one leg atop a mountain for several seasons. Her hair rises toward the sky until Brahma arrives to acknowledge her steady penance.

chapters.subtitle: Brahmā transforms Karkaṭī into a disease and provides healing.
chapters.es: After a thousand years of penance, Brahmā grants the ogress Karkaṭī a boon. She chooses to become a jīvasūcikā, a living needle, to satisfy her hunger by entering living beings. Brahmā transforms her into the disease viṣūcikā, ordaining that she afflict those with poor diets and bad habits. To counter her path, Brahmā reveals a mantra and a healing rite. This ritual involves visualizing the patient in the cooling candramaṇḍala while the disease is driven back to the himālaya mountains.

chapters.subtitle: A demoness takes the form of a tiny needle.
chapters.es: The demoness shrinks from her mountain-like stature until she becomes as small as a needle. She exists in two forms: a physical iron ayas-sūcī and a subtle jīva-sūcī shaped by her desires. Moving through dust, fabric, and mud, she enters human bodies to cause the pain of visūcikā. She wanders the world, piercing limbs and drinking blood. Even in this diminished state, she finds satisfaction in small acts of cruelty, hiding in rags or the hearts of the sick to continue her destructive path.

chapters.subtitle: Karkaṭī laments her transformation into a needle.
chapters.es: Karkaṭī the rākṣasī laments her transformation into a tiny sūcī. Having abandoned her mountain-like form through penance, she now finds herself unable to satisfy her hunger for human flesh. A single drop of blood fills her microscopic body, yet her craving remains unyielding. She drifts like a fallen leaf, suffers under the feet of passersby, and sinks into the mud. Recalling her former limbs that once touched the clouds, she regrets trading her physical power for a fragile and helpless existence.

chapters.subtitle: A needle performs intense penance on a mountain peak.
chapters.es: The needle sūcī travels to a desolate Himalayan peak to perform tapas. Balancing on her singular point, she stands motionless for seven thousand years. She ignores the onslaught of forest fires, torrential rain, and mountain winds, consuming only air through her small eye. This intense discipline purifies her mind, leading to the dawn of jñāna. Her ascetic energy eventually generates a heat so fierce that it scorches the worlds, prompting Indra to seek the cause of this universal disturbance from the sage Nārada.

chapters.subtitle: A needle-shaped ghost performs penance on a mountain.
chapters.es: Karkaṭī, as a microscopic jīvasūcī, enters living bodies through the breath. She travels through blood and sinews, causing internal pain like a sharp thorn. Despite consuming bodily essences, her lack of a physical frame leaves her perpetually hungry. To regain her mountain-sized form, she hitches a ride within a vulture’s heart to a desolate peak in the Himālaya. There, she anchors to an iron needle to perform tapas. Sensing her power, Indra dispatches the wind to locate her across the seven oceans.

chapters.subtitle: A needle performs extreme penance on a mountain peak.
chapters.es: The needle Sūcī performs severe tapas on a peak in the Himālaya. Standing on her single point, she survives on air and moonlight while dust covers her shriveled belly. The wind god Pavana observes her radiance and reports her progress to Indra. Alarmed by the heat of her austerity, the gods seek help from Brahmā. Her discipline creates a sacred trikoṇa as holy as Vārāṇasī, where even passing dust particles find liberation. Brahmā promises to visit the mountain and grant her a boon.

chapters.subtitle: Karkati becomes a giant again and achieves liberation.
chapters.es: Brahma returns to the needle-sized sūcī after her thousand-year penance. Although she finds peace in silence and seeks no favors, Brahma directs her to resume her physical form to fulfill her destiny. She expands from a minute point of consciousness into a massive rākṣasī. Her body grows like a tree from a seed, eventually reaching the size of mountain clouds. In this state, she lives as a jīvanmukta, maintaining inner purity while acting with justice in the physical world.

chapters.subtitle: Karkaṭī regains her demon body and begins her hunt.
chapters.es: Karkaṭī sheds her needle-form to regain her massive rākṣasī body. She sits in baddhapadmāsana on a mountain peak, meditating for six months until she attains clarity. When hunger returns, she vows to consume only what is earned through nyāya. A celestial voice commands her to enlighten the ignorant, declaring that those who refuse wisdom become her lawful prey. Karkaṭī descends the slopes into a Kirāta settlement under the cover of a moonless night, seeking those she might rightfully eat.

chapters.subtitle: A demoness decides to test a king and minister.
chapters.es: A thick darkness like black collyrium covers the mountain territory, hiding the moon under heavy clouds. King Vikrama and his minister enter the forest for a courageous night vigil. The demoness Karkaṭī watches them, initially viewing them as prey designated by the creator for hunters. She pauses to consider if they possess wisdom, as she values the virtuous. To determine their character, she decides to test their knowledge with questions rather than attacking them immediately.

chapters.subtitle: A giant demoness challenges two men with difficult questions.
chapters.es: A dark rākṣasī roars in the forest, her body resembling a massive black cloud with copper eyes and protruding veins. She threatens to devour the king and his minister, but they remain steady, demanding she state her purpose. Recognizing their composure, she realizes they might be knowers of the self, or ātmajña. She suppresses her laughter and challenges them to answer her questions. She promises to spare their lives only if they can solve her riddles.

chapters.subtitle: A demoness poses complex riddles to a king.
chapters.es: The rākṣasī confronts a king and his minister with a sequence of riddles. She demands they identify the minute aṇu that contains entire universes like bubbles in an ocean. Her queries describe a fire that does not burn, a light independent of sun or moon, and a single moment that encompasses an entire kalpa. She questions the nature of the ātman as a perceiver without eyes and a seed containing the world-tree. Failure to resolve these paradoxes results in their immediate consumption.

chapters.subtitle: The infinite self exists as a tiny conscious atom.
chapters.es: Consciousness exists as an infinitesimal atom, the cidaṇu, which holds the entire universe just as a seed contains a potential tree. This self is finer than space and remains motionless even when moving, as distance and time reside within its own nature. Like gold appearing as a bracelet, the single reality appears as diverse objects without changing. It remains hidden from the senses yet shines as an inner light, capable of stretching a single nimeṣa into a vast kalpa through the mind’s perception.

chapters.subtitle: The universe exists inside the atom of consciousness.
chapters.es: The cid-aṇu holds the entire universe within itself, much like a tiny bīja contains a massive tree with its branches and fruit. This consciousness projects mountains and oceans while remaining unchanged, just as gold becomes a kaṭaka without losing its essence. The cid-āditya illuminates both light and darkness, showing they share one substance. Within this subtle atom, a single moment expands into an eon. All perceived objects, from the sun to a blade of grass, exist as the movement of this pure awareness.

chapters.subtitle: A demoness stops killing and provides a healing mantra.
chapters.es: Karkaṭī abandons her malice after hearing the King’s discourse. She reveals her identity as the needle-shaped disease, Viṣūcikā, born from ancient penance. After purifying themselves in a river, she grants the King a mantra to cure the plague. Transforming into a woman, she accompanies the King to his palace. He provides her with condemned criminals as food, which she carries to the Himalayas. She alternates between long years of samādhi and returning to the kingdom for her sustenance.

chapters.subtitle: A demoness becomes a protective deity for mountain tribes.
chapters.es: The rākṣasī Karkaṭī establishes friendships with the kings of the kirāta tribes, employing yogic powers to repel diseases and malevolent spirits. During her long periods of meditation, the people install her image in cities under the names Kandarā and Maṅgaletarā to ensure continued protection. Local rulers offer her condemned criminals as food, honoring their pact. This practice endures as kings consecrate her statues to ward off calamities. Today, she is still worshipped as an enlightened deity who fulfills the desires of the mountain people.

chapters.subtitle: Explaining how the mind creates the world through stories.
chapters.es: Following the account of the demoness Karkaṭī, the narrative compares the world’s appearance to waves rising from water or fire latent in wood. The world functions as a mental expansion, like a city in the sky or a ghost imagined by a child. While the seed and fruit seem distinct, they share a single essence. Inquiry serves as medicine for the restless mind. Effective teaching uses clear analogies to spread knowledge, just as oil moves across the surface of water.

chapters.subtitle: Brahma discovers many universes in his mind.
chapters.es: Brahma wakes after the cosmic dissolution and performs his morning sandhyā. Finding the physical sky empty, he looks through his subtle mind and discovers ten other creators seated upon lotuses within the manasāmbaram. These mental universes contain rotating seasons, the meru mountains, and the tripathagā river. Brahma summons a sun from one of these realms to explain their origin.
