chapters.es: The ātman remains steady regardless of the body’s birth or dissolution. Like air passing through the sky, the self inhabits the physical frame without clinging to it. Even when mountains collapse or cosmic fires consume the world, the inner witness does not shake. The individual performs actions but discards the notion of being an agent. By ending the link between the grasper and the grasped, the mind achieves a state of peace. The wise move through the world like mirrors reflecting images without desiring them.

chapters.subtitle: Viṣṇu crowns Prahlāda and instructs him on ruling.
chapters.es: Prahlāda awakens from his meditation and addresses Viṣṇu. He describes his rest in the absolute self, free from the distinctions of loss or gain. After offering an arghyapātra to the deity, he accepts his role in the world. Viṣṇu performs a royal abhiṣeka, pouring waters from the sacred oceans and the Gaṅgā over the demon king. Installed on the throne, Prahlāda receives instructions to rule with an equal mind, ending the ancient cycle of war between gods and demons.

chapters.subtitle: Viṣṇu leaves Prahlāda and explains two types of liberation
chapters.es: Viṣṇu departs from the demon palace on Garuḍa’s wings, returning to his serpent couch upon the ocean of milk. This transition marks the distinction between jīvanmukti, liberation while living, and videhamukti, liberation after death. For the living-liberated, past impressions are like roasted seeds that cannot sprout into new births. The king awakens from deep meditation to the sound of the pāñcajanya conch. Vasiṣṭha explains that steady inquiry dispels the darkness of the world, showing that self-realization and the divine are identical.

chapters.subtitle: Self-effort and mental control lead to spiritual liberation.
chapters.es: Prahlāda attained liberation through personal effort rather than external divine favor. Viṣṇu and the Ātman are identical, like oil within a sesame seed. While formal worship of the four-armed deity aids the restless, true realization requires self-inquiry and the discipline of manonigraha. One crosses the ocean of saṃsāra by subduing the five senses. External gods cannot rescue those who refuse to examine their own minds; only the steady cultivation of pure saṃvid ends the cycle of rebirth.

chapters.subtitle: A man watches his own death and cremation.
chapters.es: The Brahmin Gādhi performs penance by standing neck-deep in a lake for eight months. When Viṣṇu appears, Gādhi asks to witness the nature of māyā. Later, while submerged during a ritual bath, he experiences a vision of his own death. He sees his pale corpse surrounded by a weeping wife and mother. His relatives carry the body to a śmaśāna filled with vultures and jackals. They place him on a pyre, where the flames consume his flesh and reduce him to ashes.

chapters.subtitle: Gādhi dreams of being an outcaste and a king.
chapters.es: Submerged in water, Gādhi perceives himself born as a śvapaca in a forest village. He grows into a hunter, weds, and raises a family in the Vindhya wilds. When death claims his kin, he wanders into the city of the Kīra people. A royal elephant selects him from the crowd, lifting him onto its back. Ministers anoint him with sandalwood and gems, crowning the former hunter as King Gavala. He assumes the throne, ruling the kingdom in state luxury.

chapters.subtitle: A king’s true identity is revealed, leading to tragedy.
chapters.es: King Kīra rules for eight years until an old śvapaca recognizes him as a kinsman. The court and citizens, feeling defiled by an outcaste ruler, treat the king like a corpse and refuse his commands. Desperate for purification, the ministers and their families leap into funeral pyres. Surrounded by a burning city, the king throws himself into the fire. As the flames consume his limbs, Gadhi awakens from the long illusion while standing waist-deep in water.

chapters.subtitle: Gādhi travels to confirm his vision of another life.
chapters.es: Gādhi emerges from a river after a trance, questioning the reality of his life as an outcaste. An ascetic guest visits his hermitage and describes a king from the Kīra country who was revealed as a śvapaca and died by fire. Seeking the truth, Gādhi travels to the Bhūtamaṇḍala region. He finds the ruined hut of Kaṭañja exactly as envisioned, surrounded by scattered bones. Villagers confirm the historical existence of the outcaste king, proving Gādhi’s internal vision had external reality.

chapters.subtitle: Gādhi learns that his past lives were mental illusions.
chapters.es: Gādhi enters a śvapaca hut filled with dry bones and lion skins. He identifies the spots where he once ate monkey meat and drank maireya. Traveling to the Kīra kingdom, he confirms that an outcaste king ruled there before entering a funeral pyre. Disturbed by these tangible memories, Gādhi performs penance until Viṣṇu appears. The deity explains that the mind projects these experiences from internal vāsanā. He reveals that the outcaste life and the royal reign were mental constructs without external reality.

chapters.subtitle: Gādhi performs penance and learns the nature of illusion.
chapters.es: Gādhi wanders the earth and finds physical traces of his life as a śvapaca. Confused by this overlap of vision and reality, he petitions Viṣṇu. The deity explains how the mind projects māyā through saṃkalpa, likening shared delusions to a common dream or the kākatālīya coincidence. Following Viṣṇu’s command, Gādhi performs ten years of penance in the Ṛṣyamūka mountains. He stills his mental constructs, attains self-knowledge, and remains as a jīvanmukta, finally free from the grip of worldly illusion.

chapters.subtitle: Conquering the mind to end the cycle of rebirth.
chapters.es: The mind acts as the central hub of a rotating wheel that generates the illusions of existence. To stop this motion, one targets the mind directly through persistent effort rather than external rituals. When the mind ceases to form saṃkalpa, or mental constructs, the world dissolves like space when a clay pot shatters. Using the tools of śāstra and inquiry, the seeker cuts down mental habits personified as wild elephants and vultures. By resting in pure saṃvit, the individual views the body with indifference.

chapters.subtitle: Uddālaka searches for a place to practice meditation.
chapters.es: On the slopes of Gandhamādana, the seeker Uddālaka contemplates the nature of liberation. He aspires to a state where mental constructs cease and the mind no longer leaps like a restless monkey between sense objects. Envisioning himself as motionless as a stone, he yearns for a stillness where birds might nest in his hair. Despite initial struggles with sleep and distraction, he eventually finds a secluded, jewel-like cave. There, he prepares to enter nirvikalpa samādhi, leaving behind all worldly thirst.

chapters.subtitle: A seeker investigates his body and mind to find truth.
chapters.es: A seeker enters a mountain cave and prepares a seat of leaves and deerskin. Sitting in padmāsana, he begins vicāraṇā by questioning his own mind. He compares sensory traps to a worm bound in a cocoon. Examining his body, he identifies flesh, blood, and bones, but finds no individual ego within these parts. He dismisses the "I" as a phantom and instructs his senses to function without attachment. He finally rests in the state of pure awareness, free from latent desires.

chapters.subtitle: The self remains separate from the body and mind.
chapters.es: Consciousness remains unstained while the body of blood and flesh stays inert like a corpse without it. The ahaṃkāra functions as a mirage that vanishes like frost under the sun during inquiry. Without this ego, the tree of saṃsāra withers and produces no further fruit. The mind and body sustain each other like a lake and a cloud, but their bond breaks when the intellect separates the self from the senses. The seeker then rests in the ātman, still and clear like the autumn sky.

chapters.subtitle: Uddālaka meditates and reaches a state of liberation.
chapters.es: Uddālaka sits in padmāsana and chants om. Through recaka, internal fire reduces his physical form to white ash. During kumbhaka, the winds settle, and via pūraka, lunar nectar reconstructs his limbs into a form resembling nārāyaṇa. He tethers his mind like an elephant, systematically dissolving mental darkness, light, and sleep. He enters citsāmānya, attaining a state of stable bliss. Though celestial beings offer him vimāna transport to heaven, he remains indifferent, living as a jīvanmukta among mountain caves and forest hermitages.

chapters.subtitle: Uddalaka attains liberation and leaves his physical body
chapters.es: Uddalaka reaches the state of sattāsāmānya by stilling his mind within a mountain cave. Sitting in padmāsana, he regulates his breath and closes the nine gates of the body until his individual consciousness merges into brahman. His physical form remains motionless for months, weathering into a dry skeleton resembling a bone lute. Eventually, divine goddesses descend from the sky, and the deity Cāmuṇḍā collects his sun-bleached remains to wear as an ornament upon her crown.

chapters.subtitle: True meditation is inner peace despite outward activity.
chapters.es: Inner coolness, or antaḥ-śītalatā, defines true samādhi regardless of whether one acts in the world or sits in seclusion. A person with thin latent desires, or vāsanā, remains a non-doer even while busy, whereas a restless meditator performs mental actions even in silence. For the tranquil, a crowded city feels like a desert, and for the agitated, a forest feels like a bustling market. This state of mind projects its own temperature onto the world, rendering the liberated individual as untainted as gold in mud.

chapters.subtitle: The self contains all things like saltiness in salt.
chapters.es: The ātman contains ego, time, and space as inherent properties, much like pungency within a pepper or saltiness within salt. Just as a stone is hard and water is fluid, the consciousness experiences world-forms within itself. Branches belong to the tree and emptiness defines the sky. These appearances of ahaṃtā do not differ from the underlying reality. The individual jīva and the absolute remain a single, unborn substance, existing like a great mountain of solid consciousness.

chapters.subtitle: A king learns self-inquiry from a visiting sage.
chapters.es: Suraghu, a king of the kirāta people on Mount Kailāsa, struggles with the burden of governing. He finds the acts of punishing and rewarding citizens distressing, feeling the pain of others as his own. When the sage Māṇḍavya visits, the king seeks a way to end his mental agitation. Māṇḍavya instructs him to practice vicāra by asking "Who am I?" Through self-inquiry and the renunciation of all external perceptions, the king learns that the ātman remains once the mind discards worldly attachments.

chapters.subtitle: King Suraghu finds his true self through self-inquiry.
chapters.es: After Sage Māṇḍavya departs, Suraghu sits alone to ask ko nāmāham, questioning his own identity. He rejects his kingdom, city, and family as mere names. Examining his physical form, he discards flesh, bones, and blood as insentient matter. He looks beyond the karmendriya and the mind, finding only pure cit remaining. This ātman connects all beings like a thread passing through pearls. Realizing that the world is a dance performed by the mind, he abandons concerns over punishment or reward and rests in his infinite nature.

chapters.subtitle: King Suraghu rules with detachment and attains final liberation.
chapters.es: Suraghu attains the highest state through discernment. He rules his kingdom for centuries without attachment, performing daily tasks like the steady sun. His mind remains calm, resembling a windless lamp flame or the deep ocean. He views the entire world as pure consciousness (cittattva), remaining in constant samādhi while active. Eventually, he leaves his physical form like a melting dewdrop. His individual soul merges into the ultimate cause, just as the space inside a broken jar becomes one with the infinite sky.

chapters.subtitle: A king finds peace and meets an old friend.
chapters.es: Parigha, ruler of the pārasīka kingdom, abandons his realm following a catastrophic famine. He retreats to a mountain cave, subsisting entirely on fallen leaves, which earns him the name Parṇāda. After a millennium of meditation, he achieves internal clarity and wanders the earth as a jīvanmukta. Eventually, he arrives at the palace of his old friend Suraghu. The two kings embrace, share a throne, and inquire about each other's spiritual health and the prosperity of their former subjects.

chapters.subtitle: Defining meditation as constant awareness, not just sitting still.
chapters.es: The speaker redefines samādhi as tattvāvabodha, or the steady realization of truth, rather than the physical stillness of a seated padmāsana posture. A mind established in this clarity remains meditative while performing daily actions, much as a river flows without interruption or time tracks its own passage. This state remains free from ego and the tension of opposites. For the enlightened, the distinction between active work and silent absorption disappears, as the mind rests permanently in the ātman, regardless of external movement or quietude.

chapters.subtitle: A king explains his lack of worldly desire.
chapters.es: The king rests in quietude, his heart cooled like a full moon or a clear autumn sky. He views the world of mountains and forests as a hollow assembly of bone, flesh, and clay. Because no object possesses inherent value, he moves beyond the need for acceptance or rejection. Concepts of the insignificant, tuccha, and the significant, atuccha, no longer stir his mind. With desire gone, the heat of passion fades like sunlight at dusk, leaving his inner self in a steady, silent state of upaśama.

chapters.subtitle: Overcoming the ego to find inner peace.
chapters.es: After Suraghu and Parigha conclude their inquiry, instructions turn to the dissolution of ahaṃkāra. As the ego-cloud vanishes from the heart’s sky, the sun of consciousness shines. A person stays free from worldly stains, like a lotus leaf in water. The individual soul, resembling an ox trapped in the mud of saṃsāra, is rescued through self-effort rather than external aid. By viewing the body as wood or stone and dismantling the mind, one experiences a silent, internal peace resembling deep sleep.

chapters.subtitle: Two friends mourn their parents on Mount Sahya.
chapters.es: If the mind is not severed from the self, the cycle of saṃsṛti persists like a flooding ocean. On the slopes of Mount Sahya, amidst waterfalls and gem-studded cliffs, Sage Atri maintains an āśrama. Two young men, Bhāsa and Vilāsa, grow up here as inseparable companions, sharing a single mind like fragrance within a flower. When their aged parents depart for heaven, the brothers perform the ūrdhvadehika rites. Overwhelmed by sorrow, they remain motionless, resembling figures in a painting.

chapters.subtitle: Two old ascetics discuss the suffering of worldly life.
chapters.es: Two aging ascetics meet in a forest, their bodies withered like trees near a ravine. Despite years of penance, they remain without vimalajñāna. One ascetic describes the cycle of saṃsāra as a recurring disease. He likens the mind to a drunken elephant and life to a thread gnawed by the rat of death. They observe how youth fades into the flowers of jarā while the mind remains trapped in a whirlpool of anxieties and endless worldly actions.

chapters.subtitle: Mental attachment to the body causes human suffering.
chapters.es: A charioteer remains unharmed when his vehicle breaks, just as the self stands apart from the body’s distress. Consciousness and the physical form meet like wood floating in water, creating a temporary reflection of worldly experience. Suffering persists only through internal attachment, antaḥ-saṅga, which serves as the seed of rebirth. One who abandons this mental clinging performs actions without becoming a doer, or kartṛ. Like a boat carrying cargo without becoming the wood it transports, the unattached mind remains still and free from the results of its deeds.

chapters.subtitle: How attachment causes suffering and how to overcome it.
chapters.es: Attachment binds the self to the body, forcing beings into suffering like donkeys carrying heavy loads or deer fleeing arrows. While the ignorant stay trapped in vandhyā attachment, the wise practice the vandyā form. Deities like Viṣṇu and the Sun move through the three worlds, performing duties without inner bondage. By abandoning the sense of "I," a jīvanmukta acts with freedom, remaining as expansive as a great river in the monsoon while staying untouched by the fire of greed.

chapters.subtitle: How a person stays detached while performing daily actions.
chapters.es: A person engages in worldly duties while the mind remains detached from physical points like the brow, palate, or heart. This mind avoids attachment to colors, senses, or the states of waking and dreaming. Resting purely in citi, the individual performs actions without being bound by their results, much as clouds do not affect the vast sky. Even while carrying the physical body, they stay as still and luminous as a jewel, indifferent to the fruits of karma.

chapters.subtitle: A person stays calm while acting in the world.
chapters.es: The individual performs worldly actions while keeping the inner self still. Like a polished sphāṭika crystal, the mind does not catch the colors of pleasure or pain. This person works like a mechanical puppet, moving through life in a state of deep sleep while fully awake. They act without the sense of being the doer, similar to a child shifting on a bed. Eventually, this stillness matures into turya. Finally, the self dissolves into the ultimate reality like a lump of salt melting in water.

chapters.subtitle: The soul stays free while the mind creates rebirth
chapters.es: The ātman remains distinct from the physical body, just as light never mixes with darkness. While the body moves by the force of internal winds like air through a hollow flute, the soul is an all-pervading reality comparable to a shoreless ocean where waves are water itself. The antaḥkaraṇa carries the reflection of this consciousness, binding the jīva to rebirth through vāsanā. Like a monkey jumping between trees, the mind carries the individual between bodies until it finally dissolves in liberation.

chapters.subtitle: Understanding how the soul is separate from the body.
chapters.es: The soul survives the body just as space remains when a clay jar breaks. This physical form consists of the bhūtapañcaka, assembled like wooden puppets from a single tree. Although mind and body touch, they remain as separate as two stone statues leaning together. A jīvanmukta acts in the world without attachment, resembling an ocean that carries jewels but is not colored by them. By looking past the connection of dṛśyadarśana, one sees that the five elements are only the self.

chapters.subtitle: A clear mind sees the soul in everything.
chapters.es: One identifies the self with the sky, the sun, the oceans, and the fire. This perspective dissolves grief. Two types of ahaṃkāra support freedom: the sense of being a transcendent essence or the entire universe. Identifying with the physical body causes distress. The ātman reflects within the puryaṣṭaka, the eight-fold subtle city, remaining unchanged even if the body perishes. Liberation resides in a mind cleared of attachments and the conceptual categories of bondage and release, not in the heavens or underworld.

chapters.subtitle: How a person achieves peace by controlling the mind.
chapters.es: Vital breath moves the body like wind shaking leaves, yet consciousness remains still and all-pervasive. When discernment dissolves the mirage of ignorance, the mind sheds desire, reaching a state of nairāśya cooler than moonbeams or sandalwood. Like a woman who performs household chores while her heart dwells on a secret lover, the jīvanmukta acts in the world without being bound by it. Free from praise or blame, the individual remains anchored in truth, viewing the three worlds as a blade of grass.

chapters.subtitle: Many beings remain liberated while performing their worldly duties.
chapters.es: Janaka and Māndhātṛ govern kingdoms with inner stillness, while Bali and Prahlāda rule the underworld without attachment. Deities like Agni, Vāyu, and Sūrya perform cosmic functions—consuming offerings, moving through space, and marking time—while remaining free. Śiva holds Gaurī and Viṣṇu engages in worldly preservation, yet both remain unchanged. This jīvanmukti occurs across all realms, from heavens to forests. Such liberation is attained through viveka and a quieted mind, allowing one to act in the world without mental fever.

chapters.subtitle: Using wisdom to cross the ocean of worldly life.
chapters.es: The world arises from brahman and remains stable through ignorance. This existence is a vast ocean where death forms whirlpools and the senses act as predatory crocodiles. Women appear as waves, their smiles like foam and eyes like lotuses. To navigate these waters, one employs the boat of prajñā and the pilot of viveka. By investigating the truth before engaging with life, a person gains strength and intellect, resembling a tree that flourishes during the spring.

chapters.subtitle: The behavior of a person liberated while still alive.
chapters.es: A jīvanmukta observes the world with the detachment of deep sleep, viewing human activity as the motion of wooden puppets. While performing daily tasks, this individual remains internally still, neither dwelling on the past nor craving the future. They act as a child among children and an elder among the aged, yet their mind remains as clear as an autumn sky. Even if the sun grew cold or the moon scorched the earth, their steady awareness of the ātman stays undisturbed by the rise and fall of worldly events.

chapters.subtitle: Ways to stop the breath and still the mind.
chapters.es: The world arises from the movement of the mind, just as a whirling firebrand creates a circle of flame. Mind and breath move together like a flower and its scent. To still the mind, one restrains prāṇa through rhythmic breathing, chanting Om, or focusing on the space between the eyebrows. By turning from the physical heart to the heart of consciousness, saṃvid, the practitioner stops the internal vibration of thought and dissolves the cycle of rebirth.

chapters.subtitle: Defining right knowledge as the unity of all things
chapters.es: Right knowledge rests on the conviction that only the paramātman exists. Common objects like clay jars and cloth are seen as manifestations of the Self. This perception replaces the illusion of a snake in a rope with the reality of the cord. The world unfolds as the vast brahman, where water and its waves are the same substance. One who sees no difference between wood and stone remains steady like a mountain against the wind, while the ignorant are swallowed by grief like fish by a heron.

chapters.subtitle: Finding the inner self by overcoming the mind.
chapters.es: A wise person addresses the mind as a vetāla, a ghost haunting the house of the body. Using the sword of vicāra, they realize the mind has no independent reality and order it to depart. The physical eyes observe forms without disturbing the inner self, just as a donkey falling into a pond does not affect a general. Once the ego and mental agitations vanish, the body becomes a quiet dwelling. The seeker then recognizes their nature as eternal cit and offers salutations to the Self.

chapters.subtitle: A seeker realizes the mind is an illusion.
chapters.es: The seeker recognizes the citta as an illusion, similar to trees that appear to move when viewed from a traveling boat. Once this mental delusion fades, only the brahman remains. The individual discards the ahaṃkāra and rests in the quiet ātman. Whether eating, walking, or sleeping, the wise perform their natural duties without agitation. They move through the world with a clear mind, free from pride and desire, as steady and cool as the autumn moon.

chapters.subtitle: A sage meditates to control his wandering mind.
chapters.es: Vītahavya seeks refuge in a cave within the Vindhya mountains. He sits on a deer skin in padmāsana, drawing his senses inward as the sun pulls in its rays at dusk. He observes his mind leaping like a monkey between objects. By addressing his senses as inert tools, he separates his inner awareness from the physical functions of the body. He likens the mind to a sickle that cannot cut without a hand, realizing only brahman exists while the individual ego dissolves into stillness.

chapters.subtitle: A sage discards his mind through self-inquiry.
chapters.es: A sage commands his senses to relinquish their hold, describing the mind as the root of agony. He likens greed to a bird pecking at the body and worry to a monkey restless in the heart. Through vicāra, the fog of ignorance lifts, revealing a heart as clear as an autumn sky. The individual discards the phantom of the ego to dwell in the turya state. No longer driven by desire, the self rests in a silent, unchanging state like a wave merging into the ocean.

chapters.subtitle: Vītahavya experiences many lives while meditating in a cave.
chapters.es: Vītahavya enters samādhi in a Vindhya cave, his breath subsiding like a fire without fuel. He remains motionless for three hundred years, oblivious to mountain storms, elephant cries, and forest fires. Eventually, monsoon mud buries his body against the stone. Within his mind, he experiences multiple lives as Indra, a vidyādhara, and a servant of Śiva. Upon waking, he perceives the world as manomaya, a projection of consciousness existing within the heart’s internal space.

chapters.subtitle: A sage retrieves his body from the earth.
chapters.es: Vītahavya perceives his former body buried beneath the mud and grass of the Vindhya mountains. To retrieve it, he enters the sun in a breath-like form. At the sun's command, the attendant Piṅgala descends and extracts the body from the earth like a lotus root from a pond. Vītahavya’s subtle puryaṣṭaka reanimates the physical frame. After bathing in a lake and offering prayers to the sun, the sage resumes his life, remaining in the forest in a state of śānti.

chapters.subtitle: Vītahavya releases his physical form to attain final liberation.
chapters.es: Vītahavya enters a Vindhya mountain cave to settle his mind. He sits motionless like a stone peak, dismissing sensory perceptions and thoughts. After years as a jīvanmukta, he seeks final liberation in the Sahya mountains. Sitting in padmāsana, he bids farewell to his body, his cravings, and his walking stick. He returns his sight to the sun and his breath to the wind. Chanting Oṃ, he subsides into himself like a flame that has consumed its fuel.

chapters.subtitle: Vīthahavya reaches the ultimate state through meditation.
chapters.es: Vīthahavya chants the praṇava and moves through its constituent mātrā steps. He sheds thoughts of the three worlds and rests in stillness like a wheel that has stopped spinning. Discarding perceptions of light and darkness, he reaches the awareness of a newborn before passing beyond the division of subject and object. He enters the turya state, which different traditions identify as śūnya, brahman, or puruṣa. Finally, he remains as the unchanging reality, more transparent than the sky and free from all conceptual stirrings.

chapters.subtitle: Sage Vitahavya reaches liberation and his body decays.
chapters.es: Vītahavya reaches the shore of the ocean of suffering and falls into silence. Like a drop of water merging into the sea, he enters a state of rest. His motionless body withers like a lotus at the end of winter. The life breaths leave the nest of his heart like birds taking flight. His physical frame remains on the forest floor while his consciousness dissolves into the cid-arṇava. By ending the mental construction of the world, he discards desire and settles into the eternal pada.

chapters.subtitle: The physical state and powers of a liberated person.
chapters.es: Vasistha explains that physical powers like ākāśagamana arise from specific materials, mantras, or efforts rather than self-knowledge. A liberated person, satisfied in the ātman, often ignores these external abilities. Vitahavya’s body remained untouched by predators because his lack of vāsanā pacified nearby animals. By stilling the internal spanda or vibration of prāṇa and mind, his physical frame became as stable as a stone, resisting decay for millennia until he chose videhamukti.

chapters.subtitle: The two types of mind destruction in liberation
chapters.es: Mind destruction occurs in two stages. In sarūpa-nāśa, the jīvanmukta remains unswayed by pleasure or pain. The mind transforms into sattva, where virtues like maitrī flourish like spring blossoms. Here, the ego vanishes while the physical form persists. In arūpa-nāśa, occurring at videhamukti, even this purity dissolves. No qualities, directions, or light remain. This state resembles a clear, empty sky, entirely free from the seeds of rebirth and the dualities of worldly existence.

chapters.subtitle: The causes of the mind and the world.
chapters.es: The world grows like a vine from the seed of the body, which itself springs from the citta. This mind relies on two sources: the movement of breath, or prāṇaspanda, and latent impressions called vāsanā. As breath flows through the channels, consciousness stirs into thought. Yogis restrain this breath to quiet the mind. These habits and movements arise from the perception of objects. By abandoning these perceptions, the mind dissolves into pure existence, a changeless state that reflects all things like a clear mirror.

chapters.subtitle: Methods for controlling the mind and attaining supreme knowledge.
chapters.es: Achieving the supreme state requires the simultaneous practice of vāsanā-kṣaya, vijñāna, and manonāśa. Like a coordinated army, the destruction of latent tendencies, the attainment of wisdom, and the dissolution of the mind work together to sever the heart's knots. Attempting to suppress the mind through force rather than the yukti of breath control or association with the wise is like binding a wild elephant with lotus fibers. By steadying the prāṇa and resting in pure saṃvit, one performs daily actions while remaining a non-doer.

chapters.subtitle: The qualities of a wise and unattached person.
chapters.es: An inquirer protects the mind through vicāra, causing pure qualities to settle upon him like birds on a lake. He remains unmoved by the sound of lutes or thunder, the taste of poison or milk, and the touch of thorns or silk. Regarding the world as saṃvit, he performs daily duties without saṅga. Just as the milky ocean retains its color during the churning of Mandara, he maintains a steady nature amidst prosperity and disaster, acting without internal distress.

chapters.subtitle: The court listens to teachings and completes evening rituals.
chapters.es: The discourse on liberation begins as the afternoon sun descends. The royal court sits motionless, captivated by the sage’s words until the sounding of drums and conchs marks the end of the session. The teacher instructs Rāma to catch the bird of the mind and perform his āhnika rituals. After the assembly disperses like lotuses, the listeners complete their evening baths and meals. On silken beds, they meditate on the saṃsārottaraṇopāya before the night ends and the moon fades.

chapters.subtitle: Vasistha teaches Rama that the world is Brahman.
chapters.es: At daybreak, Rāma and his brothers visit the hermitage and join an assembly of kings and sages at the palace. Vasiṣṭha instructs Rāma to recognize the world as brahman, inseparable from consciousness like pungency from a peppercorn. He explains that as desires fade, the agitated citta becomes sattva, the quiet state of a jīvanmukta. Rāma is urged to discard the ego and remain settled in his true nature as the infinite and partless cidātmā.

chapters.subtitle: Consciousness and the world are one.
chapters.es: Infinite worlds rise within consciousness like waves upon a deep ocean. Just as sweetness dwells in sugarcane and heat in fire, the perceived universe remains inseparable from the self. This continuity links the jīva, mind, and body into one movement. The knower acts without attachment, viewing enemies as friends and remaining as still as a block of wood during joy or sorrow. When internal vāsanā vanish like a lamp without oil, the individual lives free from the bonds of ego.

chapters.subtitle: The end of desire leads to a stable mind.
chapters.es: The ātman appears as many beings like one moon reflecting in multiple water jars. When the poison of desire fades, ignorance departs like autumn mist. Achieving acittatva breaks the cycle of latent impressions, scattering them like pearls from a snapped thread. The seeker remains stable, free from thought, as wind rests in the open sky. These instructions awaken the mind like drums rouse a king, entering the heart like water soaking into dry soil.

chapters.subtitle: A person finds inner peace and realizes his nature.
chapters.es: Inner stillness arrives like parched earth cooled by rain. Mental constructs and doubts dissolve like night-ghosts at sunrise, leaving the heart clear as an autumnal lake. The universe appears without the mist of duality, revealing the ātman as the only reality. Free from the mirage of desire, the seeker laughs at his former bondage. He rests in his own nature, steady and joyful, having discarded the false distinctions between existence and non-existence after bathing in nectar-like words.

chapters.subtitle: The soul remains separate from the physical body.
chapters.es: The ātman exists as a constant presence, distinct from the deha like light from darkness. Though the body experiences birth, old age, and death, the conscious self remains untouched, resembling a lotus leaf in water or the sky unaffected by wind. For those identifying with the physical form, life becomes a repetitive cycle of suffering and futile action. This ignorance transforms the world into a vast forest of desires, while the wise perceive it merely as a shallow pool.

chapters.subtitle: Human aging and the cycles of time.
chapters.es: The human frame consists of bone and flesh, though often likened to golden jars. Kāla devours ripened worlds like fruit, remaining hungry after consuming entire universes. A cosmic potter turns the wheel of time, producing fleeting bubbles of existence. Deities such as Rudra appear and vanish within a single blink of consciousness. The three worlds are a house where the sun and moon serve as windows. This entire movement of saṃsāra, driven by desire and decay, is the spreading darkness of avidyā.

chapters.subtitle: Ignorance is described as a growing vine.
chapters.es: The creeper of avidyā grows upon the mountain of consciousness within the forest of worldly existence. Its stem is the physical body, and its wide leaves are the three worlds. Days bloom as flowers while nights hover as dark bees. Planets toss like blossoms in the sky, and stars cluster as unexpanded buds. This vine feeds on mental impressions, rooted in the soil of pleasure and pain. Though it shelters gods and serpents alike, the sharp worm of vicāra eventually cuts its core.

chapters.subtitle: How consciousness creates and dissolves the world through ignorance.
chapters.es: A subtle movement arises from pure consciousness like a whirlpool in water. This movement becomes the mind, dividing into the three qualities of sattva, rajas, and tamas. These qualities form avidyā, appearing as gods, sages, and the material world. Just as a bubble emerges from and dissolves back into water, ignorance and knowledge arise from the same source. When these ideas vanish, consciousness remains, acting like a magnet that moves the body without performing any action itself.

chapters.subtitle: Knowledge removes ignorance and reveals the universal essence.
chapters.es: The mind entangles itself in its own vāsanā like a silkworm weaving a cocoon. In trees and rocks, consciousness rests in a dormant state similar to deep sleep, where the seeds of future births remain hidden. This ignorance, or avidyā, dissolves when examined closely, just as darkness vanishes when met with a lamp. By inquiring into the nature of the body, the seeker finds the universal essence of sattāsāmānya. Ultimately, the distinction between the world and the infinite brahman disappears.

chapters.subtitle: All things in the world are one consciousness.
chapters.es: Persistent practice dissolves the growth of avidyā rooted in the heart. Just as waves belong to the ocean and gold forms a bracelet, all objects are essentially brahman. This consciousness exists as heat in fire, cold in ice, and sweetness in sugar. One recognizes the self in the sun, the sky, and even blades of grass. By abandoning the false sense of I, the practitioner sees the entire universe as a single, unchanging reality, remaining peaceful through every experience.

chapters.subtitle: The wise perform worldly duties with a steady mind.
chapters.es: Liberated beings move through the world without attachment to life or death. They rule kingdoms under ceremonial umbrellas, wander through Nandana gardens, and follow the duties of śruti and smṛti. They walk across battlefields strewn with fallen elephants and face the loss of wealth without sorrow. Their minds remain steady like Mount Meru while they view all existence as cinmātram. Rāma declares his confusion has vanished like autumn mist and resolves to act according to these instructions.

chapters.subtitle: Two methods for reaching liberation through knowledge and breathing.
chapters.es: Two methods exist for crossing saṃsāra: self-knowledge and the control of breath. Self-knowledge removes latent mental tendencies through awareness, whereas breath control, or prāṇasaṃrodha, relies on physical postures and quiet settings. Knowledge serves as the simpler route because it persists across all states of being. However, practitioners of breath control achieve stillness by stopping the movement of air through the nose and mouth. This restraint of the vital airs halts the mind’s fluctuations and leads to a silent state of liberation.

chapters.subtitle: Vasiṣṭha visits a long-lived crow on Mount Meru.
chapters.es: Vasiṣṭha hears the sage Śātātapa describe a long-lived crow named Bhuśuṇḍa who dwells on Mount Meru. Seeking the bird, Vasiṣṭha travels to a ruby-colored peak that glows like fire against the sky. This summit reaches toward the stars and houses a kalpataru tree entwined with gold vines. Amidst singing gandharva and flowering forests, Bhuśuṇḍa lives in a hollow branch. Vasiṣṭha arrives at this glowing peak to witness the bird who has survived many ages of the world.

chapters.subtitle: An ancient crow lives inside a large celestial tree.
chapters.es: A celestial tree extends into the sky, its branches laden with gems and swathed in clouds. Within its canopy, swans chant the Sāmaveda while peacocks learned in Śaiva wisdom rest near apsarās. In a sheltered hollow sits Bhusuṇḍa, an ancient crow who has outlived several world cycles. He observes the rise and fall of deities like Indra and Śiva. By steadying his breath through prāṇa-spanda, he remains peaceful, his memory preserving the history of many past eons.

chapters.subtitle: Vasiṣṭha meets an ancient crow named Bhuśuṇḍa.
chapters.es: Vasiṣṭha descends upon the mountain peak like a falling star, startling the gathered crows. Bhuśuṇḍa rises from a heap of leaves to offer a greeting of flowers and a seat of fresh kalpataru foliage. After providing water for hospitality, the crow inquires about the sage’s journey and his internal state of samatā. Vasiṣṭha expresses his desire to learn about the bird’s longevity and origins. Bhuśuṇḍa prepares to recount his life story to the visiting sage.

chapters.subtitle: The sage Bhusuṇḍa describes his peaceful state of mind.
chapters.es: Bhusuṇḍa stands steady like Mount Mandara at rest, his dark form resembling a monsoon cloud. He views the three worlds as a bilva fruit held in his palm and regards worldly existence as light as straw. With a deep voice and a smile, he prepares to recount his nature and his understanding of the cycles of birth and death. His mind remains full like the ocean of milk, untouched by fear or agitation, as he begins his nectar-like speech.

chapters.subtitle: Goddesses abduct Umā and hold a wild festival.
chapters.es: Hara resides in the cremation ground, adorned with bhasma, snakes, and a crescent moon. Animal-headed pramatha attendants surround him while the mātaraḥ goddesses, including the crow-riding Alambusā, gather in the sky. After worshiping Tumburu and Bhairava, these goddesses decide to prove their power. They use magic to abduct Umā from Hara’s side. The group celebrates with a rowdy festival of wine, meat, and dancing that fills the heavens with noise.

chapters.subtitle: Twenty-one crows are born and move to Mount Meru.
chapters.es: During a divine festival, the haṃsī mounts of Brāhmī mate with the crow Ālambusa. These geese lay twenty-one eggs within the navel-lotus of the creator. From these eggs emerge crows who receive the goddess’s grace and attain a quiet mind. Seeking solitude, the brothers visit their father before flying to Mount Meru. They settle in a jeweled nest on a kalpataru tree, where they remain in silent meditation, overlooking the worlds from their high perch.

chapters.subtitle: A long-lived crow explains his survival and inner peace.
chapters.es: Bhuśuṇḍa offers arghya and pādya to his guest under an ancient tree. He explains how his brothers perished across vast yuga cycles while he remained steady. He tracks passing ages by observing the flow of his own prāṇa and apāna breaths. Amidst cosmic storms and burning suns, the crow stays in his nest, viewing the world’s changes as harmless waves. He remains content and finds the fruit of his long life in meeting a wise visitor.

chapters.subtitle: An ancient bird recounts surviving many world cycles.
chapters.es: The ancient crow Bhuśuṇḍa inhabits a kalpavṛkṣa that remains unmoved during cosmic dissolution. When fire and wind consume the world at kalpānta, he leaves his nest to hover in the empty sky. By practicing specific dhāraṇā methods, he survives elemental forces and enters a state of suṣupti until the next creation begins. He remembers past ages where the earth was buried in ash, epochs without a sun, and the recurring cycles of the samudramanthana and various divine incarnations.

chapters.subtitle: Recurring world cycles and historical events.
chapters.es: An observer recounts witnessing the repetitive cycles of creation. He remembers Vasiṣṭha appearing in eight different births from the elements. He recalls twelve churnings of the ocean and five instances of the earth being lifted from the sea. Across many ages, poets compose the Rāmāyaṇa twelve times and the Bhārata seven. Viṣṇu takes various forms, appearing as Buddha, Rāma, and Kṛṣṇa. Mountains shift and world-ages change their sequence while the witness remains in his nest, watching the universe arise and dissolve like bubbles.

chapters.subtitle: Bhushunda explains how a steady mind avoids death.
chapters.es: Bhushunda explains that death avoids those whose hearts are not bound by the threads of vāsanā. He describes the body as a tree hollowed by the worms of mental pain or scorched by the fire of anger. A steady mind stays unmoved by greed, unlike a monkey leaping between branches. Recognizing that nothing in the heavens, earth, or netherworld is permanent, the wise man rests his attention solely in the unchanging state of pure consciousness.

chapters.subtitle: The physical body and the flow of vital breath.
chapters.es: The body is a house supported by three pillars and nine gates. The ego resides here as a householder, served by the senses as doorkeepers. Within this frame, three lotus-like organs regulate the vital winds, prāṇa and apāna. These winds circulate through the iḍā and piṅgalā channels, powering digestion, speech, and movement. By following the continuous flow of these breaths through waking, dreaming, and deep sleep, a practitioner detaches from worldly bonds and halts the cycle of rebirth.

chapters.subtitle: How the movement of breath leads to inner stillness.
chapters.es: Prāṇa and apāna flow continuously through the body, active even during sleep. Prāṇa rises from the heart lotus, heating the limbs like a sun, while apāna descends like a cooling moon. Practitioners observe the transitions between inhalation, exhalation, and retention. In the still moments between the outgoing recaka and the incoming pūraka, the breath pauses in kumbhaka. By focusing on the gaps where breaths emerge and dissolve, the mind rests in the underlying cit that powers every physical movement.

chapters.subtitle: Bhusuṇḍa describes his state of equanimity and longevity.
chapters.es: Bhusuṇḍa maintains an unchanging state by balancing the internal movements of prāṇa and apāna. He observes the birth and dissolution of beings without dwelling on the past or future. Whether walking, standing, or dreaming, he views objects like wood, fire, and sky with equal composure. He discards the notion of ownership over his body and recognizes all existence as cit. By abandoning the desire for results and remaining a friend to all, he endures through ages, watching the world like a wave in the cosmic ocean.

chapters.subtitle: Vasiṣṭha departs and meets the bird in later ages.
chapters.es: Vasiṣṭha prepares for departure at midday after hearing the bird's history. Bhusuṇḍa honors the sage with an arghya of pearls and blossoms gathered from the kalpavṛkṣa. When Vasiṣṭha rises toward the saptarṣi constellation to join his wife, the crow follows him for a yojana until the sage takes his hand and stops him. Through the transitions from the kṛtayuga to the tretāyuga, Vasiṣṭha returns to the mountain summit, finding the bird still living there, physically unchanged.

chapters.subtitle: The mind creates the body and the world.
chapters.es: The physical body, built of skin and bone, exists as a mental projection rather than a permanent creation. Like the optical illusion of two moons or a city in the clouds, the world takes shape through the force of saṃkalpa. Attachment and aversion, or rāga and dveṣa, sustain this long dream. By observing the movement of breath and discarding the notion of a separate self, the mind achieves a natural coolness. This clarity reveals the ātman as an undivided, constant presence.

chapters.subtitle: Controlling the mind and understanding the nature of god.
chapters.es: The mind serves as the central hub of the saṃsāracakra, driving the cycle of rebirth. While a physical body decays faster than a painted figure, the individual remains haunted by the ego-ghost inhabiting this empty dwelling. On Mount Kailāsa, Śiva instructs that the supreme deity is neither a person nor an element, but infinite consciousness. True worship discards external rituals for internal offerings of sāmya, bodha, and śama. Like a dream city, the entire universe exists only as the space of consciousness.

chapters.subtitle: How pure consciousness becomes an individual and experiences suffering.
chapters.es: Pure consciousness, or cit, animates all existence like sap within a vine or light in a lamp. It resides within the body, breathing and eating, yet remains distinct from external rituals using incense. Through saṃkalpa, this singular reality identifies as an individual jīva, assuming the limitations of mind and ego. It wanders through various births—as deities, animals, or plants—like a straw tossed in a whirlpool, experiencing the cycle of worldly suffering until it remembers its original, undivided nature.

chapters.subtitle: How consciousness moves the mind and body
chapters.es: Pure consciousness acts as an unchanging light, like a lamp illuminating a room without participating in its activity. It dwells equally in pots, trees, and animals. Through a sequence of instruments described as chariots, consciousness connects to the physical world: the soul carries the intellect, the intellect moves the mind, and the vital breath, or prāṇa, drives the body. Because mind and prāṇa are linked, the flickering of breath causes the mind to wander, just as wind stirs dust. When movement ceases, the underlying cit remains.

chapters.subtitle: How the soul animates the body and then departs
chapters.es: Pure consciousness forgets its nature and functions as the mind, driving the body like a chariot drawn by the horses of prāṇa. This jīva animates limbs just as a magnet moves iron. It resides within the heart lotus as the eightfold puryaṣṭaka. When the vibration of the heart ceases, the life force settles and the subtle body departs. The physical frame falls like a log, while the soul enters the ātivāhika state, wandering through new visions driven by its latent vāsanā.

chapters.subtitle: Thoughts create the world and ending them brings peace.
chapters.es: Reality appears as a duality, much like water forming waves or a vine producing flowers. Consciousness takes on the form of an individual soul, imagining a physical body through persistent thought, or saṃkalpa. This mental construction functions like a ghost born from a dreamer’s fear or a city appearing in the clouds. Suffering arises from these willful imaginings, but it dissolves when the mind stops projecting. By abandoning these mental resolutions, the self moves beyond the cycle of birth and recovers its original, unified state.

chapters.subtitle: Three stages of reaching the highest consciousness.
chapters.es: Pure cit binds itself by identifying with limited forms. When the mind dissolves like autumn clouds, three stages of stillness emerge. First, consciousness rests in its own nature, free from the movement of cetya. Second, it remains a witness, as stable as the interior of a stone or salt within water. Finally, it transcends even names like brahman or ātman. The world appears as a silent, undivided whole. Having explained these paths, the speaker and listener rest together in silence.

chapters.subtitle: Shiva identifies pure consciousness as the only true God
chapters.es: Shiva likens the body to a machine driven by the prāṇa wind. When this breath departs, the frame remains motionless like a stone. Pure consciousness, or cit, reflects within the mind like an image in a mirror. From this singular source, deities like Brahmā and Viṣṇu emerge like sparks from heated iron. This mahādeva requires no ritual invocation, as he resides perpetually within all things. Recognizing this internal reality halts the cycle of rebirth, leaving the soul like a parched seed that cannot sprout.

chapters.subtitle: How consciousness contains the entire universe within itself
chapters.es: Pure cit functions as the root of all seeds and the primary source of action. It appears as both the vast sky and a solid wall, holding the mass of mahāmeru within a single atom. A momentary nimeṣa contains an entire kalpa without losing its brevity. Though smaller than a hair’s tip, it wraps around the seven oceans. This sarvātman transforms into nonsensical syllables and logical truths alike, existing as a body without form and an actor who never moves.

chapters.subtitle: How divine consciousness and destiny govern the universe.
chapters.es: A seed draws moisture to sprout while consciousness flows as sap in blossoms and vibrates as wind against the skin. This cit establishes the order of niyati, governing the movement of stars and the passage of kāla. Various powers, including jñāna-śakti and kriyā-śakti, perform a cosmic dance within the theater of the universe. The supreme witness observes this play of time and space, remaining inseparable from the movement while providing the light by which the world functions.

chapters.subtitle: How to worship the supreme self through meditation.
chapters.es: The deity cinmātra resides in every object, from clay pots to celestial beings. This cosmic form encompasses the infinite sky as its neck and the cardinal directions as arms, while time stands as its gatekeeper. True worship rejects external offerings like incense and flowers, favoring the internal nectar of bodha. By maintaining awareness during physical acts like breathing or walking, the practitioner transforms daily life into a ritual. This mental dedication provides the fruits of ancient sacrifices, leading to a state of liberation.

chapters.subtitle: Daily life acts as ritual worship of the self
chapters.es: Inner worship involves identifying the ātman within the body as the inner śiva. This ritual continues during every physical state, whether walking, standing, waking, or sleeping. The mind acts as a doorkeeper and the senses function as attendants. Instead of external incense, the person offers up every life event that occurs naturally, including food, sensory pleasure, poverty, and illness. Maintaining samatā, or constant equanimity, transforms every worldly movement and sensory experience into a continuous act of devotion.

chapters.subtitle: Worshiping the self through everyday experiences and worldly events
chapters.es: Consciousness recognizes itself within every action and experience. Feelings of desire and hatred emerge from the ātman like sparks from a fire. Instead of external rituals, the mind treats worldly events—poverty, royalty, pleasure, or pain—as natural offerings or upahāra. This perspective dissolves the distinction between the worshiper and the deity. One remains like a clear crystal, where the stains of birth and sorrow find no purchase. Every perception of a jar or a body becomes an act of devotion to śiva.

chapters.subtitle: The soul uses knowledge to destroy ignorance and illusion.
chapters.es: A seeker uses scriptures and a teacher’s guidance to scrub away ignorance, much like a washerman uses alkaline earth to clean a stained garment. While labels such as śiva or brahman differ, they point to a singular reality beyond the senses. Pure consciousness assumes the form of an individual soul and constructs the subtle ātivāhika-deha. This world resembles a mirage or a dream city; it dissolves when mental tendencies cease, revealing the self through its own quiet light.

chapters.subtitle: Shiva explains creation and departs with Parvati.
chapters.es: The first living being, or jīva, perceives a body arising from the infinite space like a person in a dream. This initial being takes the form of sadāśiva, viṣṇu, or pitāmaha through mental resolve. Entire cycles of time, from a nimeṣa to a mahākalpa, unfold within every atom. All entities, from gods to blades of grass, emerge as mental constructs without altering the underlying brahman. Having finished his teaching, the deity departs into the sky with his consort and retinue.

chapters.subtitle: Vasiṣṭha explains self-worship and Rāma reaches steady awareness.
chapters.es: Vasiṣṭha explains that creation belongs to the ātman just as fluidity belongs to water or movement to wind. He performs ātman worship through daily actions, maintaining steady awareness while interacting with the world. He instructs Rāma to view life as waves upon an ocean of cinmātra, where joy and grief find no footing. Rāma declares his doubts resolved. He stands steady like Mount Sumeru, no longer seeking heaven or fearing hell, resting in the turyā state beyond desire and fear.

chapters.subtitle: Stopping the mind and breath to reach reality.
chapters.es: Relinquishing desire and the ego leads to a state of equanimity. When the mind moves, the world appears like ripples on water or dust in the air. By stilling the prāṇa and erasing vāsanā, the seeker halts the vibration of consciousness. The mind of the wise transforms from citta into sattva, much like copper turning into gold. Actions performed without attachment leave no trace. This cessation of mental movement reveals the single reality of brahman.

chapters.subtitle: A giant fruit represents the origin of the universe.
chapters.es: A massive bilva fruit exists beyond the reach of time and cosmic winds. It lacks roots, skin, and seeds, consisting entirely of a dense, sweet pulp. This pulp is saṃvid, the essence of consciousness. From its own self-delight, it projects the concepts of "I" and "you," space, and time. It manifests the brahmāṇḍa as a series of constructions, including mountains, stars, and living beings. Despite these diverse appearances, the fruit remains a single, unchanging mass of peaceful awareness.

chapters.subtitle: The universe exists within consciousness like carvings in stone.
chapters.es: A massive stone contains lotuses, conchs, and carvings within its dense, solid mass. These forms face different directions and entwine, yet they remain inseparable from the rock. This cicchila, or consciousness-stone, holds the earth, sky, and mountains without any gaps. Just as a śālabhañjikā carving resides within a stone pillar, the world exists within the density of brahman. The universe does not arise or perish but stays fixed like patterns inside a rock, resting in the stillness of suṣupta.

chapters.subtitle: The universe and consciousness are one and the same.
chapters.es: Consciousness holds the universe just as a solid stone contains uncarved shapes within its core. This reality appears diverse yet remains a single substance, similar to how the fluid inside a peacock’s egg, the mayūrāṇḍa-rasa, contains the colors of every feather. The world resides in Brahman like reflections in a clear mirror or heat in the sun. Every object, from a blade of grass to a mountain, exists as an inseparable ripple of this singular cit, appearing manifold only to the unreflective eye.

chapters.subtitle: Sages remain still while acting in the world.
chapters.es: Sages and deities rest in the turyapada state, watching the world with unblinking eyes as their mind and prāṇa cease moving like figures in a painting. Just as a peacock resides potentially within its seed, the entire universe exists within pure consciousness. Though bodies break like clay jars, the inner ātmakāśa remains whole. One acts in the world without ego, recognizing that all movements are brahman. This realization ends the cycle of rebirth, as the self remains still during daily tasks.

chapters.subtitle: A teacher explains why the soul never truly changes.
chapters.es: Unlike milk curdling into an irreversible state, brahman remains unchanged through the beginning, middle, and end of all things. The apparent shift in the world is a mere tremor of consciousness. Concepts like avidyā serve only as temporary tools to guide the unawakened, much as a teacher uses logic to lead a student toward the truth. Prakṛti and ātman exist as a single reality, inseparable as wind and its movement. Once knowledge burns the seeds of mental construction, the cycle of suffering ceases to sprout.

chapters.subtitle: How the subtle body and senses perceive the world.
chapters.es: Consciousness moves toward ideation and becomes the jīva or the puryaṣṭaka, an eight-fold subtle body. This entity perceives objects by projecting its power through the eyes and ears. External forms reflect in the pupils and are grasped by the inner mind. These perceptions are internal images driven by latent vāsanā. While the physical body remains at death, the senses fail because the jīva no longer operates them. The jīva wanders like a wave until it recognizes its nature as the all-pervading self.

chapters.subtitle: How the mind creates the illusion of the world
chapters.es: The puryaṣṭaka emerges as a subtle body comprising the mind, intellect, ego, and five elements. This jīva projects its surroundings through its own thoughts, much like a child imagining a ghost in the dark. These internal movements of consciousness solidify into the external world, mirroring the mechanics of a long dream or a mirage. Latent desires, or vāsanā, act as the primary bond. When these impressions dissolve through inquiry, the individual moves from the density of matter toward the clarity of mukti.

chapters.subtitle: Viṣṇu incarnates as Kṛṣṇa and Arjuna to help Earth.
chapters.es: The universe exists as a persistent dream where individual souls mistake illusions for reality. When Yama pauses his duties to perform penance, the earth groans under the weight of an immortal, overpopulated humanity. To relieve this burden, Viṣṇu prepares to descend in the twin forms of Kṛṣṇa and Arjuna. Before eighteen armies of the Pāṇḍavas and Kauravas, Arjuna falters in grief. Kṛṣṇa restores his resolve by teaching that the ātman remains eternal, unborn, and untouched by the destruction of the physical body.

chapters.subtitle: Arjuna learns about selfless action and the eternal self.
chapters.es: Arjuna receives instruction to discard the sense of agency and perform his kṣatra duty. The ātman exists within all bodies like a thread through muktāphala beads or butter in milk. While the unawakened worship a deity holding a cakra and gadā, the wise recognize one consciousness pervading every form, like gold in ornaments or water in waves. By offering actions to brahman, the individual remains a witness, like a mirror that reflects objects without being altered by their destruction.

chapters.subtitle: Doing your duty while staying calm and unattached.
chapters.es: Sensory contacts like heat and cold come and go, yet the ātman remains untouched by bodily decay. One endures these changes by seeing that the unreal has no being. Like the ocean remaining still while rivers pour into it, the steady mind absorbs desires without losing its peace. One performs every action—eating, giving, or working—as an offering to brahman. By dropping the ego of the doer and ignoring the fruits of labor, the individual engages with the world while resting in total stillness.

chapters.subtitle: Understanding the eternal soul and the end of rebirth.
chapters.es: The eternal ātman remains untouched when the physical body perishes. Identification with the body creates the illusion of loss, yet the jīva simply moves between forms like a bird leaving a cage. Latent impressions, known as vāsanā, drive this movement, carrying sensory habits like the wind carries a floral scent. Through puruṣārtha, or individual effort, a person dissolves these impressions. Liberation occurs when the mind discards the sense of agency, leaving the soul in its natural, changeless state.

chapters.subtitle: The mind creates the world like a wall-less painting.
chapters.es: A jīvanmukta performs duties that arrive in the stream of time, remaining as cool as a person in deep sleep. The mind acts as a painter, sketching the three worlds upon the void without a supporting wall. This abhitti-citra portrays the universe as a celestial woman with mountains for limbs and the sun and moon for eyes. Though the mind projects the solidity of a diamond, the image remains empty. By withdrawing the senses, the individual finds stillness in the underlying ākāśa.

chapters.subtitle: Knowledge burns latent desires to free the mind.
chapters.es: The world appears like a painting that precedes its own wall. This cosmos emerges from and dissolves into empty space. Latent impressions, or vāsanā, bind the self like a rope. Just as a reflection stays inseparable from a mirror, the universe remains within brahman. When the fire of knowledge burns the seeds of these impressions, they no longer grow into a forest of rebirth. The liberated person then remains untouched by pleasure or pain, like a padmapatra or lotus leaf in water.

chapters.subtitle: Arjuna regains his clarity and prepares for battle.
chapters.es: Arjuna declares his delusion gone and his memory of the self restored. As mental fluctuations subside, his mind enters the state of sattva. Just as snow melts on a burning mountain, worldly desires and ignorance disappear upon realizing pure consciousness. Arjuna rejects the ego as a remedy against the poison of the senses. Now free from doubt, he takes up his gāṇḍīva bow and begins the battle, filling the earth with rivers of blood and the sky with the dust of combat.

chapters.subtitle: Realizing how the movement of consciousness creates the world.
chapters.es: The ātman exists within all beings like oil in seeds or sap in trees. When consciousness vibrates as cit-spanda, the world takes form; when this movement stills, the cycle of saṃsāra ends. A steady mind acts as a mirror, reflecting external events without absorbing their impressions. By abandoning the ego and sensory desires, the individual views the universe as a long dream. This realization dissolves the names of bondage and liberation, revealing an unchanging stillness beneath the constant flux of mental constructs.

chapters.subtitle: Everything in the world contains the same universal consciousness.
chapters.es: Universal existence, sattāsāmānya, resides within every form, including deities like Viṣṇu and Hara. It functions as the sound in hearing, the taste on the tongue, and the light in the eye. This essence manifests as the growth in trees, the stillness of stones, and the movement of creatures. Like water forming waves, the cit or consciousness assumes the appearance of diverse objects from childhood to death. As the sun sets, the assembly departs for evening rituals.

chapters.subtitle: Creation viewed as a persistent dream.
chapters.es: The world appears solid even though creation is a dream. Consciousness projects the sarga in a single nimeṣa, yet the ahaṃtā makes this blink feel like a long era. Anomalies such as fire burning in the ocean and stones growing lotuses show that nature's laws are merely habits of a dream. Like a deer falling through successive pits, the jīva wanders from one delusion to another, mistaking this constant shifting for a stable and permanent reality.

chapters.subtitle: A monk experiences a chain of different lives.
chapters.es: A meditating bhikṣu imagines himself as a man named Jīvaṭa. Through a sequence of dreams, this identity transforms into a brāhmaṇa, a king, and a celestial woman. The soul continues its journey as a deer, a creeping vine, and a bee. After an elephant crushes the bee, the entity becomes an elephant, returns to a bee, and finally becomes a swan. By associating with other swans and gaining viveka, the mind settles into videhamukti, dissolving the cycle of vāsanā.

chapters.subtitle: Rudra awakens his past lives and they merge together.
chapters.es: A bird transforms into Rudra upon realizing its identity within the city of Rudrapura. This Rudra recalls a sequence of past lives as a monk, the soldier Jīvaṭa, a Brahmin, and a king. He travels through the cidākāśa to find these former selves, awakening their dormant bodies from their respective worlds. These awakened figures merge into a collective of a hundred Rudras. They share a single saṃvid, viewing their previous incarnations as mere ripples within the infinite mind.

chapters.subtitle: Individuals return home before joining Rudra's retinue.
chapters.es: Jīvaṭa and the other figures born from the bhikṣu’s dream encounter Rudra, who instructs them to return to their dwellings. They reunite with their wives to exhaust remaining worldly desires. This expansion of a single saṃkalpa into many forms follows the way Viṣṇu appears simultaneously in different incarnations. After finishing their earthly lives, these individuals travel to the city of Rudra to serve as his gaṇas. They dwell among jeweled groves, unified after wandering through separate worlds.

chapters.subtitle: The sage promises to investigate a monk's existence.
chapters.es: A jīva wanders through successive dreams, falling from the heights of consciousness like a stone tumbling down a mountain. This soul moves between delusions of birth and death until it sheds the sense of "I" in the body. After hearing of a wandering monk caught in such dreams, the prince asks if this man exists in the physical world. The sage promises to seek the answer through samādhi. As the midday drum sounds, the court disperses to perform daily duties and evening rituals.

chapters.subtitle: A monk experiences many world cycles through meditation.
chapters.es: Vasistha searches the seven islands through his internal vision to find the meditating monk. In the northern land of Jina, he locates a monk named Dīrghadṛś sitting in a locked hut atop an anthill. Though twenty-one days pass in the physical world, the monk experiences thousands of years within his mind. This cycle repeats across infinite world-cycles, where figures like Vyāsa and Nārada reappear like waves on water. All existence arises from the cidākāśa as mental perceptions, appearing like sparks flying from a fire.

chapters.subtitle: A monk leaves his body and finds liberation.
chapters.es: The king’s servants find the mendicant’s body decaying in a hut and cast the lifeless frame into the water. Having attained the state of jīvanmukta, the mendicant exists beyond physical death. This world arises from the movement of the mind, just as gold takes the form of a bracelet. When mental vibration, or spanda, ceases, the distinction between dreamer and dream dissolves. Only the stillness of brahman remains, an undivided reality where the concepts of bondage and liberation no longer hold meaning.

chapters.subtitle: Four kinds of silence and the path to liberation.
chapters.es: Four forms of silence define the ascetic path. Vāṅmauna halts speech, while akṣamauna restrains the senses. Physical stillness resembling a log is termed kāṣṭhamauna. Unlike these forced restraints, suṣuptamauna characterizes the liberated person. This deep-sleep silence involves no suppression of breath or sensory awareness. Instead, the mind recognizes the world as a unified expression of saṃvid. One engages in worldly actions without ego-driven concepts of self or other, remaining permanently anchored in the tranquil state of turya.

chapters.subtitle: Breath and mind control lead to spiritual freedom.
chapters.es: The mind and prāṇa function together like a chariot and its driver. When the movement of breath stops, the mind becomes still, and when the mind settles, the breath follows. Liberated beings perform actions spontaneously, like a crow alighting on a palm tree, without being bound by social rules or personal desire. Through sāṃkhya inquiry or yoga practice, a seeker dissolves mental impressions called vāsanā. This transformation turns the mind into sattva, much like copper becoming gold, ending the cycle of rebirth.

chapters.subtitle: A ghost poses riddles to a king at night
chapters.es: A giant vetāla from the Vindhya forest enters a city and stops a king patrolling at night. Driven by hunger but restrained by justice, the creature demands the king answer several riddles. These questions describe a sun whose rays are universes and an atom containing the three worlds. They explore how the jīva moves through dreams without losing its radiant nature. If the king fails to explain how a single atom encompasses the cosmos, the vetāla threatens to devour him.

chapters.subtitle: A king compares the universe to motes in sunlight.
chapters.es: Answering a vetāla, a king portrays the universe as a fruit on a branch, growing into forests, mountains, and islands. These landscapes form the body of a cosmic man, whose chest holds countless worlds. Thousands of such beings appear as mere hairs on a great sun. This sun is citsūrya, the light of consciousness. All worlds are motes of dust floating in its radiance, existing only through pure awareness.

chapters.subtitle: The universe exists as layers of pure consciousness.
chapters.es: Pure consciousness vibrates like fragrance within a flower or dust in a strong wind. Just as the trunk of a banana tree, the rambhāstambha, consists entirely of internal layers, the universe contains only brahman at its core. This reality is subtler than an atom yet provides the foundation for Mount Meru. The three worlds exist as the marrow of awareness, appearing solid despite their dreamlike nature. Like a child’s imaginary ghost, or vetālabālaka, the world lacks independent form, leading to a state of stillness.

chapters.subtitle: A demon finds peace and Bhagīratha succeeds.
chapters.es: The vetāla loses his hunger and enters deep meditation after hearing the king’s wisdom. This world resides within the cidaṇu, a minute point of consciousness, and vanishes through inquiry. One clears the mind until it resembles the empty sky, remaining steady and indifferent to desire. With this internal peace and samadarśana, or equal vision, even the most difficult goals are attained. Such steady focus allowed Bhagīratha to bring the Gaṅgā river down to the earth.

chapters.subtitle: King Bhagīratha renounces his kingdom for spiritual knowledge.
chapters.es: King Bhagīratha rules a prosperous realm but finds the daily repetition of eating, sleeping, and working hollow. He questions the value of worldly success and seeks an end to the cycle of birth and death. The sage Tritala teaches him that all suffering stems from ahaṃkāra, the ego. To dissolve this internal knot, the king must renounce his sovereignty and possessions. He is advised to live as a beggar, stripping away social status to realize the underlying cinmātra, or pure consciousness.

chapters.subtitle: King Bhagiratha renounces his wealth and kingdom for peace.
chapters.es: Bhagiratha performs the agniṣṭoma sacrifice and distributes his horses, gold, and land among his kin and the brāhmaṇa community. Wearing only a loincloth, he leaves his kingdom to an enemy and lives in total anonymity. Returning later to beg for food, he refuses to reclaim his throne, treating power as dry grass. He eventually joins his teacher, Tritala. Together, they wander through forests and cities in a state of samatā, indifferent to physical pleasure or supernatural powers while their bodies follow their natural course.

chapters.subtitle: Bhagīratha becomes king and brings the Ganges to earth.
chapters.es: Bhagīratha leaves his life as a wandering mendicant when citizens seeking a leader crown him their king. He remains calm and detached while governing multiple realms. Upon learning that his ancestors in Pātāla require celestial water for liberation, he entrusts his kingdom to ministers and retreats to the forest. Through millennia of tapas, he pleases Brahmā and Śiva. He successfully brings the Gaṅgā down from the sky to the earth, where it flows as the Trimārgagā to purify his lineage.

chapters.subtitle: King Śikhidhvaja and Queen Cūḍālā marry and live together.
chapters.es: King Śikhidhvaja rules the Malwa territory near the Vindhya mountains. After a youthful conquest of the earth, he governs with justice. During spring, he seeks a companion and marries Princess Cūḍālā of Saurashtra. The couple resides in forest groves and lotus ponds, sharing their knowledge of the śāstras and fine arts. Their lives blend together like fragrance and flower. They study music, dance, and philosophy, reaching a state of mutual understanding as they manage the kingdom's affairs together.

chapters.subtitle: Chudala discovers her true self through internal inquiry.
chapters.es: As youth fades like water leaking from a broken jar, a royal couple seeks a remedy for the cycle of saṃsāra. The queen examines her body, senses, and mind, finding them as hollow as a corpse. She traces the source of life beyond the ego to the pure consciousness of mahā-cit. Perceiving the universe as an undivided expression of this single reality, she discards all fear and remains as still and clear as the autumn sky.

chapters.subtitle: A queen explains her spiritual radiance to her husband.
chapters.es: Queen Cūḍālā attains a state of internal stillness, abandoning desires and the dualities of pleasure and pain. Her physical form acquires a clarity compared to clear clouds and blossoming vines. King Śikhidhvaja notices her transformation and asks if she has found a hidden kingdom or drank nectar. She explains her radiance as the result of resting in the ātman. By viewing the world as both everything and nothing, she remains indifferent to external status, finding contentment within her own heart's vastness.

chapters.subtitle: Cūḍālā learns to fly using breath control.
chapters.es: King Śikhidhvaja dismisses Cūḍālā’s spiritual insights as incoherent babble and departs for his bath. Undeterred, Cūḍālā seeks a secluded place to master flight through prāṇāyāma. She concentrates on the āntraveṣṭanikā channel where the kuṇḍalinī resides like a coiled serpent. By directing prāṇa into this central power, she awakens internal consciousness. The five elements and latent vāsana combine to form the body and the world, appearing like shifting waves that rise and dissolve within the ocean of existence.

chapters.subtitle: How mental distress causes physical illness through irregular breath.
chapters.es: The kuṇḍalinī power resides within the body as life-force, moving through breaths like prāṇa and apāna. When mental distress or cravings agitate the mind, the breath becomes irregular, obstructing the channels and causing food to turn into disease. Yogis restore balance through controlled inhalation and exhalation, which purifies the mind and stabilizes the internal fire and cooling moon essence. By focusing on the junction where these opposing forces meet, one removes sickness and gains steady awareness of the self.

chapters.subtitle: Ways to transform the body and reach pure consciousness.
chapters.es: A spark of fire rises from the heart-lotus, dissolving the physical limbs like melting gold. The kuṇḍalinī energy ascends as a trail of smoke, carrying the ego into new shapes. Through firm visualization, consciousness flows into objects like water, becoming a mountain or a blade of grass. While breath control allows the life-force to inhabit different bodies, the path of knowledge reveals the physical form as a mental illusion, returning the individual to the state of pure cinmātra.

chapters.subtitle: Cūḍālā gains powers and a hunter finds a gem
chapters.es: Cūḍālā masters the siddhi of flight, moving through solid objects like a thread through a pearl. She attempts to instruct King Śikhidhvaja in ātmajñāna, but he dismisses her as a young wife. Vasiṣṭha compares a teacher’s words to a man searching for a lost varāṭikā shell in a forest. While looking for the worthless coin, the man happens upon a cintāmaṇi gem. In the same way, the effort spent on external study unexpectedly brings the seeker to the realization of brahman.

chapters.subtitle: Śikhidhvaja renounces his kingdom and becomes a forest ascetic.
chapters.es: Restless despite his wealth, Śikhidhvaja abandons his palace to seek peace in the wilderness. He rejects the pleas of his wife Cūḍālā and departs at midnight while she sleeps. After twelve nights of travel, he reaches the slopes of Mount Mandara. He builds a leaf hut and settles into a life of tapas. Using a kamaṇḍalu, akṣamālā, and mṛgājina, he performs daily prayers and gathers forest fruits. The king replaces royal luxury with a strict routine of japa and quiet solitude.

chapters.subtitle: Cūḍālā visits her husband in the forest disguised as a boy.
