The self is cidākāśa, a space of consciousness that cannot be burned or cut. Bodies perish, but this awareness remains constant across generations. Identifying with the physical frame brings fear, yet recognizing oneself as the infinite sky turns even the fire of a collapsing world into a shower of blossoms.

chapters.subtitle: People have different beliefs, but true wisdom is rare.
chapters.es: Reality follows one’s firm conviction, appearing as atoms, a void, or an illusion. While most beings, from yakṣas to ants, drift like straw in a whirlpool, a rare few live as a jīvanmukta. These individuals, including prahlāda and vibhīṣaṇa, remain undisturbed by the currents of worldly existence. Those possessing viveka are as scarce as the kalpadruma tree, standing apart from the common biological growth that covers the world.

chapters.subtitle: The traits and value of virtuous people
chapters.es: Wise individuals remain steady, neither yielding to anger nor excessive joy. They speak with gentleness and welcome seekers like lotuses receiving bees. While they maintain a calm interior like rain clouds, their outward actions remain simple and disciplined. These figures stand as firm mountains against the agitation of the mind. To cross the ocean of saṃsāra, a traveler seeks their company as a reliable boat. Their presence acts on others through proximity, spreading virtues like wind carrying pollen from a flower.

chapters.subtitle: All living and non-living things share one consciousness.
chapters.es: Ants scramble for food while trees endure a shallow sleep, sensitive to touch. Even stones rest in a heavy, dreamless slumber. Every living form, from insects to humans, carries a sense of ahaṅkṛti and experiences the pull of hunger and fear. These lives unfold within cidākāśa, the conscious sky, where the world appears like a reflection in a mirror. Just as a wave breaking does not diminish the ocean, the passing of a physical body leaves the underlying consciousness untouched.

chapters.subtitle: Awareness shapes how people experience life and death.
chapters.es: Individual experience follows the firm convictions of saṃvid. Just as smoke drifts through the sky or whirlpools stir the ocean, worldly cycles arise within cidvyoma. Those who view the body as mere matter encounter the dullness of a stone, while others perceive the universe as a svapnanagara or dream city. Consciousness radiates like moonlight, projecting life-seeds that move like insects in the air. Realizing this, the wise perform their duties like machines, remaining untouched by the world’s shifting joys and sorrows.

chapters.subtitle: Pure consciousness remains while bodies come and go.
chapters.es: Pure consciousness (cinmātra) pervades everything, appearing as the world like a dream within an empty sky. This awareness takes the form of humans, gods, and objects, yet it remains birthless and deathless. Different traditions name it brahman, śūnya, or śiva. While the physical body might turn to dust or endure like a mountain, the underlying consciousness is unaffected. Death is either the end of worldly fever or a festival of a new body. One moves through life without fear, acting and eating without attachment to results.

chapters.subtitle: Characteristics of a wise person in the world
chapters.es: The liberated person treats stones as friends and forest trees as kinsmen. They move through crowded cities as if through empty space, viewing disasters as festivals. While engaging in social customs, their internal state remains cool like candana wood. They may appear as kings, beggars, or silent hermits, yet they remain anchored in the realization that the self is acchedya and eternal. Like a calm ocean, they accept what comes without seeking or rejecting the results of their daily actions.

chapters.subtitle: Consciousness is eternal and the world is only an appearance.
chapters.es: Pure cinmātra remains indestructible even when the body perishes. The world never truly began; it appears as a display within the cinnabhas, much like a city perceived in a dream. While people chase fleeting honors as death approaches, this śāstra serves as a lamp and a medicine for the mind. It rejects the reality of material atoms, comparing worldly objects to the horns of a hare. Daily inquiry dissolves the illusion of the seen, allowing one to rest in silent, undivided awareness.

chapters.subtitle: Consciousness appears as the physical world like a dream.
chapters.es: The five elements—space, air, fire, water, and earth—emerge from the formless void like a dream. Physical objects lack substance, appearing only through the light of jñapti, or pure consciousness. This waking world resembles a dream city where the cetanākāśa manifests as the perceived universe. Individual identity and the cosmos are a single, dense block of reality. Liberation follows when the mind rests in its own nature, remaining undisturbed by the presence or absence of the physical body.

chapters.subtitle: The world is compared to a long dream.
chapters.es: The waking world functions exactly like a dream, where consciousness experiences itself as mountains, cities, and oceans. A dreamer interacts with friends and faces death, only to wake into this life; similarly, one who dies in this world wakes into another. Objects like a ghaṭa or śaila remain inseparable from consciousness, just as heat resides in fire or motion in wind. These states are identical, appearing as solid matter while remaining as empty and luminous as the sky.

chapters.subtitle: Defining pure consciousness and the world's dreamlike nature.
chapters.es: Pure consciousness, or cidākāśa, exists in the momentary gap between perceptions or the stillness before sleep. It functions like a thread binding a garland, sustaining all objects without being seen. Since the physical world lacks a material cause, it exists as a dreamlike appearance within this internal sky. A person acts in the world with a quiet mind like a living stone, remaining internally still. All forms arise and dissolve in this space, which fulfills thoughts like a kalpadruma or wish-fulfilling tree.

chapters.subtitle: The world is an illusion made of pure consciousness.
chapters.es: The universe shines as cit, filling the void like a transparent sky. Though people move and act, they remain as still as wood, for the physical world lacks a creative cause. Just as one sees floating hair or a river in a mirage, the earth and sky appear without actually existing. Attempting to analyze these elements is like trying to wash the empty air. The mind perceives a dream-like reality where no material objects truly arise from the formless expanse of consciousness.

chapters.subtitle: King Vipaścit mobilizes his army against a four-sided invasion.
chapters.es: King Vipaścit rules the city of Tatamiti and worships the fire deity Vahni. Messengers arrive at his court reporting the death of regional governors and the collapse of borders. A wounded general from the north enters, bleeding and breathless, to announce a fourfold invasion. The king commands his ministers to open the armories and distribute weapons. Soldiers don their kaṅkaṭa armor while elephants and horses gather. The palace fills with the clamor of mobilization as enemy forces surround the kingdom.

chapters.subtitle: A king sacrifices himself to gain four bodies.
chapters.es: Ministers advise the king to use daṇḍa against invading forces. After reflecting on his long reign, the monarch enters a fire temple to worship Agni. Fearing defeat in his old age, he decapitates himself with a sword, offering his head as an āhuti. He requests four bodies to defend his realm. From the sacrificial kuṇḍa, four identical warriors emerge on horses. These figures, armed and armored, possess the king's combined strength to destroy enemies in all directions.

chapters.subtitle: A violent battle breaks out near a city.
chapters.es: A fierce battle erupts outside the city, engulfing villages in fire and smoke. Arrows and tomara spears block the sun while elephants collide with the force of waves. Rivers of blood sweep away chariots as headless kabandha corpses dance among feasting vetāla ghouls. Warriors embrace death amidst the clanging of kankaṭa armor and the roar of stone-throwing machines. As houses burn and townspeople flee, the sky fills with dust, severed limbs, and the rhythmic flapping of blood-stained banners.

chapters.subtitle: King Vipascit defeats the enemy army with divine weapons.
chapters.es: King Vipascit marches from his city with a caturaṅga force as drums thunder like a dissolving aeon. He confronts an enemy host surging like a yuddhoddhatārṇava or battle-ocean, where umbrellas form foam and chariots create whirlpools. Drawing his bow, he releases the vāyavyāstra and parjanyāstra. Rivers of arrows, stones, and lightning sweep the field. The opposing army scatters like dust in a gale, leaving behind a mire of blood, flesh, and fallen elephants.

chapters.subtitle: The fates of various armies after a great battle.
chapters.es: The cedi fall into the southern ocean after being cut down in sandalwood forests. Pārasīka troops wash away like leaves in the tide, while yavana soldiers are consumed by crocodiles on the western shore. Darada fighters hide in mountain crevices, and hūṇa forces sink into northern sands. While some groups find refuge in celestial groves, others are slain by lions or eat poisonous fruits. Torrential winds and weapon-fire sweep the landscape, scattering the broken remnants of these armies into caves, rivers, and distant woods.

chapters.subtitle: The four wise men reach the great salt ocean.
chapters.es: The four vipaścit pursue their retreating enemies until they reach the shores of the great oceans. Both friendly and hostile forces diminish like evaporating streams, and weapons vanish into a clear sky. Before them lies the vast sea, filled with makara and whirlpools. The water reflects the celestial sphere, while its waves crash against forests of clove and palm. Reaching the salt ocean's edge, the travelers observe the reflecting heavens and the deep roar of the tides.

chapters.subtitle: A survey of mountains, oceans, and seasonal winds.
chapters.es: Travelers observe a landscape where ocean waves strike mountain slopes and forests of bakula and coconut trees. Mount Ṛkṣavat echoes with roars while the Malaya range stands like a sandalwood-anointed wrestler. Winds carry the scent of mango and kadamba blossoms from the Gandhamādana caves to the sea. From the Vindhya peaks to the shores of Pārasīka, the air moves through camphor groves and jasmine. Vidyādhara couples leave flower beds in mountain valleys as the sun reflects off the gem-filled waters.

chapters.subtitle: Attendants describe mountains, oceans, and rural life.
chapters.es: Attendants describe the mountain ranges of Himādri and Malaya. Rivers enter the ocean like fine threads woven into cloth. Within the sea, Keśava sleeps near the subaqueous vaḍavānala fire. Raindrops transform into muktāphala within oyster shells and elephant temples. Seasonal winds carry the scent of sandalwood through villages where women swing under campaka trees. At sunset, the sun’s chariot descends and the moon floods the sky like milk, illuminating the peaks of Kailāsa and tribal dwellings.

chapters.subtitle: Descriptions of the sky, mountains, and different animals.
chapters.es: Warriors clash with śara and śūla while celestial chariots carry the fallen upward. Above the dust, the ākāśa remains vast and empty, containing the sun and moon without losing its purity. On Suvela's slopes, a Vidyādhara woman sings to a tree that regains its form as her husband. Peacocks dance in mountain villages near the Kāverī river. While the kāka searches for filth among lotuses, the kokila hides in the thickets, waiting for vasanta to reveal its song.

chapters.subtitle: Descriptions of a lake, its lotuses, swans, and bees.
chapters.es: Bees swarm over kahlāra and utpala lotuses in a lake where rājahāṃsa swans glide. Pollen turns the water golden as wind shakes the stalks. A swan pursues its own reflection in the ripples, mistaking the image for a mate. While some bees enjoy mango blossoms, others struggle against thorns or the winter cold. The lake moves like a woman with waves for eyes and bird songs for anklets. Water droplets fall from lotus leaves as birds stir the thick reeds.

chapters.subtitle: Birds and travelers respond to the rainy season.
chapters.es: The monsoon landscape unfolds as water birds like the baka and madgu hunt for fish among the lotuses. A wagtail captures an insect, while peacocks dance at the sight of dark clouds, ignoring lake water to wait for rain. Travelers observe these scenes, watching women carry cooling lilies to friends distressed by separation. Some birds mistake forest fire smoke for rain clouds. The wind carries the scent of padma and kumuda flowers through lakeside trees as travelers remember their distant homes.

chapters.subtitle: A man survives a funeral pyre through mental devotion.
chapters.es: A traveler recounts his separation from his wife while resting near a mountain. Seeing a cloud wreathed in lightning, he addresses it as a messenger. His physical stillness leads others to believe he is dead; they transport him to a śmaśāna and set his body upon a citī. Immersed in memories of his beloved, he remains oblivious to the heat, experiencing the flames as cool moonlight. He concludes by describing the cremation ground, filled with skulls, vultures, and the scent of smoke.

chapters.subtitle: Companions describe the mountain forest and its inhabitants.
chapters.es: Winds carry pollen through banana groves and cool the brows of women. Sea breezes stir lions in mountain caves. Tall tāla trees stand like pillars, while red kiṃśuka flowers resemble blood-stained warriors. Celestial gandharva couples sleep on the peaks of Mahendra, and divine singers rest under kalpavṛkṣa canopies. In the hermitage of Mandapāla, natural enemies like lions and elephants play together. Kings from four oceans gather with tributes, intending to rule with patience amidst the quiet vanavilāsinī landscape.

chapters.subtitle: Kings receive a boon to reach the world's end.
chapters.es: The Vipaścit kings establish camps on the ocean shore. After reflecting on the vast distances they have crossed using divine vehicles, they summon the fire god Hutāśana. They ask for a boon to reach the final edge of the physical world. They intend to travel in their physical bodies for as long as possible, and should their forms fail, to continue the journey through their minds. Hutāśana grants their request and disappears as the sun begins to rise.

chapters.subtitle: Four kings walk across the ocean toward the horizon.
chapters.es: The four kings settle their earthly affairs at dawn and walk into the sea, ignoring the cries of their grieving households. Empowered by mantra-śakti, they step onto the waves as if on solid ground. They climb mountainous billows and spin through āvarta whirlpools without fear. Though sea monsters swallow them, the brothers emerge unscathed, carried by winds across hundreds of yojanas. Rising toward the sun and sinking toward pātāla, they travel the deep waters like royal swans moving through white foam.

chapters.subtitle: The travels and transformations of the four kings.
chapters.es: Four forms of Vipaścit travel on foot across distant dvīpa and oceans to observe the workings of avidyā. One is swallowed by a giant fish and expelled near Kṣīroda, while another is cursed to become a yakṣa in Śākadvīpa. They encounter demons in Krauñcadvīpa and live among apsaras in the kalpavṛkṣa forests of Meru. Whether transformed into stone or carried by the bird Hemacūḍa to Kuśadvīpa, they undergo physical transformations and births across a mythical landscape of islands and celestial groves.

chapters.subtitle: One consciousness takes many different forms and bodies.
chapters.es: A single saṃvid projects varied experiences within itself, much like a mirror reflects multiple objects. Deities like Viṣṇu act through multiple bodies at once, performing deeds across distant lands. One consciousness experiences life as a lion in the Śākadvīpa region, a frog in a golden cave, or a lover in a grotto. Beings sleep on mountain slopes, transform into stones, or dwell in dark wells for years. These movements through space occur while consciousness remains a single ciddhana.

chapters.subtitle: Liberated sages perform worldly duties while remaining detached.
chapters.es: Vipaścit transforms into a tree, a stone, and a lion across various regions. One manifestation of the sage uses rituals or knowledge to free another from these physical binds. Vasiṣṭha explains that such practitioners remain caught between awareness and ignorance. In contrast, the jīvanmukta acts in the world with a still mind. Deities like Viṣṇu and Śiva, or kings like Janaka, fulfill their roles—ruling, fighting, or grieving—without internal distress. They view the three worlds as an empty rainbow appearing within the cidākāśa.

chapters.subtitle: The journeys and fates of the four Vipaścits.
chapters.es: The four forms of Vipaścit meet separate ends: one is crushed by an elephant on Krauñcadvīpa, others perish by fire, curses, or a crocodile's jaws. Their consciousness survives through saṃskāra, observing the earth's oceans and islands from the sky. One gains knowledge from Janārdana and reaches nirvāṇa. Another rules a kingdom on Śālmalidvīpa, while a third survives inside a sea monster before ascending the golden slopes of Mount Lokāloka. They finally encounter the absolute darkness at the universe's edge.

chapters.subtitle: How consciousness creates the structure of the universe.
chapters.es: The earth hangs in space like a toy created by a child’s imagination. It appears solid because consciousness, cit, perceives it as such, similar to how eye disease creates illusions of floating hair. Stars circle the mountain lokāloka within the ākāśa. This cosmic egg, the brahmāṇḍa-kharpara, possesses a thick shell resembling a wood-apple. Directional movements like rising or falling are merely flashes of thought; within this universal sphere, no absolute up or down exists.

chapters.subtitle: Vipaścit travels through cosmic layers into infinite space.
chapters.es: Vipaścit journeys beyond the physical shell of the universe, casting off his material form for a subtle ātivāhika body. He passes through successive layers of water, fire, wind, and ether, each expanding tenfold. Beyond these barriers, he enters the infinite brahmākāśa. Countless world-eggs appear and vanish in this vast space like ripples. Vipaścit continues to wander through endless cycles of creation, encountering new gods and lands. This movement persists because his mind remains bound to its initial resolve within the limitless consciousness.

chapters.subtitle: Vipaścit travels through worlds and becomes a pet deer.
chapters.es: Vipaścit wanders through islands and celestial shells, propelled by his vāsanā. While one aspect of him attains brahmatā through the company of the wise, another takes the form of a deer. The brahmāṇḍa, split by the creator, allows passage across different world-systems. By chance, this deer enters the current world-system. The animal is revealed to be the pet recently gifted to Rāma by the king of Trigarta. The deer walks into the assembly, displaying its spotted hide and restless gaze.

chapters.subtitle: A deer transforms back into a human king.
chapters.es: Vasistha summons a fuel-less, smokeless fire through meditation within the assembly. The deer, formerly King Vipashcit, leaps into the golden flames. His animal form dissolves, and he emerges as a man named Bhāsa, wearing an akṣamālā and a golden yajñopavīta. After regaining memory of his previous births, Bhāsa bows to the sages and King Dasharatha. Vasistha declares the end of the king's long wandering through avidyā, and the assembly welcomes him back to human society.

chapters.subtitle: Vipaścit recounts his past lives and the spherical earth.
chapters.es: Three princes wander the spherical bhūgolaka like ants on a ball, seeking an edge that does not exist within the cosmic brāhmasankalpa. Vipaścit recounts his own journey through infinite cycles. He inhabited the bodies of a thousand-year tree, a golden deer on Mount Meru, and a celestial nymph. He describes cities drifting through the sky and a woman whose body reflects the three worlds. These diverse forms arise from cit, appearing as solid realities across the relative directions of the void.

chapters.subtitle: A traveler experiences multiple lives across different realms.
chapters.es: A traveler rests with an apsaras on Mount Mandara before she carries him into the sky. He lives seven years as a bee within a golden lotus on the Gaṅgā. He sees a realm where self-luminous beings dwell without sun, stars, or scriptures. Later, as the ascetic Amarasoma, he traverses mountain peaks and falls into a vast celestial ocean. Though he views the world as avidyā, he continues to drift through shifting dreams, unable to find the end of the illusion.

chapters.subtitle: A giant body falls from the sky onto earth.
chapters.es: A massive human form composed of flesh falls from the sky alongside the sun. The impact shatters mountain peaks and destabilizes the saptadvīpa across the earth. Seeking safety from the tremors, the narrator enters the body of Agni, who carries him into the atmosphere on a parrot’s back. From above, celestial beings observe the fallen body covering the entire globe. Siddhas and ṛṣis gather to offer prayers to the goddess Kālarātri, who wears a garland of skulls and animal skins.

chapters.subtitle: A goddess drinks blood from a giant fallen corpse.
chapters.es: An immense corpse falls from the sky, covering the saptadvīpa and extending beyond the horizon. The goddess Caṇḍikā appears with a retinue of ghosts and spirits. She consumes the blood of the body until her form grows full, then begins a dance across the firmament. As lesser spirits feast on the remains, the world transforms into a landscape of blood and bone. From the lokāloka peaks, the gods lament the loss of the earth's forests and oceans under the carnage.

chapters.subtitle: The gods rebuild the world from a giant corpse.
chapters.es: The gods observe the remnants of the giant corpse after spirits consume its flesh. Layers of fat, or medas, spread across the seven islands like white clouds, eventually drying to form the earth’s surface. This substance gives the world its name, medinī. Five remaining bones settle as mountain peaks. The gods gather the leftover blood into a single basin, creating the madirārṇava or ocean of wine, where bhūta spirits and yogeśvarī deities drink and dance. Prajāpati then initiates a new creation cycle.

chapters.subtitle: An asura becomes a mosquito, a deer, and a hunter.
chapters.es: Pure consciousness assumes the form of an arrogant asura who destroys a sage’s forest dwelling. Cursed to become a maśaka, he lives briefly among grasses until a deer’s hoof crushes him. Because he sees the animal while dying, he is reborn as a deer, and later as a hunter. When he encounters a sage in the woods, he learns that life is as fleeting as lightning or wind-blown clouds. He then seeks nirvāṇa to end these constant rebirths.

chapters.subtitle: A sage explores the universe hidden inside the heart.
chapters.es: A hunter discards his bow and arrows to live as a silent ascetic. To understand dreams, an observer projects prāṇa into the heart of another creature. Inside narrow channels of blood and heat, he discovers the ojas, shimmering like white clouds. Within this substance, he observes a complete universe of oceans, mountains, and gods. This internal vision reveals that waking and dreaming are identical movements of the ciddhātu. Ultimately, the perceived world dissolves into the quiet, unborn state of bodha.

chapters.subtitle: A person enters another's mind and watches him sleep.
chapters.es: The narrator enters another's consciousness, merging like a drop of oil on water. This union produces a doubled vision of two suns and two earths until the perceptions fuse. When the host sleeps, his senses withdraw into the hṛt-kośa like a tortoise retracting its limbs. Entering the heart-space, the narrator experiences the stillness of suṣupti. He observes how the mind projects the body and prāṇa from the formless brahman, just as a city is created within a dream.

chapters.subtitle: A dreamer watches the world dissolve in a flood.
chapters.es: The mind and prāṇa act as a chariot and driver, together manifesting the perceived world. When breath settles into the nāḍī channels, the observer enters a sleeper’s heart to witness a dream of the cosmic pralaya. He experiences a householder’s terror in a great deluge before remembering his ascetic identity through samādhi. Detached, he watches gods and mountains drift like debris in the churning currents, seeing the entire universe dissolve into the waters of a single, vast ocean.

chapters.subtitle: Vasiṣṭha witnesses cosmic cycles and a dream life
chapters.es: Vasiṣṭha enters the heart of a cosmic being and witnesses the world's destruction by water. Falling into a dream, he lives a century as a village brāhmaṇa, tending to cattle and domestic rituals. A guest’s discourse on cinmātra restores his memory. He exits the being’s body through the prāṇa and observes his own meditating form. Returning to the inner heart, he watches twelve suns incinerate the universe, turning the world into a sphere of flame while remaining untouched by the heat.

chapters.subtitle: Fire and wind consume the seven worlds.
chapters.es: The observer remains calm amidst a cosmic conflagration, recognizing the flames as part of a dream. A violent wind arises, roaring like thunder and carrying the debris of the seven worlds through the sky. This wind mixes with fire to form alātacakra or whirling firebrands. Mountains, rivers of flame, and falling celestial beings fill the air. Covered in white ash and draped in sheets of fire, the wind dances like the god Hara during the final dissolution of the universe.

chapters.subtitle: How consciousness creates the world and human action.
chapters.es: The narrator observes oceans, fires, and entire mountain ranges held within the small chamber of a human heart. This world lacks a material cause, manifesting only as cidākāśa, the space of consciousness. Just as a dream city lacks physical brick, the universe arises from thought. Primordial beings remain free from karma, yet individual souls create their own chains by imagining themselves separate from Brahman. Like a whirlpool that is only water, the world dissolves once the mind recognizes its true, undivided state.

chapters.subtitle: The world is a mental dream within consciousness.
chapters.es: A wise observer sees the physical world dissolve into brahman just as a perceived snake vanishes into a rope. This universe lacks a material cause, appearing instead as a sequence of mental impressions like a dream (svapna). Within the heart of consciousness (saṃvit), cities and cycles of birth and death arise as non-obstructive forms. Even at death, the mind constructs new worlds from latent habits. All existence resides within the sky of awareness, remaining peaceful and unchanging despite the appearance of movement.

chapters.subtitle: The world exists as a dream within consciousness.
chapters.es: The world appears like light flickering in the empty sky. Just as many people dream of different cities while sleeping in the same room, countless worlds exist within consciousness without touching one another. These forms emerge like whirlpools in water, lacking a physical cause. From the formless brahman, a natural order called niyati arises to govern time and space. Though mountains and oceans seem solid, they are only the flicker of a single atomic consciousness perceived as a dream.

chapters.subtitle: How bodily humors influence a person's dreams and perceptions.
chapters.es: The jīva perceives shifting realities based on the balance of bodily humors. When śleṣma increases, the mind envisions cool lotus ponds and snowy peaks. An excess of pitta transforms the landscape into scorched earth and forest fires. Dominant vāta triggers sensations of flight, falling, and quaking mountains. In deep sleep, the soul rests like a frog encased in stone or a seed within a fruit. These fluctuating physical states project the appearance of a solid world from the silent void of cit.

chapters.subtitle: The world exists as a dream within cosmic consciousness.
chapters.es: While drifting within the cosmic heart during a rain of mountains, a witness falls into suṣupti, a dense and dark sleep. Consciousness eventually awakens like a lotus, transitioning into a svapna state where the world arises from within. This universe is held in cit just as heat stays in fire. Since existence is merely a manifestation of sat, the world is fundamentally aja, or unborn. Lacking an external cause, the cosmos persists like a dream city, remaining eternally śānta.

chapters.subtitle: How the world and old habits reappear after sleep.
chapters.es: A world emerges from deep sleep like waves from a sea or blossoms on a tree. It appears as a wall-less painting in the sky, housing beings like seeds inside a dāḍima. Upon seeing an old village and family, the pull of vāsanā draws the observer back into a household. The mind acts as an ādarśa, reflecting what it sees until clear knowledge fails. Only steady study and satsaṅga can still the movement of this world-delusion, the jagadbhrama.

chapters.subtitle: Waking and dreaming states are the same.
chapters.es: A seeker asks why some dreams come true. The answer lies in saṃvit, or consciousness, which hardens into whatever reality it perceives. Like a crow landing on a palm fruit, events occur through kākatālīya logic. There are no objects outside the mind. Instead, the waking world operates as a long dream, while dreams act as brief waking states. This entire universe flows like water within brahman. Waking, dreaming, and deep sleep are different names for the same flicker in the void.

chapters.subtitle: A guest explains that the world is a dream.
chapters.es: After sixteen years of domestic life, a householder receives the sage Ugratapas as a guest. Following a meal, the host asks why entire populations suffer collective calamities like famine or drought. The sage smiles and instructs his host to examine his own identity. He identifies both guest and host as dream-figures within a dream-world. This world is a projection of pure consciousness, cinmātra. Causality governs the world only when the mind perceives it; otherwise, the universe remains without external cause.

chapters.subtitle: The universe exists as a movement within infinite consciousness.
chapters.es: The vast sky of consciousness, cidākāśa, holds the minds of all beings, from the creator down to the smallest creature. This space remains infinite and motionless, even as the world appears as a temporary vibration within it. For the wise, the perceived universe shines as the natural glow of pure awareness. The foolish mistake these flickering images for solid matter. All movements of thought eventually settle into an absolute silence, where the distinction between the seer and the seen vanishes into one calm reality.

chapters.subtitle: A fire destroys everything revealing a dream world.
chapters.es: A fire consumes the city and forest, reducing homes and bodies to ash. The wind scatters the remains like flower petals. Like the sage Agastya drinking the ocean, the fire subsides after devouring everything. This destruction shows the waking world is a dream. Every person is a svapnapuruṣa existing within the cidākāśa, or space of consciousness. The sage lives as a householder, knowing all forms are simply the ātman appearing as worlds, much like heat shimmering in gold.

chapters.subtitle: A sage predicts future destruction and life in a forest.
chapters.es: The sage sits in silence on his bed after speaking, while the listener contemplates the world as a svapna. This creation appears like a dream, lacking physical foundations like pṛthvī yet seeming solid. The sage predicts a future famine and a conflict between local chiefs that will destroy the listener’s village and family. Afterward, they will dwell together in a quiet grove. A forest of śāla and tamāla trees will grow there, housing lotuses and birds like the celestial Nandana garden.

chapters.subtitle: A hunter receives teachings about the nature of reality.
chapters.es: A weary hunter enters the forest to practice penance. He learns about the nature of dreams and ātmajñāna through shared stories. As years pass, the observer remains indifferent to living or dying. He examines the surrounding landscape, realizing that earth, wind, and mountains are not solid matter but the radiance of pure consciousness, or cinmātra. He questions the origin of this illusion, seeing that the universe appears without a physical source, existing only as a mental reflection in the void.

chapters.subtitle: A teacher describes reality as a dream to a hunter.
chapters.es: A speaker views the earth, wind, and mountains as a unified consciousness. He moves through the world without ego, performing tasks as they arise while remaining a non-doer. He describes the hunter before him and the surrounding landscape as figures in a mutual dream. This reality exists as cinmātra, where individuals perceive what their own minds construct. Because wisdom requires time to settle, the hunter must engage in abhyāsa to move beyond dualities and reach a state of permanent quiet.

chapters.subtitle: A hunter explores the universe with a giant body.
chapters.es: A hunter stands stunned after hearing a sage’s teaching, his mind wavering like a man lost at sea. Seeking the physical boundary of the world, he performs tapas for centuries. He receives a boon of an ever-expanding body that travels through the sky with the speed of a garuḍa. He flies past countless universes, witnessing them as mere leaves in the vastness of space. Recognizing that a physical form cannot exhaust the reach of avidyā, he eventually abandons his massive frame through breath control.

chapters.subtitle: A king discovers that consciousness grants whatever one seeks.
chapters.es: After the physical frame falls, the jīva drifts as a subtle vātalava in the expanse of space. It perceives a new world, assuming the identity of King Sindhu. Following a battle against the ruler Vidūratha, Sindhu questions his minister about his opponent’s defeat. The minister explains that while Vidūratha sought mokṣa from Sarasvatī, Sindhu focused his saṃkalpa on worldly conquest. The internal consciousness, or saṃvid, fulfills the specific intentions shaped by a person's mental habits and long-standing abhyāsa.

chapters.subtitle: A minister explains soul categories and Sindhu seeks liberation.
chapters.es: King Sindhu questions the origins of his ignorance. His minister explains that brahman assumes the form of the mind and a subtle ātivāhika body, making the world appear like a dream. He identifies Sindhu as a tāmasa-tāmasi soul, a category defined by long cycles of darkness. To escape this, Sindhu abandons his kingdom for the forest. By practicing vicāra and seeking the company of the wise, he uses personal effort to reach a state beyond worldly changes.

chapters.subtitle: A hunter's massive corpse transforms into the earth's soil.
chapters.es: After his companion attains nirvāṇa, a vyādha obtains a boon to grow to an immense size. He traverses the sky until weariness leads him to abandon his physical form. His corpse falls like a thunderbolt, covering the world. The goddess Caṇḍikā devours the flesh, while the remaining medas, or fat, hardens into the terrestrial ground. This transformation explains the origin of the term medinī for the earth, where forests and cities eventually emerge from the ancient remains.

chapters.subtitle: Vipaścit becomes a deer and learns about reality.
chapters.es: The fire god Agni vanishes into the clear sky to attend a sacrifice. Vipaścit wanders through the heavens, witnessing worlds inhabited by creatures with bodies of clay, wood, and stone. After an encounter with Indra, he inhabits the body of a deer, eating grass on mountain ridges until a hunter captures him. These shifting forms reveal the power of avidyā. All perceived objects are simply the vibration of cit, existing within brahman like a dream or the wind’s movement in space.

chapters.subtitle: Vasistha explains how thoughts create heaven and hell
chapters.es: The assembly returns at dawn after a night of rest. Vasistha describes avidyā as a mirage or a tangled vine that disappears when studied. Through saṃkalpa, the mind builds its own reality in the space of consciousness. Pure thoughts manifest as celestial cities with nectar lakes and multiple suns. Conversely, impure intentions produce hellish landscapes where bodies endure crushing stones, raining embers, and sharp blades. The jīva inhabits these mental forms, moving between bodies based on its own habitual conceptions.

chapters.subtitle: The world is a reflection of the mind.
chapters.es: Waves rise from the sea and vibrations emerge from the wind, just as the world appears within the cidvyoman. This waking state operates like a dream city, held in place only until a new perception replaces it. Dreams and waking life share one nature as fluctuations of consciousness. By discarding the notion of ignorance, one eats, drinks, and acts in the world while remaining as motionless as a stone, anchored in the unchanging sky of the self.

chapters.subtitle: Seeing the world as consciousness to overcome worldly suffering
chapters.es: The universe exists as cit, appearing like a city within a dream or a phantom made of clay. Just as waves rise within a river, worldly forms move within the space of consciousness. This perceived reality acts as a shadow without independent substance. To escape the cycle of birth, one must use the boat of a clear mind. By subduing the senses and the restless wind of the mind, the individual crosses the ocean of saṃsāra before old age and death arrive.

chapters.subtitle: How to control the mind and senses.
chapters.es: One tames the wild elephant of the mind using the sharp hook of saṃvit. Just as leather shoes protect the feet as if the whole earth were covered in hides, mastering the mind subdues every sense. This world appears without a root, resembling a dream or a mirage in the empty sky of awareness. By turning from objects to the state of turya, the seeker moves beyond debates over duality. Constant reflection on these truths dissolves ignorance like salt seasoning food.

chapters.subtitle: The world exists only as pure consciousness.
chapters.es: Living atoms float within the solar sphere of consciousness. Upon reaching awakening, objects shed their individual forms to merge into a single reality. For the sage, distinctions between I and you disappear, much like a mirage failing to form on a solid mountain. This world lacks a beginning, a cause, or a creator. Like a dream city, the universe remains a void of pure awareness, or cinmātram. Even the first being, Brahmā, exists only as the light of the cidgagana shining within itself.

chapters.subtitle: Waking and dreaming states are essentially the same.
chapters.es: Waking and dreaming states flow into one another, differing only in their duration. A sleeper perceives the dream as jāgrat, while the waking person views the dream as fleeting. Both states arise from cic-camatkṛti, a flash of consciousness that appears as the three worlds. This world-appearance remains śūnya, or void, like a dream landscape. Whether in water, stone, or the sky, these forms lack a solid core. Recognizing that all perceived objects are mere flickers of consciousness removes the need for attachment.

chapters.subtitle: The universe exists as carvings inside a solid stone.
chapters.es: Reality remains a vast, solid stone lacking any hollows or joints. Within its diamond-hard core, patterns form mountains, oceans, cities, and celestial bodies. These markings represent the elements of earth, water, fire, and air, alongside the ego and mind. Although the stone is unbroken and singular, carvings of gods and demons reside in its heart. Every object exists as a density of cinmātra, the pure consciousness that constitutes the flesh of this stone, appearing as markings within a single, seamless block.

chapters.subtitle: The world is a dreamlike appearance in pure consciousness.
chapters.es: The theories of ātmakhyāti and asatkhyāti vanish like the horns of a rabbit. This world appears as a crowded place yet remains as empty as the sky. Like a city seen in a dream or a woman’s reflection, the jagat lacks physical substance. A person walks and acts while maintaining the silence of a stone. The five elements, or pañcabhūta, never truly formed. Waking and dreaming states are mere memories. Consciousness shines steadily, unchanging like the wind in an empty sky.

chapters.subtitle: Consciousness spontaneously appears as the physical world.
chapters.es: The universe emerges spontaneously within consciousness like waves rising on the sea or branches extending from a tree. This appearance lacks an external cause, manifesting as a dream in the void of cit. Just as scent belongs to a flower and heat to fire, the world is inseparable from its source. Notions like buddhi and ahaṃkāra label parts of this single reality. Like a doll resting uncarved within a wooden pillar, the cosmos resides within brahman as a natural, effortless form.

chapters.subtitle: The traits and inner state of a liberated person.
chapters.es: The jīvanmukta rests in the cidvyoman, unmoved by the fluctuations of pleasure and pain. While performing daily tasks, they appear as if in a deep, dreamless sleep, their internal focus remaining steady in the absolute. This traveler has finished the long journey through the world’s forest and now reclines on the bed of space. They swallow the entire universe into their own being, becoming smaller than an atom yet more expansive than the sky, finally finding quietude in the self.

chapters.subtitle: Good actions and virtues act as a supportive family.
chapters.es: One’s own action, svakarma, serves as a constant companion and protector. It behaves like a father providing comfort and a spouse offering restraint. This companion purifies the individual like fire refining gold, encouraging yajña and dāna. Alongside svakarma, the quality of muditā acts as a loyal wife, while karuṇā and samatā provide internal stability. A person living with these virtues moves through the world like a figure in a painting, remaining silent during idle talk but speaking clearly on truth.

chapters.subtitle: The world is made of consciousness, not physical matter.
chapters.es: The universe appears as the ether of consciousness, saṃvidākāśa, yet lacks material substance. Just as a dream city arises from the mind, the three worlds emerge within the pure space of brahman. This reality resides in the interval between one perception and the next. The sun and moon shine only through this internal light. One who realizes this remains steady, neither grasping nor discarding actions, embodying a natural state like the inherent heat of fire or the coldness of ice.

chapters.subtitle: The physical world exists only as a mental idea.
chapters.es: The first prajāpati exists as pure cidvyoma, free from pṛthvī and physical form. He operates as mind alone, similar to a city built of saṅkalpa. Since the world never truly formed, smṛti has no objects to recall. This appearance of mountains and rivers mirrors a dream city or a mirage in the desert. These forms arise by chance, like the kākatālīya logic, existing as the silent movement of consciousness while remaining unchanged in the absolute void of Brahman.

chapters.subtitle: Everything in the world is made of one consciousness.
chapters.es: The infinite ātman occupies the body and the physical world just as a tree holds its leaves. Pure consciousness appears as stones, wood, and moving beings within a cosmic dream. These cycles of creation rise like whirlpools in the ocean of cidvyoman. The cosmic person, virāṭ, manifests with the sky as a head and the earth as feet. Just as a golden bracelet remains gold, the universe is recognized as brahman when the mind ceases to see separate forms and names.

chapters.subtitle: Understanding that the world is an illusion brings liberation.
chapters.es: Pure cit appears as the universe, resembling waves moving across an ocean. This world operates like a dream city experienced during the waking state, existing without a physical cause. Liberation is not a state of insentience or deep sleep; it is the total absence of the perceived object. This clarity arises from studying the śāstra and refining the intellect, rather than through rituals or pilgrimages. Knowledge alone dissolves the illusion of creation, leaving the quiet stillness of the underlying reality.

chapters.subtitle: The world is a dream created by consciousness.
chapters.es: The cidvyoma projects the world like a dream city, though no physical elements ever arise. Just as water moves in whirlpools or wind creates a breeze, the manas produces the appearance of earth and sky within itself. These forms resemble hair-tufts seen in an empty sky, existing only as an optical error. By investigating these verses, the student recognizes that the observer and the object are the same brahman. This knowledge stops the restless mind and dissolves the false reality of the body.

chapters.subtitle: Consciousness creates many worlds within atoms.
chapters.es: A single point of consciousness, the cidvyoma-paramāṇu, perceives itself as an individual soul through a dream. It adopts the form of ego and intellect, eventually imagining a physical body with five senses. Within this mental framework, the three worlds appear, populated by living beings and elements. These infinite universes reside within every atom, appearing substantial while remaining part of the void. Like waves moving across water, these countless worlds remain identical to the pure brahman from which they arise.

chapters.subtitle: The world is a causeless dream within pure consciousness.
chapters.es: The universe arises from brahman without an external cause, appearing as a spontaneous dream or mental projection. Just as heat belongs to fire and waves belong to water, the world is the inherent nature of consciousness. Objects take shape through imagination but lack physical substance, resembling a city seen in the clouds. The wise recognize that nothing is ever created or destroyed in this silent expanse. By abandoning mental impressions, one finds liberation while living, seeing only the steady light of cit in every form.

chapters.subtitle: How consciousness moves the body and becomes the universe.
chapters.es: Intangible consciousness moves the physical body. Like a blacksmith’s bellows, prāṇa flows through internal channels to animate the limbs. However, the distinction between solid matter and spirit is illusory. Ten brothers, the sons of Indu, meditate on becoming creators until their physical forms wither. Their focus transforms their minds into ten distinct universes. Ultimately, mountains, trees, and earth are saṃvid, existing as a non-obstructive reality. This pure awareness, devoid of birth or death, appears as the tangible world.

chapters.subtitle: All things consist of awareness rather than solid matter.
chapters.es: Pure consciousness constitutes the entire world. Physical bodies appear solid but remain non-obstructive cidvyoma, or consciousness-space. Like a dream during waking hours, the limbs and organs are simply forms of awareness. This reality functions without physical causes, as the mind experiences whatever it perceives. Multiplicity merges into unity like rivers entering an ocean. Awareness appears as diverse objects, similar to many shadows in a lamp-lit room. Ultimately, creation is a mere ripple in the infinite ocean of brahman.

chapters.subtitle: A man performs penance to become a world ruler.
chapters.es: Kundadanta travels to the Śrīparvata mountain where he finds an ascetic hanging upside down from a tree by a mauñja rope. The man endures the elements for twelve years to gain sovereignty over the seven continents. After Kundadanta waits with him for six months, a figure appearing like a second sun emerges from the solar orb to grant the request. The ascetic is promised a reign of seven thousand years. He descends from the branch, performs ritual ablutions, and breaks his fast with fruit.

chapters.subtitle: Two travelers find a monk in a barren forest.
chapters.es: Two travelers journey toward Mathurā, passing through the village of Rodha and the city of Sālīsa. They rest on beds of kadalī leaves in a dense forest. Entering a wilderness to find the hermitage of Gaurī, they encounter only a barren wasteland. Under a single tree, an aged ascetic sits in deep samādhi. Once awakened, the monk explains that the goddess Sarasvatī formerly inhabited this grove. Her presence once sustained a flowering forest of kadamba trees, which has now vanished into the sun-scorched earth.

chapters.subtitle: Eight brothers receive conflicting boons and a sage's curse.
chapters.es: Under a kadamba tree where Gaurī once dwelled, a former king recounts the story of eight brothers. These men performed tapas to rule the earth, while their wives secured a boon from Pārvatī to keep their husbands' spirits within their homes after death. However, their parents offended the sage Durvāsas during a pilgrimage, leading to a curse that twists their divine rewards. The brothers now return to their households to face these conflicting promises and the reality of their shared destiny.

chapters.subtitle: Eight brothers become kings within their own homes.
chapters.es: Eight brothers die, leaving their consciousness in the space of their home. Their deeds manifest as personified vara with four arms and śāpa with three eyes. These figures travel to Brahmā to settle a conflict over the brothers' destiny. After the śāpa forms vanish, the brothers assume ātivāhika bodies. Each brother simultaneously rules the sapta-dvīpa within his own house. One enjoys a palace in Ujjayinī while another wages war, each king unaware of the others inhabiting the same room.

chapters.subtitle: The world is a mental creation within consciousness.
chapters.es: The hermit under the kadamba tree describes the world as a city of imagination arising within the sky of consciousness, or cidākāśa. Awareness takes the form of the three worlds while remaining motionless, just as water creates a whirlpool. Nature follows its laws; fire rises and water flows downward within this mental structure. This display of stars, seasons, and gods like Brahmā operates like a dream or the optical illusion of hair floating in the sky. He then returns to his meditation.

chapters.subtitle: Kundadanta attains liberation after hearing Vasiṣṭha’s discourse to Rāma.
chapters.es: The aged monk enters a motionless meditation, ignoring all further speech. Kundadanta returns home, but after his brothers and friend pass away, he seeks the kadamba tree ascetic in grief. The ascetic directs him to Ayodhyā to hear Vasiṣṭha’s instructions to Rāma. After listening to the discourse, Kundadanta declares his doubts resolved. He perceives worlds existing within a mustard seed and rests in the knowledge that all existence is the beginningless brahman, unfolding within the self.

chapters.subtitle: All things exist as reflections of a single consciousness.
chapters.es: Vasishtha describes the world as an extension of brahman, comparing it to a vine branching through consciousness. The universe exists within an atom, just as an atom occupies the cosmos. This reality operates like a dream where the observer and the observed remain a single substance. The first creator, prajāpati, solidified these forms through thought, establishing elements, scriptures, and natural laws. Moving and unmoving beings arise from saṃvid like waves or droplets appearing and dissolving in a vast, silent ocean.

chapters.subtitle: Consciousness creates the physical world and the human senses.
chapters.es: The fixed order of the universe, or niyati, governs why the sun burns and days fluctuate in length. Pure consciousness manifests the five elements—earth, water, fire, wind, and ether—as if in a dream. From the seed of I-ness, or ahaṃtā, the senses emerge to perceive specific qualities. The tanmātras of sound, touch, form, taste, and smell arise through various physical organs. Despite this outward appearance of creation and movement, the underlying reality remains as still as the interior of a stone.

chapters.subtitle: How the mind creates the subtle body and world
chapters.es: Consciousness turns toward objects and takes names like jīva, citta, and ahaṃkāra. This mental activity forms the ātivāhika deha, a subtle body as empty as space. Like a dream city, the universe appears without physical substance. This being, called Brahmā or Nārāyaṇa, gradually imagines a dense frame of bones, limbs, and nerves. The ancient person then perceives the sun, earth, and skies as the limbs and structure of his own physical form.

chapters.subtitle: How the first creator perceives a physical world.
chapters.es: The subtle body (ātivāhika) of the first creator conceives of forms that gain stability through persistent awareness. This shift from subtle light to a seemingly dense physical body (ādhibhautika) happens like a dream turning into a felt reality. Identifying as Brahmā, the creator develops the sense of "I" and "mine" while projecting mountains and skies. He chants the syllable Om, organizes the Veda, and establishes names and worldly actions. This entire world remains a mental construction, appearing solid only through the power of habit.

chapters.subtitle: The world is an uncaused dream within pure awareness.
chapters.es: Bondage begins when knowledge takes the form of an object. Liberation arrives when this objectivity fades. The universe lacks a primary cause and never truly emerged; it flickers like a mirage or a city seen in a dream. Like the movement of wind, the mind projects the world through habit and memory. When these latent vāsanā dissolve through inquiry, the perceived solidity of the body and world vanishes. All things remain as the silent, unproduced cinmātra, free from the cycle of birth and death.

chapters.subtitle: Consciousness appears as the world without any external cause.
chapters.es: The world exists as the ultimate reality, appearing through a coincidental process known as kākatālīya. Just as light requires no physical wall when consciousness serves as both the radiance and its own support, the universe remains a sky within a sky. Without an external cause, the void of consciousness (cidvyoma) shines as its own object. This display functions like a mental city or a dream, where the distinction between the seer and the seen dissolves into the single, steady glow of brahman.

chapters.subtitle: The realization that the world is pure consciousness.
chapters.es: Awareness reveals that the long-held world-delusion never truly began. This saṃsāra is actually paramākāśa, an infinite sky of consciousness where worlds and mountains appear like a mirage or an imaginary city. Just as a child’s ghost or a rabbit’s horn vanishes upon inspection, the world-illusion disappears through vicāra. Only a dense mass of awareness remains, standing as an unborn and non-dual reality. This state is inherently peaceful, changeless, and spans all directions without a beginning or an end.

chapters.subtitle: Realizing the world is actually pure consciousness
chapters.es: The mind sees the three worlds as a calm expanse of space, finding no distinction between the universe and brahman. Just as water resides within water, the world sits within consciousness. The ghost of worldly existence, known as the saṃsārapisāca, vanishes as the sun of ignorance sets. The speaker dwells in nirvāṇa, resting without doubt or desire. He exists as both the entire universe and a total void, where the movement of rising and setting ends.

chapters.subtitle: How a liberated person acts in the world.
chapters.es: A jīvanmukta moves through the world with a mind as still as a stone. While the body performs daily tasks, the internal self remains empty of desire. This state resembles a dreamless sleep where the distinction between the observer and the observed vanishes. The universe appears not as solid matter but as a ripple of cit within the vast ākāśa of consciousness. By discarding mental constructions, the individual attains a silence that persists during physical activity and speech.

chapters.subtitle: The mind returns to pure consciousness by ending illusions.
chapters.es: The universe appears as a persistent illusion, much like the blue of the sky or a mirage in the desert. When the mind ceases its activity, this perceived world dissolves back into pure brahman. This state of nirvāṇa resembles a calm ocean or a lamp protected from the wind. By discarding the concepts of "I" and "this," the practitioner rests in the stillness of ākāśa, where the boundaries between the self and the external world finally disappear.

chapters.subtitle: Consciousness forms the world through mental imagination.
chapters.es: The world appears as a structure of thought, similar to a city envisioned in a dream. These perceptions arise when consciousness fluctuates, yet they possess no reality independent of the observer. Like water forming waves, the diverse shapes of the cosmos remain identical to the silent brahman. When the citta stops its movement, the false division between the self and the external world vanishes. Individual experience dissolves into the quietude of pure existence, leaving no trace of the material universe.

chapters.subtitle: The mind creates the world like a dream.
chapters.es: The world emerges as a mental construction, resembling a city in a dream or water in a mirage. Physical forms like mountains and oceans exist only as vibrations of consciousness. When mental fluctuations subside, the distinction between the observer and the seen dissolves into the quietude of brahman. This state of nirvāṇa represents the end of birth and death, achieved by recognizing that all appearances are identical to the absolute.

chapters.subtitle: The mind creates the world through its own thoughts
chapters.es: The mind functions as the seed for the expansive tree of the world, producing the fruits of pleasure and pain. When this mental seed is burnt by the fire of self-knowledge, the external world dissolves into the space of cidākāśa. Objects appear as reflections in a clear mirror, possessing no reality of their own. A person who stabilizes their mind in this pure state performs worldly duties while remaining internally silent. This condition resembles a lamp that burns steadily, unaffected by the wind of external movement.

chapters.subtitle: The world is an illusion created by the mind.
chapters.es: The world rises like a city in the clouds, appearing through the mind’s constant movement. Just as a dreamer perceives solid landscapes during sleep, the intellect projects mountains and oceans while the inner self remains silent. These forms have no more reality than heat waves in a desert. When the mind stops its outward search, the distinction between the observer and the objects of sight vanishes. The intellect recognizes all appearances as the single essence of brahman, finally resting in the state of śānti.

chapters.subtitle: The assembly honors Vasiṣṭha after his teaching.
chapters.es: Vasiṣṭha finishes his discourse, leaving the assembly in silent samādhi. Celestial siddhas shout in approval, sounding drums and scattering pārijāta blossoms. King Daśaratha offers his kingdom to the sage, who declines, accepting only a formal prostration. Rāma and his brothers bow at the teacher's feet, disappearing briefly under the heap of falling flowers. Sages such as Viśvāmitra confirm the teaching’s purity. This collective celebration ends with the sky and earth joined in a final exchange of honors and fragrant petals.

chapters.subtitle: Rama describes his inner peace and accepts his duties.
chapters.es: Rāma sits in padmāsana with joined palms, his gaze fixed on Vasiṣṭha. He describes his mind as clear as an autumn sky, free from internal knots. He compares his awareness to a crystal placed within a crystal palace. Viewing the world as a portion of the cidākāśa, he performs daily actions without joy or grief. Vasiṣṭha confirms Rāma’s arrival at the viśoka state and directs him to fulfill the requests of Viśvāmitra while governing the kingdom alongside his father.

chapters.subtitle: Rama and the assembly respond to the teaching.
chapters.es: The assembly of kings feels a cooling sensation as if drenched in nectar. Rāma’s face reflects a steady light like the moon over a milk ocean, while Daśaratha and Vāmadeva voice their approval. Rāma explains that his ignorance has vanished like dross from gold in a fire. He no longer identifies with his physical body but perceives the entire universe. Comparing his heart to a blooming lotus cooled by the water of jñāna, he offers a salutation to his own peaceful self.

chapters.subtitle: The assembly breaks and meets again the next morning.
chapters.es: The sun reaches the sky’s center, and conch shells signal the hour for midday rituals. King Daśaratha honors the sages and kings with pearls, gold, and camphor before the assembly disperses for bathing and daily duties. When the sun rises the following morning, the council reconvenes in the palace. Rāma approaches Vasiṣṭha, stating that his mind has attained nirvāṇa. He reports that all doubts have vanished and the perception of duality has dissolved, leaving him in a state of quietude.

chapters.subtitle: Consciousness appears as earth, water, fire, and air.
chapters.es: Pure consciousness appears as the physical universe, just as a dream city arises within the mind. The cid-ākāśa assumes the density of mountains, the fluidity of water, and the heat of fire without leaving its own nature. Like waves in the ocean, these forms are movements of the same substance. This cit-kacana produces the perception of time, space, and action. Nothing is created or destroyed; the entire world remains silent and still, like the interior of a solid stone.

chapters.subtitle: Vasistha explains how consciousness appears as the physical world.
chapters.es: Rama asks how formless consciousness perceives a physical body and world. Vasistha describes diverse creations: some worlds dwell inside stones or wind, while others feature forests hanging upside down from the sky. These forms lack material substance; mountains possess no hardness and water holds no fluidity. Instead, the universe exists as cidvyoman, or consciousness-space. Like a city appearing in a dream, the cosmos is a flicker of awareness. It remains śānta, or peaceful, existing as the inherent movement within wind.

chapters.subtitle: A king asks Vasiṣṭha about creation and rebirth.
chapters.es: King Prajñapti of Īlāvatī greets Vasiṣṭha in his golden city within Kuśadvīpa to pose a series of inquiries regarding the nature of existence. He asks how a new creation begins after total dissolution, or saṃhāra, and how formless actions lead to physical bodies in the afterlife. The king questions the mechanics of rebirth without parents, the efficacy of boons, and how consciousness, or saṃvedana, shapes reality. He seeks to reconcile the contradictions between ritual law and the tangible experiences of life and death.

chapters.subtitle: The world is an expression of pure consciousness.
chapters.es: The world exists as a city built of imagination, appearing real through the power of consciousness or saṃvit. Since no physical matter exists at the start of a cycle, the universe arises as a dream-like reflection within the absolute brahman. Those who claim the body produces awareness are compared to blind frogs in a dark well. After death, the mind projects new environments based on mental impressions. Peace comes only when the individual recognizes that all perceived forms are actually the infinite sky of awareness.

chapters.subtitle: The world exists as a city created by thought.
chapters.es: The universe functions as a city of thought. Individuals experience reality based on their own mental projections, much like a child imagining a town. Blessings, curses, and social laws operate through the force of firm conviction in the saṃvid. The rising and falling of awareness constitute the creation and dissolution of all things. Like a jewel reflecting light or wind moving through the sky, consciousness projects these forms while remaining unchanged. Even the properties of herbs and grass stem from this initial mental design.

chapters.subtitle: People experience life and death through mental perception.
chapters.es: A person dying at a sacred site like Prayāga perceives two realities at once. While relatives weep over a cold corpse and burn it in a funeral pyre, the deceased finds himself alive in a new body. This occurs through the saṃkalpa, or mental resolve, of Brahman that structures the universe. Since the world is essentially cidākāśa, or the space of consciousness, merit and sin determine these differing perceptions, much like the varied scenes appearing within a single dream.

chapters.subtitle: The mind creates its own reality and world.
chapters.es: Meditators who focus on the moon do not reach the physical sky; they inhabit individual lunar orbs within their own minds. A man remains in his room while governing seven islands in his mental space. Deeds like charity and penance bear fruit like objects appearing in a dream. The physical body is identical to brahman, serving only as a tool for awakening. This world exists as cid-ākāśa, eventually dissolving into the silent, dense stillness of a stone once the mind finds rest.

chapters.subtitle: Vasishtha describes the world as a mental construction.
chapters.es: Vasishtha rises into the sky after his discourse, explaining that all existence is brahman, a nameless and stainless void like the ākāśa. The worlds seen by beings are constructs of thought, or saṃkalpa. Like a city of imagination or waves in water, these forms never leave their source. Wise men move through the world like wooden puppets, dāru-nara, performing actions while remaining silent within. Even the sight of one’s own death in a dream is empty, for the underlying reality remains unchanged.

chapters.subtitle: Consciousness contains the world like heat exists in fire.
chapters.es: The self perceives its identity as the expanse of brahman, where the world exists like an eddy in water. Consciousness functions naturally, just as heat belongs to fire or coolness to the moon. Though the universe appears through the sense of "I," it remains the silent, unborn reality of ākāśa. Duality persists only until the seeker looks past verbal descriptions. Within this void, the concepts of time and direction emerge, yet the single reality remains as still as the sky.

chapters.subtitle: Only consciousness remains after the universe dissolves.
chapters.es: In a past age, a student asks his teacher what survives the great dissolution of the universe. The teacher explains that mountains, directions, and even deities like Viṣṇu and Rudra vanish like a fading dream. Only the clear cidākāśa remains. Because the world is an appearance within this consciousness, nothing is truly created or destroyed. Every object, from a blade of grass to a clay pot, shares the same nature as the infinite. Established in this truth, the ruler governs without worldly thirst.

chapters.subtitle: A celestial flower rain and a seven-day city festival.
chapters.es: Celestial drums sound and a rain of flowers falls upon the assembly as the teaching concludes. The listeners feel their doubts dissolved by the analogies of dreams and mirages. King Daśaratha summons ten thousand brāhmaṇas from various lands to receive food and gifts. For seven days, the city celebrates with music from vīṇās and drums. Women dance in the streets while the citizens feast, marking their collective transition into a state of peace.

chapters.subtitle: Attaining liberation and the rewards of studying the text
chapters.es: Rāma and King Daśaratha attain freedom from sorrow by steadying their minds in the self. These teachings on liberation loosen the knots of desire within the heart and prevent a return to saṃsāra. Anyone who reads, writes, or sponsors the recitation of this mokṣasaṃhitā gains the merit of a royal sacrifice. Upon completing the study, practitioners honor wise scholars with food, drink, and gifts. Steady contemplation of these analogies leads to the state of a jīvanmukta.

chapters.subtitle: Characters find peace and resolve to perform their duties.
chapters.es: The messenger concludes his narration to King Ariṣṭanemi, who remains in quietude while the messenger returns to Indra's court. Suruci ponders these teachings on the slopes of Gandhamādana. Sutīkṣṇa acknowledges to Agastya that his ignorance has vanished. He perceives the world as gold shaped into ornaments or waves rising from water. Reciting the Vedic truth that all is brahman, he resolves to perform his daily duties without attachment, offering his mind and body to his teacher as the final act of gratitude.

data.v: यतः सर्वाणि भूतानि प्रतिभान्ति स्थितानि च । यत्रैवोपशमं यान्ति तस्मै सत्यात्मने नमः
data.es: Salutations to that being whose nature is Truth, from whom all beings appear and in whom they abide, and in whom alone they finally come to rest.
data.anv: यतः सर्वाणि भूतानि प्रतिभान्ति स्थितानि च (सन्ति), यत्र एव उपशमं यान्ति, तस्मै सत्यात्मने नमः।

pc.e: to the Self whose nature is Truth

data.v: ज्ञाता ज्ञानं तथा ज्ञेयं द्रष्टादर्शनदृश्यभूः । कर्ता हेतुः क्रिया यस्मात्तस्मै ज्ञस्यात्मने नमः
data.es: Salutations to that Self of pure consciousness, from whom arise the knower, knowledge, and the known; the seer, the act of seeing, and the object seen; and the doer, the cause, and the action.
data.anv: ज्ञाता ज्ञानं तथा ज्ञेयं द्रष्टा-दर्शन-दृश्य-भूः कर्ता हेतुः क्रिया यस्मात् (भवति), तस्मै ज्ञस्य आत्मने नमः।

pc.e: the source of the seer, seeing, and the seen

data.v: स्फुरन्ति सीकरा यस्मादानन्दस्याम्बरेऽवनौ । सर्वेषां जीवनं तस्मै ब्रह्मानन्दात्मने नमः
data.es: Salutations to that Supreme Bliss which is the Brahman, from whom droplets of joy sprinkle upon the heavens and the earth, and who is the life-force of all beings.
data.anv: यस्मात् आनन्दस्य सीकराः अम्बरे अवनौ (च) स्फुरन्ति, (यः) सर्वेषां जीवनं (अस्ति), तस्मै ब्रह्म-आनन्द-आत्मने नमः।

pc.e: to the Self of Supreme Bliss which is Brahman

data.v: सुतीक्ष्णो ब्राह्मणः कश्चित्संशयाकृष्टमानसः । अगस्तेराश्रमं गत्वा मुनिं पप्रच्छ सादरम्
data.es: A certain Brāhmaṇa named Sutīkṣṇa, whose mind was pulled by doubts, went to the hermitage of Sage Agastya and questioned the sage with great respect.
data.anv: संशय-आकृष्ट-मानसः कश्चित् सुतीक्ष्णः (नाम) ब्राह्मणः अगस्तेः आश्रमं गत्वा सादरं मुनिं पप्रच्छ।

pc.e: whose mind was pulled by doubts

data.v: भगवन्धर्मतत्त्वज्ञ सर्वशास्त्रविनिश्चित । संशयोऽस्ति महानेकस्त्वमेतं कृपया वद
data.es: O Holy One, O knower of the essence of Dharma, who is well-versed in all scriptures! I have one great doubt; please resolve it for me out of your compassion.
data.anv: भगवन् धर्म-तत्त्व-ज्ञ सर्व-शास्त्र-विनिश्चित, (मे) एकः महान् संशयः अस्ति, त्वम् एतं कृपया वद।

pc.e: O knower of the essence of Dharma
O one well-versed in all scriptures

data.v: मोक्षस्य कारणं कर्म ज्ञानं वा मोक्षसाधनम् । उभयं वा विनिश्चित्य एकं कथय कारणम्
data.es: Is action the cause of liberation, or is knowledge the means to liberation, or is it both? Having determined the truth, please tell me the singular primary cause.
data.anv: मोक्षस्य कारणं कर्म (किम्)? वा ज्ञानं मोक्ष-साधनम् (किम्)? वा उभयम् (किम्)? विनिश्चित्य एकं कारणं कथय।

data.v: उभाभ्यामेव पक्षाभ्यां यथा खे पक्षिणां गतिः । तथैव ज्ञानकर्मभ्यां जायते परमं पदम्
data.es: Just as the flight of birds in the sky is possible only with both wings, even so the supreme state is attained through the combination of both knowledge and action.
data.anv: यथा उभाभ्याम् एव पक्षाभ्यां खे पक्षिणां गतिः (भवति), तथा एव ज्ञान-कर्मभ्यां परमं पदं जायते।

data.v: केवलात्कर्मणो ज्ञानान्नहि मोक्षोऽभिजायते । किंतूभाभ्यां भवेन्मोक्षः साधनं तूभयं विदुः
data.es: Liberation is certainly not born from action alone, nor from knowledge alone; rather, liberation arises from both. Therefore, the wise know both to be the means.
data.anv: केवलात् कर्मणः (वा) ज्ञानात् मोक्षः न हि अभिजायते, किंतु उभाभ्यां मोक्षः भवेत्, (तस्मात्) उभयं तु साधनं विदुः।

data.v: अस्मिन्नर्थे पुरावृत्तमितिहासं वदामि ते । कारुण्याख्यः पुरा कश्चिद्ब्राह्मणोऽधीतवेदकः
data.es: Regarding this matter, I shall tell you an ancient legend. In the past, there was a Brāhmaṇa named Kāruṇya who had studied the Vedas.
data.anv: अस्मिन् अर्थे (अहं) ते पुरा-वृत्तम् इतिहासम् वदामि, पुरा कारुण्य-आख्यः कश्चित् ब्राह्मणः अधीत-वेदकः (आसीत्)।

data.v: अग्निवेश्यस्य पुत्रोऽभूद्वेदवेदाङ्गपारगः । गुरोरधीतविद्यः सन्नाजगाम गृहं प्रति
data.es: He was the son of Agniveśya; having mastered the Vedas and the auxiliary sciences and completed his studies under his Guru, he returned home.
data.anv: (सः) अग्निवेश्यस्य पुत्रः अभूत्, वेद-वेद-अङ्ग-पारगः गुरोः अधीत-विद्यः सन् गृहं प्रति आजगाम।

pc.e: who had mastered the Vedas and Vedangas

data.v: तस्थावकर्मकृत्तूष्णीं संशयानो गृहे तदा । अग्निवेश्यो विलोक्याथ पुत्रं कर्मविवर्जितम्
data.es: At that time, Agnivēśya remained at home, silent and inactive, filled with doubt. Then, observing his son to be completely devoid of any action, he addressed him.
data.anv: तदा (सः) अग्निवेश्यः अकर्मकृत् तूष्णीं संशयानः (सन्) गृहे तस्थौ। अथ पुत्रं कर्मविवर्जितं विलोक्य (अग्निवेश्यः)...

data.v: प्राह एतद्वचो निन्द्यं गुरुः पुत्रं हिताय च । किमेतत्पुत्र कुरुषे पालनं न स्वकर्मणः
data.es: For his son's welfare, the teacher spoke these reproachful words: "O son, what is this? You are not performing your own prescribed duties."
data.anv: गुरुः हिताय च पुत्रं एतत् निन्द्यं वचः प्राह - पुत्र! एतत् किम्? (त्वम्) स्वकर्मणः पालनं न कुरुषे।

data.v: अकर्मनिरतः सिद्धिं कथं प्राप्स्यसि तद्वद । कर्मणोऽस्मान्निवृत्तेः किं कारणं तन्निवेद्यताम्
data.es: "Tell me, how will you attain perfection if you are intent on inaction? State the reason for your withdrawal from these duties."
data.anv: अकर्मनिरतः (सन्) सिद्धिं कथं प्राप्स्यसि? तत् वद। अस्मात् कर्मणः निवृत्तेः किं कारणम्? तत् निवेद्यताम्।

data.v: यावज्जीवमग्निहोत्रं नित्यं संध्यामुपासयेत् । प्रवृत्तिरूपो धर्मोऽयं श्रुत्या स्मृत्या च चोदितः
data.es: "One should perform the fire-sacrifice as long as one lives and regularly worship at the three junctions of the day. This path of action is the dharma ordained by both Śruti and Smṛti."
data.anv: यावज्जीवम् अग्निहोत्रं (कुर्यात्), नित्यं संध्याम् उपासयेत्। अयम् प्रवृत्तिरूपः धर्मः श्रुत्या स्मृत्या च चोदितः।

data.v: न धनेन भवेन्मोक्षः कर्मणा प्रजया न वा । त्यागमात्रेण किंत्वेके यतयोऽश्नन्ति चामृतम्
data.es: "Liberation is not attained through wealth, nor by rituals, nor by progeny; however, some ascetics attain immortality through renunciation alone."
data.anv: धनेन मोक्षः न भवेत्, कर्मणा न, प्रजया वा न। किंतु एके यतयः त्यागमात्रेण अमृतम् अश्नन्ति।

data.v: इति श्रुत्योर्द्वयोर्मध्ये किं कर्तव्यं मया गुरो । इति संदिग्धतां गत्वा तूष्णींभूतोऽस्मि कर्मणि
data.es: "O Teacher, what should I do between these two conflicting scriptural injunctions? Having fallen into such doubt, I have become silent regarding all actions."
data.anv: गुरो! इति द्वयोः श्रुत्योः मध्ये मया किं कर्तव्यम्? इति संदिग्धतां गत्वा कर्मणि तूष्णींभूतः अस्मि।

data.v: इत्युक्त्वा तात विप्रोऽसौ कारुण्यो मौनमागतः । तथाविधं सुतं दृष्ट्वा पुनः प्राह गुरुः सुतम्
data.es: O dear one, having spoken thus, the compassionate young Brāhmaṇa became silent. Seeing his son in that state, the teacher spoke to him again.
data.anv: तात! इति उक्त्वा असौ विप्रः कारुण्यः मौनम् आगतः। तथाविधं सुतं दृष्ट्वा गुरुः पुनः सुतम् प्राह।

data.v: शृणु पुत्र कथामेकां तदर्थं हृदयेऽखिलम् । मत्तोऽवधार्य पुत्र त्वं यथेच्छसि तथा कुरु
data.es: "O son, listen to a certain story. Having fully understood its meaning in your heart from me, O son, you may then do as you wish."
data.anv: पुत्र! एकाम् कथां शृणु। मत्तः तत् अर्थम् अखिलम् हृदये अवधार्य, पुत्र! त्वं यथा इच्छसि तथा कुरु।

data.v: सुरुचिर्नाम काचित्स्त्री अप्सरोगणोत्तमा । उपविष्टा हिमवतः शिखरे शिखिसंवृते
data.es: There was a certain woman named Suruci, the best among the host of Apsarās, who was seated on a peak of the Himavat mountains frequented by peacocks.
data.anv: सुरुचिः नाम काचित् अप्सरः-गण-उत्तमा स्त्री, शिखि-संवृते हिमवतः शिखरे उपविष्टा (आसीत्)।

pc.e: the best among the host of Apsaras

data.v: रमन्ते कामसंतप्ताः किन्नर्यो यत्र किन्नरैः । स्वर्धुन्योघेन संसृष्टे महाघौघविनाशिना
data.es: There, Kinnarīs afflicted by desire sport with Kinnaras, and the place is touched by the flow of the celestial river Gaṅgā, which destroys a multitude of great sins.
data.anv: यत्र काम-संतप्ताः किन्नर्यः किन्नरैः (सह) रमन्ते, (यत्) महा-अघ-ओघ-विनाशिना स्वर्धुनी-ओघेन संसृष्टे (अस्ति)।

pc.e: by the flow of the celestial river
which destroys a multitude of great sins

data.v: दूतमिन्द्रस्य गच्छन्तमन्तरिक्षे ददर्श सा । तमुवाच महाभागा सुरुचिश्चाप्सरोवरा
data.es: That most illustrious and best among the Apsarās, Suruci, beheld the messenger of Indra traveling through the sky and spoke to him.
data.anv: सा महाभागा अप्सरोवरा सुरुचिः अन्तरिक्षे गच्छन्तम् इन्द्रस्य दूतम् ददर्श (सा) तम् उवाच च ।

data.v: देवदूत महाभाग कुत आगम्यते त्वया । अधुना कुत्र गन्तासि तत्सर्वं कृपया वद
data.es: O noble messenger of the gods, from where are you coming? Where are you going now? Please tell me all of that.
data.anv: महाभाग देवदूत त्वया कुतः आगम्यते (त्वम्) अधुना कुत्र गन्ता असि तत् सर्वम् कृपया वद ।

data.v: साधु पृष्टं त्वया सुभ्रु यथावत्कथयामि ते । अरिष्टनेमी राजर्षिर्दत्त्वा राज्यं सुताय वै
data.es: O fair-browed one, you have asked well. I shall tell you everything as it is. The royal sage Ariṣṭanemi, having indeed given his kingdom to his son, has departed.
data.anv: सुभ्रु त्वया साधु पृष्टम् (अहं) ते यथावत् कथयामि । राजर्षिः अरिष्टनेमिः सुताय राज्यम् दत्त्वा वै (स्थितः) ।

data.v: वीतरागः स धर्मात्मा निर्ययौ तपसे वनम् । तपश्चरत्यसौ राजा पर्वते गन्धमादने
data.es: That virtuous and dispassionate soul went out to the forest for penance. That king is now performing austerities on Mount Gandhamādana.
data.anv: सः धर्मात्मा वीतरागः तपसे वनम् निर्ययौ । असौ राजा गन्धमादने पर्वते तपः चरति ।

data.v: कार्यं कृत्वा मया तत्र तत आगम्यतेऽधुना । गन्तास्मि पार्श्वे शक्रस्य तं वृत्तान्तं निवेदितुम्
data.es: Having performed my duty there, I am now returning from that place. I am going to the side of Śakra to report that news.
data.anv: मया तत्र कार्यम् कृत्वा अधुना ततः आगम्यते । तम् वृत्तान्तम् निवेदितुम् शक्रस्य पार्श्वे गन्ता अस्मि ।

data.v: वृत्तान्तः कोऽभवत्तत्र कथयस्व मम प्रभो । प्रष्टुकामा विनीतास्मि नोद्वेगं कर्तुमर्हसि
data.es: O Lord, tell me what news transpired there. I am humble and desirous of asking; you should not feel any annoyance.
data.anv: प्रभो तत्र कः वृत्तान्तः अभवत् मम कथयस्व । (अहं) प्रष्टुकामा विनीता अस्मि (त्वं) उद्वेगम् कर्तुम् न अर्हसि ।

data.v: शृणु भद्रे यथावृत्तं विस्तरेण वदामि ते । तस्मिन्राज्ञि वने तत्र तपश्चरति दुस्तरम्
